Chronic endometritis, characterized by disrupted endometrial microbiota and elevated CD138-positive plasma cells, impairs endometrial receptivity and is associated with thin endometrium, unsuccessful assisted reproductive technology cycles, and infertility. The combination therapy substantially increased endometrial thickness, reduced inflammatory CD138+ cells by greater margins than antibiotics alone, and restored expression of critical implantation signaling molecules (LIF and αVβ3 integrin) that are suppressed by chronic inflammation. Clinical outcomes were markedly improved, with the combined treatment group achieving a 68.6% clinical pregnancy rate and 60.8% live birth rate compared to 48% and 39% respectively in the antibiotics-only group. The mechanical stimulation from low-frequency ultrasound flushing enhances local blood circulation and promotes anti-inflammatory effects beyond antibiotic treatment alone, making this integrated approach more effective for treating chronic endometritis and optimizing endometrial function in fertility programs.

title: Progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) in preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy: a retrospective study and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) is an emerging approach that prevents premature luteinizing hormone surges using oral progestin, thereby protecting oocyte yield and quality while enhancing patient compliance through improved accessibility and reduced injection burden. This retrospective study and meta-analysis evaluated PPOS efficacy for preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy by assessing euploid embryo rates, the primary indicator of embryo quality. Although early progestin exposure raises theoretical concerns about asynchrony between endometrial and embryo development, PPOS demonstrated comparable outcomes to standard GnRH antagonist protocols across multiple fertility parameters, including clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and euploid blastocyst rates, while achieving a significantly reduced abortion rate. Key prognostic factors including female age, anti-mullerian hormone levels, antral follicle count, and estradiol levels on trigger day independently influenced euploid blastocyst rates, but the ovarian stimulation protocol itself was not associated with embryo aneuploidy outcomes. The PPOS protocol offers broader clinical applications in fertility preservation, oocyte donation, and prevention of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, positioning it as an effective alternative to conventional stimulation methods in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Endometrial echo patterns of embryo transfer day affect pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer cycles: a retrospective clinical study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study evaluated how endometrial echo patterns on embryo transfer day predict pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer cycles. The key finding was that non-trilinear endometrial patterns were associated with significantly higher live birth rates and lower early miscarriage rates compared to trilinear patterns, which showed elevated first trimester abortion rates and reduced live birth rates. Pattern A endometrium demonstrated the poorest outcomes with only 19.05% live birth rate and elevated miscarriage risk. Endometrial thickness and echo pattern serve as critical markers of endometrial receptivity, with Pattern C showing reduced thickness and estrogen levels on progesterone initiation day. The study supports the clinical utility of detailed ultrasound assessment of endometrial morphology on transfer day as a predictor of assisted reproduction success, suggesting that endometrial characterization beyond simple thickness measurements may help identify cycles with compromised implantation potential.

title: Effect of Comprehensive Individualised Interventions on the Clinical Outcomes of Patients With Recurrent Implantation Failure: A Single‐Centre Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that comprehensive individualized interventions targeting specific endometrial abnormalities significantly improve pregnancy outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure (RIF). The research identified four major endometrial pathologies associated with RIF: window of implantation (WOI) displacement (34% incidence), elevated CD138+ cells indicating chronic endometritis (9.3% incidence), immune-cell proportion imbalances including elevated uterine natural killer cells and macrophages (7.5% incidence), and endometrial microbiota dysfunction (4.1% incidence). Each pathology was targeted with specific interventions—WOI displacement was managed by delaying embryo transfer timing, chronic endometritis was treated with antibiotics, immune abnormalities were addressed with dexamethasone, human chorionic gonadotropin, platelet-rich plasma, or immunoglobulin infusions based on specific immune profiles, and microbiota dysfunction was treated with Lactobacillus supplementation. Patients receiving these individualized, mechanism-based interventions achieved a cumulative ongoing pregnancy rate of 60%, substantially higher than the 37% baseline rate for empirical treatment. This study supports the clinical value of endometrial diagnostics in RIF classification and demonstrates that tailored immunological and microbiological interventions, informed by endometrial biopsy analysis, can substantially improve reproductive outcomes.

title: Impact of different endometrial preparation protocols on pregnancy outcomes in patients at high risk for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome: a propensity score matched retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This study compared three endometrial preparation protocols for frozen embryo transfer in patients at high risk for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS). The ovarian stimulation protocol demonstrated superior reproductive outcomes, achieving the highest live birth rate (70.71%) and lowest miscarriage rate (6.48%), while the natural cycle protocol also showed favorable results with higher live birth rates and lower cesarean delivery rates compared to hormone replacement therapy. Hormone replacement therapy exhibited the poorest outcomes, associated with younger maternal age, elevated baseline FSH levels, and female-factor infertility, alongside the highest preterm birth and miscarriage rates. Cryopreservation of whole embryos via vitrification effectively mitigated OHSS risk by avoiding the ovarian stimulation inherent to fresh cycles, with successful implantation requiring endometrial thickness ≥7 mm and progesterone levels <1.5 ng/mL. These findings suggest that for patients at high OHSS risk, tailoring the endometrial preparation protocol to individual patient characteristics, particularly favoring ovarian stimulation or natural cycle approaches over hormone replacement therapy, can substantially improve pregnancy outcomes while maintaining safety.

title: High multiple pregnancy rates after double embryo transfers in human: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that double embryo transfer (DET) significantly increases clinical pregnancy rates (49.6% vs 33.5%), live birth rates (39.2% vs 24.1%), and multiple pregnancy rates (25.4% vs 0%) compared to single embryo transfer (SET). While DET substantially improves reproductive outcomes, the associated 25.4% multiple pregnancy rate carries significant risks, including increased maternal, fetal, and neonatal complications. Key predictive factors for success include female age, embryo quality, smoking status, and embryo cohort score, with transfers of two good-quality embryos showing substantially better outcomes than transfers involving poor-quality embryos. Notably, the number of embryos transferred was the only variable reliably controlling multiple pregnancy odds, and sequential SET achieved comparable cumulative pregnancy rates to DET while eliminating the multiple pregnancy risk. The findings support preferring SET over DET from a safety perspective, particularly as poor-quality embryos retain implantation potential despite reduced viability, allowing for more conservative transfer strategies.

title: Insemination methods for embryos transferred in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles do not impact reproductive outcomes in couples with non-male factor infertility
summary: This study examined whether insemination methods affect reproductive outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer (FET) cycles for couples with non-male factor infertility. While unadjusted analyses showed that intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) was associated with higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates compared to conventional in vitro fertilization (IVF), these differences disappeared after adjustment for confounding variables, indicating no significant treatment benefit of ICSI in this population. ICSI-inseminated embryos demonstrated marginally lower post-thaw survival rates than conventionally fertilized embryos, potentially due to increased sensitivity of ICSI embryos to cryopreservation stress on cellular structures. The study revealed that ICSI was associated with increased miscarriage rates and a lower proportion of male live births, while improvements in vitrification protocols and ICSI optimization over time contributed to overall gains in reproductive success from 1995 to 2018. These findings suggest that in frozen-thawed cycles for non-male factor infertility, insemination method does not meaningfully impact pregnancy or live birth outcomes, though cryopreservation may differentially affect embryos created through different fertilization approaches.

title: Effectiveness of non-pharmacological interventions on pregnancy rates in infertile individuals undergoing IVF/ICSI: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and network meta-analysis evaluated non-pharmacological interventions as adjuncts to IVF/ICSI treatment for infertility, demonstrating that multiple behavioral and complementary approaches significantly enhance reproductive outcomes. Cognitive-behavioral therapy, acupuncture (particularly electroacupuncture), lifestyle interventions, health education, and music therapy were all associated with increased clinical pregnancy rates, higher numbers of oocytes retrieved, and improved embryo quality. Electroacupuncture showed particularly robust effects, improving endometrial thickness, HCG positive rates, embryo implantation, and live-birth rates while reducing ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome incidence and cycle cancellation rates. Self-weight management and psychological interventions also improved multiple fertility parameters including oocyte retrieval, fertilization rates, and embryo implantation. These non-pharmacological interventions are easily implemented with minimal side effects, addressing the psychological stress inherent to infertility treatment while enhancing the physiological outcomes necessary for successful pregnancy establishment during assisted reproductive technology.

title: Analysis of Surplus Cryopreserved Blastocysts in Fresh Donor Oocyte Cycles
summary: This study examined how the number of fresh donor oocytes used in in vitro fertilization cycles affects both live birth rates and the accumulation of surplus cryopreserved blastocysts. The research demonstrates that live birth rates plateau at 7 to 9 oocytes, with no significant improvement when more than 15 oocytes are fertilized, while the number of supernumerary blastocysts increases dramatically with increasing oocyte numbers. Patients receiving fewer than 7 oocytes experienced significantly lower live birth rates (50.8%), whereas those with successful pregnancies had a median of 6 supernumerary blastocysts remaining compared to 3 for unsuccessful cycles. The optimal fertilization strategy appears to be 8 to 10 fresh donor oocytes, which achieves high live birth rates while minimizing excess embryo accumulation. Notably, the vast majority of donor oocyte recipients (74.7%) obtained 15 or more oocytes despite no additional reproductive benefit, suggesting that current clinical practice may unnecessarily create large numbers of surplus embryos. These findings provide evidence-based guidance for personalizing oocyte fertilization decisions based on individual reproductive goals and concerns about embryo disposition.

title: Effects of different embryonic development days on clinical outcomes of freeze-thawed embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

This study compared freeze-thawed embryo transfer outcomes based on embryonic development stage, finding that Day 5 (D5) blastocyst transfer consistently achieved superior clinical results compared to Day 3 (D3) and Day 6 (D6) transfers. D5-blastocysts demonstrated significantly higher embryo implantation rates, biochemical pregnancy rates, and clinical pregnancy rates across all age groups. The inferior outcomes with D6-blastocyst transfer stem from multiple mechanisms: excessive blastocyst expansion creates large ice crystals during freezing that damage embryos, zona pellucida loss requires forceful manipulation during thawing causing additional injury, and prolonged in vitro culture increases aneuploidy risk through abnormal spindle body formation and epigenetic changes. D3 cleavage-stage embryos also showed favorable outcomes with lower abortion rates and good pregnancy rates, though generally inferior to D5 transfer. These findings establish D5-blastocyst transfer as the optimal developmental stage for freeze-thawed embryo transfer, particularly important given that female fertility significantly declines after age 35 and embryo quality deteriorates with advancing maternal age.

title: Comparative analysis of endometrial gland imaging and pinopode detection for assessing endometrial receptivity
summary:
# Summary

This study compared two complementary diagnostic approaches for assessing endometrial receptivity in infertile patients undergoing IVF-ET treatment. Pinopodes—specialized microvillus structures on endometrial epithelial cells that appear during the mid-secretory phase—are established markers of endometrial receptivity that facilitate embryo attachment and implantation through pinocytosis-mediated transport of nutrients, cytokines, and growth factors. High-definition hysteroscopy combined with image recognition technology enabled quantification of endometrial gland density and gland opening size, both novel markers of receptivity. The pregnancy group demonstrated significantly higher pinopode counts and developmental maturity, increased pinocytosis activity, greater endometrial gland density, and larger gland openings compared to the non-pregnancy group. Both approaches showed strong positive correlations with each other and effectively predicted pregnancy outcomes, with timing of embryo transfer based on pinopode detection yielding improved clinical pregnancy rates. These findings establish that integrated assessment of pinopode morphology and endometrial gland characteristics provides a non-invasive, objective, and accurate evaluation of endometrial receptivity to enhance IVF-ET success.

title: Effects of Various Transfer Strategies of Frozen-Thawed Cleavage-Stage and Blastocyst Embryos on Pregnancy and Neonatal Outcomes in Different Age Groups
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how different frozen-thawed embryo transfer strategies affect pregnancy outcomes across age groups, comparing cleavage-stage and blastocyst transfers with varying embryo quality and quantity. Blastocyst transfer prevents premature exposure to altered uterine environments and improves live birth rates through enhanced embryo screening, though blastocyst culture risks leaving patients without available embryos. Better endometrial-embryo synchronization during frozen-thawed cycles, combined with improved vitrification and thawing techniques, yields superior pregnancy outcomes compared to fresh cycles. The optimal transfer strategy is age-dependent: women under 35 should prioritize single high-quality blastocyst transfer to minimize multiple pregnancies and perinatal complications, while women 35 and older benefit from double high-quality blastocysts or high-quality cleavage embryos to maximize clinical pregnancy and live birth rates. Advanced maternal age significantly decreases blastocyst euploid rates and singleton live birth rates after age 34, making embryo selection and synchronization increasingly critical for older patients seeking to achieve favorable reproductive outcomes.

title: The Role of Granulocyte Colony Stimulating Factor in Mitigating the Risk of Recurrent Miscarriages: A Comprehensive Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF), a hematopoietic cytokine produced by bone marrow cells, macrophages, and fibroblasts, significantly reduces recurrent miscarriage risk through multiple mechanisms. Intrauterine infusion of G-CSF during the ovulatory phase is particularly effective, reducing miscarriage rates by approximately 52% (RR = 0.48) while enhancing endometrial receptivity, promoting endometrial vascular remodeling, and facilitating embryo adhesion and invasion. G-CSF operates through paracrine mechanisms that regulate endometrial stromal cell activation and decidualization, stimulate vascular endothelial cell proliferation and neovascularization, and induce trophoblast proliferation and maturation. The cytokine's effects improve the endometrial immune environment, increase progesterone levels, and enhance endometrial sensitivity to estrogen, collectively creating an environment conducive to successful embryo implantation. Meta-analysis confirms that intrauterine G-CSF administration substantially increases live birth rates in patients with recurrent miscarriage, while subcutaneous injection and post-pregnancy administration show minimal efficacy. These findings establish intrauterine G-CSF infusion during ovulation as an effective therapeutic intervention for recurrent miscarriage prevention.

title: The Effectiveness of Live Birth Rate of Traditional Chinese Medicine Intervention for Infertile Women Undergoing a Second Round of IVF Is Influenced by Age
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) and Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) enhance fertility outcomes in women undergoing a second round of IVF, with particular attention to the moderating role of maternal age. TCM intervention improved clinical pregnancy rates to 51.11% and live birth rates to 42.22%, while a meta-analysis confirmed that CHM significantly enhanced both clinical and ongoing pregnancy rates across multiple trials. However, maternal age emerged as a critical determinant of treatment success, with women under 38 years demonstrating substantially higher live birth rates compared to older women, reflecting age-associated declines in oocyte quality, ovarian reserve, and increased chromosomal abnormalities that contribute to pregnancy loss and implantation failure. The findings suggest that the integration of CHM into conventional IVF protocols represents a promising complementary approach to improving fertility outcomes, though its effectiveness is significantly constrained by advancing maternal age. This work underscores the time-sensitive nature of fertility interventions and supports implementing minimum three-month CHM treatment courses prior to embryo transfer to optimize primordial follicle maturation and oocyte development.

title: Gonadotropin‐releasing hormone agonist protocols for pituitary suppression in assisted reproduction
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRHa) are widely used in assisted reproduction to suppress luteinizing hormone surges and prevent premature egg release during ovarian stimulation, ultimately improving live birth rates. This Cochrane systematic review compared multiple GnRHa protocols, finding that long protocol GnRHa may improve clinical pregnancy rates compared to short protocol approaches, though live birth and ongoing pregnancy rates showed little difference between these regimens. Among dosing strategies, a 100 µg GnRHa dose appeared superior to lower doses (25 µg) for improving clinical pregnancy rates, while higher 500 µg doses did not confer additional benefit over 80 µg in short protocols. Comparisons of other protocol variations—including ultrashort versus long protocols, reduced versus standard dosing, and different administration durations—demonstrated uncertain or no significant differences in outcomes. The review found insufficient evidence to determine differences in ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and miscarriage rates across protocols, suggesting these adverse events occur at similar frequencies regardless of GnRHa approach. Overall, while GnRHa protocols effectively support assisted reproduction, evidence for superiority of specific protocols remains limited, with clinical pregnancy rates showing the most consistent improvements in longer protocol applications.

title: Effect of repeated controlled ovarian stimulation on pregnancy outcomes in fresh embryo transfer cycles: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined how repeated controlled ovarian stimulation (COS) affects pregnancy outcomes in fresh embryo transfer cycles. Controlled ovarian stimulation, achieved through gonadotropin injection to induce superovulation, is a critical component of in-vitro fertilization and involves protocol choices such as GnRH agonists versus antagonists. While animal studies suggest that repeated stimulation may cause ovarian oxidative stress, mitochondrial DNA mutations, and reduced oocyte quality, this clinical study found that up to four or five repeated COS cycles did not impair ovarian reserve function and actually positively influenced clinical pregnancy rates. Key factors independently predicting successful outcomes included advanced maternal age (negatively associated with pregnancy and live birth rates while increasing miscarriage risk), elevated basal estradiol levels, increased endometrial thickness, and optimal embryo developmental stage, with each additional millimeter of endometrial thickness significantly improving clinical pregnancy odds. These findings suggest that repeated COS cycles within reasonable limits may be a viable strategy for optimizing reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: Impact of relative estradiol changes during ovarian stimulation on blastocyst formation and live birth in assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that estradiol (E2) response during controlled ovarian stimulation is a critical predictor of assisted reproductive technology success, with moderate-high and high E2 responders achieving significantly higher blastocyst formation rates, clinical pregnancy rates (54.5-61.5% vs. 35.5%), and live birth rates (44.9-52.0% vs. 25.7%) compared to low responders. Higher E2 levels correlate strongly with improved oocyte retrieval, maturation, and fertilization outcomes, while being positively associated with younger patient age and better ovarian function parameters such as lower baseline FSH levels. However, the study reveals a critical dose-response relationship: excessively high E2 levels during ovarian stimulation create suboptimal uterine conditions, leading to abnormal placentation and increased risks of adverse neonatal outcomes including small-for-gestational-age infants and low birth weight. These findings establish E2 response level as an important clinical indicator for predicting embryological outcomes and pregnancy success, suggesting an optimal E2 response window that maximizes both embryo development and endometrial receptivity.

title: Developmental potential of zygotes without pronucleus (0PN) and pregnancy outcomes after single euploid blastocyst transfers
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates the developmental potential and reproductive outcomes of zero-pronucleus (0PN) zygotes compared to normal two-pronucleus (2PN) zygotes in assisted reproductive technology. Although 70.4% of 0PN zygotes underwent fertilization and reached cleavage stage, they demonstrated significantly lower developmental potential, with only 5.3% forming biopsied blastocysts compared to substantially higher rates in 2PN zygotes, which were 19.6 times more likely to produce biopsied blastocysts. The euploidy rate was lower in 0PN-derived blastocysts (39%) than 2PN-derived blastocysts (45%), though interestingly, when euploid blastocysts from either group were transferred as frozen embryos, clinical pregnancy and live birth rates showed no statistically significant differences, indicating that a small proportion of 0PN-derived embryos can support normal pregnancy. The insemination method influenced outcomes, with ICSI resulting in similar euploidy rates between 0PN and 2PN groups, while conventional IVF showed lower euploidy rates in the 0PN group. These findings suggest that despite their lower overall developmental competence, euploid 0PN-derived blastocysts may be viable alternatives for patients without better embryo options.

title: Comparative efficacy of intrauterine infusion treatments for recurrent implantation failure: a network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This network meta-analysis evaluated intrauterine infusion treatments for recurrent implantation failure (RIF), a condition with multifactorial causes including embryonic, uterine, and immunological factors. Four active treatments—platelet-rich plasma (PRP), granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (G-CSF), peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs), and human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG)—were compared against placebo and control groups for their ability to enhance endometrial receptivity and improve pregnancy outcomes. PRP emerged as the most effective intervention, demonstrating the highest efficacy rankings for both clinical pregnancy rates (84.5% SUCRA value) and live birth rates (81.4% SUCRA value), followed by PBMCs and G-CSF. All four active treatments significantly improved clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and endometrial thickness compared to placebo and untreated controls. These findings establish intrauterine infusion therapy as an evidence-based approach to enhance endometrial function and improve reproductive outcomes in women with recurrent implantation failures.

title: The role of granulocyte–macrophage colony-stimulating factor in female assisted reproductive technology treatment: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) significantly enhances reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology (ART) by improving clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, implantation rates, and available embryo rates, with meta-analysis of non-randomized controlled trials showing an odds ratio of 1.90 for implantation rates. GM-CSF is naturally produced by female reproductive tract epithelial cells under estrogen stimulation and functions to enhance immune responses, promote hematopoietic cell proliferation and differentiation while inhibiting apoptosis, thereby creating a more favorable environment for embryo development and implantation. Animal models demonstrate that GM-CSF deficiency results in reduced inner cell mass, delayed blastocyst formation, and fetal resorption, while GM-CSF-enriched culture media enhance inner cell mass viability, increase blastocyst formation rates, and reduce apoptosis with improved live birth outcomes and minimal chromosomal abnormalities. The review highlights particular benefits for patients with persistently thin endometrium, where GM-CSF supplementation improves endometrial receptivity and embryo-endometrial interactions, though some outcome measures such as blastocyst formation rate, good blastocyst rates, and miscarriage rates showed no significant differences between GM-CSF and control groups. Notably, the positive effects were predominantly observed in non-randomized controlled trials, suggesting potential bias, while randomized controlled trial data showed less pronounced benefits, warranting careful interpretation of clinical efficacy.

title: Effect of embryo cryopreservation before surgery on clinical outcomes in IVF patients with endometrioma
summary: Endometriosis significantly impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms, including reduced ovarian reserve, compromised oocyte quality, and a chronic inflammatory peritoneal environment that interferes with ovulation, fertilization, and endometrial receptivity. Ovarian endometrioma surgery, while potentially improving the peritoneal microenvironment, paradoxically damages ovarian reserve by reducing antral follicle count and requiring higher FSH stimulation with fewer oocytes retrieved. This study demonstrates that embryo cryopreservation before surgery (ECBS) substantially improves reproductive outcomes in IVF patients with endometrioma, achieving significantly higher ongoing pregnancy rates (88.2% vs. 58.2%) and lower miscarriage rates (0% vs. 35.5%) compared to surgery followed by fresh embryo transfer. Multivariate analysis identified ECBS as the only independent factor associated with ongoing pregnancy, and the procedure was further associated with reduced incidence of placenta previa complications. By cryopreserving embryos before surgery, patients avoid the damaging effects of endometrioma excision on ovarian reserve while still benefiting from improved peritoneal conditions. This strategy effectively mitigates endometriosis-related fertility impairment and represents an optimized treatment pathway for IVF patients with ovarian endometrioma.

title: Endometrial compaction can improve assisted reproductive technology outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles using hormone replacement therapy: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that endometrial compaction (EC), measured as the percentage reduction in endometrial thickness following progesterone administration in hormone replacement therapy frozen-embryo transfer cycles, significantly improves assisted reproductive technology outcomes. Estrogen stimulates initial endometrial thickening during the proliferative phase, while progesterone subsequently triggers endometrial compaction and secretory changes that create favorable conditions for embryo implantation. The study found that EC exceeding 10% was associated with substantially increased biochemical pregnancy rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and ongoing pregnancy rates, with EC of 10-15% and >15% increasing the probability of a positive β-hCG test by more than 5- and 6-fold respectively, and increasing the probability of fetal heart detection at five weeks post-transfer. Notably, EC >15% demonstrated the strongest effect on ongoing pregnancy outcomes, with the probability of ongoing pregnancy at 12 weeks of gestation increasing more than 6-fold at this threshold. The authors suggest that measurement of endometrial compaction via ultrasound represents a practical, non-invasive method to assess endometrial receptivity and predict success in frozen-embryo transfer cycles, particularly when EC exceeds 10% threshold.

title: Comparison of the euploidy rate in preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy cycles following progestin-primed versus gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist protocol: a randomized controlled study
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial compared embryo euploidy rates between progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) and GnRH antagonist protocols for preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A) cycles. Both PPOS and antagonist protocols produced comparable euploidy rates (12.5% versus 16.0%, respectively), with no significant differences in positive pregnancy test rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or live birth rates between groups. Although the PPOS protocol required lower total FSH doses while maintaining higher serum estradiol and LH levels at trigger, the total number of blastocysts and euploid blastocysts were equivalent between protocols. The study demonstrates that PPOS, which inhibits the pituitary LH surge through progestin administration, achieves similar embryo quality outcomes as the conventional antagonist approach, though the negative impact of progestins on endometrial receptivity necessitates frozen embryo transfer rather than fresh transfer in PPOS cycles. These findings suggest both ovarian stimulation protocols are viable options for patients requiring PGT-A, with comparable reproductive outcomes despite different hormonal suppression mechanisms.

title: Oral Dydrogesterone Versus Vaginal Progesterone for Luteal Phase Support in Frozen–Thawed Embryo Transfer Cycles: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that oral dydrogesterone and vaginal progesterone produce comparable reproductive outcomes for luteal phase support in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles, with no significant differences in clinical pregnancy rates, ongoing pregnancy rates, live birth rates, or miscarriage rates between the two routes of administration. Oral dydrogesterone offers superior patient tolerability and ease of administration compared to vaginal progesterone, which is commonly associated with vaginal irritation, discharge, and bleeding, as well as intramuscular injections that cause local pain and inflammation. The analysis confirms the noninferiority of oral dydrogesterone therapy, supported by landmark trials including LOTUS II, which documented normal liver enzyme function and low congenital disorder rates across treatment groups. Dydrogesterone's improved oral bioavailability and established safety profile in treating menstrual disorders and endometriosis make it a viable alternative for luteal phase support in frozen-thawed cycles. Heterogeneity in clinical pregnancy outcomes was attributed to variations in dydrogesterone dosage, suggesting that standardized dosing protocols may optimize efficacy. Given its patient-friendly administration profile and equivalent reproductive outcomes, oral dydrogesterone may serve as a practical substitute to vaginal progesterone in future frozen embryo transfer protocols.

title: Euploidy rate and pregnancy outcomes in preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy cycles using progestin-primed ovarian stimulation versus GnRH antagonist protocol
summary: This study compared progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) using medroxyprogesterone acetate with GnRH antagonist protocols for controlled ovarian hyperstimulation in preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy cycles. While PPOS resulted in fewer oocytes retrieved and lower embryo yields at intermediate stages, it achieved comparable euploidy rates (43.3% vs. 45.0%), aneuploidy rates, and pregnancy outcomes including clinical pregnancy rates and implantation rates compared to GnRH antagonist protocols. Notably, PPOS demonstrated a significantly lower miscarriage rate (7.9% vs. 16.8%), particularly in women under 38 years old (10.2% vs. 20.5%), and reduced whole genome amplification failure rates. Euploidy rates declined with advancing maternal age in both protocols, with the highest rates observed in women aged 20–35 years. These findings suggest that PPOS does not negatively impact blastocyst chromosomal status or reproductive outcomes while offering the clinical advantage of reduced miscarriage risk and avoiding fresh embryo transfer due to progesterone's detrimental endometrial effects.

title: Endometrial Thickness Change in Response to Progesterone is Associated with Ongoing Pregnancy Rate in Women with Treated Intrauterine Adhesion - A Prospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study examines endometrial thickness dynamics in women with treated intrauterine adhesion (IUA) undergoing frozen-thaw embryo transfer, demonstrating that endometrial expansion in response to progesterone supplementation is a critical predictor of reproductive success. The key finding is that endometrial expansion of 10% or greater during the luteal phase correlates with significantly higher ongoing pregnancy rates (55.3% vs 26.3%), as well as improved clinical pregnancy, implantation, and positive β-hCG rates compared to minimal expansion. Absolute endometrial thickness on embryo transfer day was also positively associated with ongoing pregnancy rates, while notably, the serum estradiol-to-progesterone ratio was negatively correlated with pregnancy outcomes. The study establishes that monitoring endometrial thickness changes during hormonal preparation—following estradiol valerate administration and progesterone supplementation—provides valuable information for predicting embryo implantation success in IUA patients, suggesting that dynamic endometrial responsiveness rather than baseline thickness alone determines receptivity and reproductive potential.

title: Investigating the Effect of Endometrial Thickness Changes and Compaction on the Fertility Rate of Patients Undergoing ART: A Prospective Study
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This prospective study demonstrates that endometrial characteristics, particularly thickness and compaction, are critical determinants of fertility outcomes in assisted reproductive technology (ART) patients. Endometrial thickness measured on the day of embryo transfer (secondary thickness) emerged as a significant predictor of clinical pregnancy, with a dose-dependent relationship observed between endometrial compaction percentage and pregnancy success rates—patients with 5% compaction achieved 30.4% pregnancy rates compared to only 5.5% in those with 20% compaction. Thin endometrium (less than 7 mm) was strongly associated with adverse outcomes including miscarriage, abnormal placentation, and decreased live birth rates. The hormonal regulation of endometrial development involves estrogen-promoted cell growth and angiogenesis during the follicular phase, followed by progesterone-mediated cessation of endometrial volume expansion after ovulation. For endometriosis patients undergoing ART, depot leuprolide acetate combined with estrogen and progesterone replacement was used for treatment, with endometrial preparation protocols incorporating these hormones to optimize receptivity. These findings establish endometrial thickness and compaction as modifiable factors that warrant clinical attention to improve fertility outcomes in ART cycles.

title: Bioinformatic Analysis of Complex In Vitro Fertilization Data and Predictive Model Design Based on Machine Learning: The Age Paradox in Reproductive Health
summary: This bioinformatic study developed machine learning and deep learning predictive models to estimate live birth outcomes in IVF cycles, revealing an "age paradox" where reproductive age significantly impacts success rates. The research integrated multiple biological parameters including hormonal markers (FSH, LH, AMH, AFC, estradiol), paracrine factors regulating oocyte maturation (GDF9, BMP-15, BMP-6), and mitochondrial function indicators that collectively influence embryo quality. Key innovations examined include controlled ovarian stimulation with GnRH agonists to prevent premature ovulation, in vitro maturation and activation techniques to optimize follicle development, and enhanced embryo culture media incorporating albumin, amino acids, and growth factors to better replicate the natural reproductive environment. The study identified that preimplantation genetic testing and single-embryo transfer substantially improved IVF outcomes, while endometrial scratching showed promise for treating endometriosis and potentially increasing implantation rates. Notably, antioxidant supplementation (vitamins, melatonin, zinc, selenium) and various growth factor interventions failed to demonstrate efficacy in improving live birth rates. These findings underscore that predictive modeling combining demographic, hormonal, and embryological variables can optimize personalized IVF strategies to overcome age-related reproductive challenges.

title: Serum concentrations of medroxyprogesterone acetate were undetectable on OPU+5 days and had no effect on the serum progesterone level in patients undergoing the progestin-primed ovarian stimulation protocol
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) in the progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocol, which uses oral progestins to prevent premature luteinizing hormone surge during assisted reproduction. Despite MPA being undetectable in serum by egg retrieval day, the research demonstrates that MPA administration does not affect serum progesterone levels, endometrial receptivity, or reproductive outcomes including fertilization rate, pregnancy rate, and live birth rate compared to low-dose controls. Both MPA and progesterone activate similar endometrial gene expression patterns, including upregulation of genes like SPARCL1, SLC7A8, and FKBP5 that support endometrial remodeling, while downregulating epithelial and vascular markers. The PPOS protocol offers practical advantages including reduced gonadotropin consumption, increased oocyte retrieval, and improved cumulative live birth rates, particularly in older women. Notably, no correlation was found between total MPA dosage and reproductive outcomes, and population studies showed no increased risk of congenital abnormalities. These findings support the safety and efficacy of MPA-based PPOS for patients undergoing in vitro fertilization.

title: Pregnancy outcomes in freeze-all versus fresh embryo transfer cycles of women with adenomyosis and endometriosis: a systemic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are highly prevalent causes of infertility associated with multifactorial pathophysiology including chronic inflammation, fibrosis, oxidative stress, reduced endometrial receptivity, and diminished ovarian reserve. In assisted reproductive technology cycles, controlled ovarian hyperstimulation produces supraphysiologic estradiol and progesterone levels that further impair endometrial receptivity and create embryo-endometrium asynchrony, contributing to poor pregnancy outcomes in affected women. This meta-analysis demonstrates that freeze-all embryo transfer (FET) strategies significantly improve clinical pregnancy rates (OR: 1.25), live birth rates (OR: 1.31), and implantation rates (OR: 1.27) compared to fresh embryo transfer in women with endometriosis, likely by allowing precise endometrial development control and enabling GnRH agonist pretreatment to optimize the uterine environment. For adenomyosis patients, FET and fresh transfer outcomes were comparable, suggesting that the endometrial dysfunction in adenomyosis differs mechanistically from endometriosis-associated pathology. These findings support FET as the preferred strategy for endometriosis patients undergoing IVF/ICSI, while individualized approaches may be warranted for adenomyosis cases.

title: Frozen-thawed double cleavage-stage or frozen-thawed single day 6 blastocyst stage embryo transfer: which is preferable for patients younger than 35 without day 5 blastocyst formation?
summary:
# Summary

For young patients under 35 years old without day 5 blastocyst formation, this study evaluated which frozen-thawed embryo transfer strategy—double cleavage-stage embryos or single good-quality day 6 blastocysts—offers optimal reproductive outcomes. Double cleavage-stage embryo transfer achieved higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates comparable to single good-quality day 6 blastocyst transfer, but at the cost of significantly elevated multiple pregnancy rates (34.4% versus 1.6%), which carries substantial maternal and fetal risks including preterm birth, hypertensive disorders, and gestational diabetes. Single good-quality day 6 blastocyst transfer achieved comparable live birth rates while dramatically reducing multiple pregnancy risk, with implantation rates exceeding those of cleavage-stage embryos due to superior trophectoderm morphology and euploidy rates. Critically, poor-quality day 6 blastocysts demonstrated higher early miscarriage rates, emphasizing the importance of embryo quality assessment. The study concludes that single good-quality day 6 blastocyst embryo transfer represents the optimal strategy for these patients, as it balances reproductive efficacy with safety by preventing the serious complications associated with multiple gestations while maintaining comparable pregnancy outcomes through enhanced embryo selection and physiologic synchronization with endometrial receptivity.

title: Intrauterine infusion of autologous platelet-rich plasma modulates endometrial immune status and improves pregnancy outcomes in patients with persistent chronic endometritis
summary: Chronic endometritis causes infertility by reducing endometrial receptivity through persistent inflammation characterized by pathogenic bacterial infections and abnormal immune cell infiltration. While antibiotic treatment is the gold standard, some patients with persistent chronic endometritis remain refractory to conventional therapy. This study demonstrates that intrauterine infusion of autologous platelet-rich plasma (PRP) effectively treats refractory chronic endometritis by modulating the endometrial immune microenvironment, significantly reducing CD138+ plasma cells, CD56+ natural killer cells, CD8+ T cells, and T-bet+ Th1 cells. PRP-treated patients exhibited upregulation of endometrial receptivity-related genes, wound healing genes, and antimicrobial response pathways, while simultaneously downregulating immune response genes. Consequently, patients with cured chronic endometritis achieved substantially improved clinical outcomes, with implantation rates increasing from 21.1% to 54.3% and clinical pregnancy rates rising from 38.5% to 75.0%. These findings establish PRP infusion as a promising therapeutic approach for antibiotic-refractory chronic endometritis, restoring endometrial function and reproductive success.

title: The Follicular Output Rate was Improved with 3-Day Letrozole Administration Compared with 5-Day Letrozole Administration Under Progestin-Primed Ovarian Stimulation
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates letrozole (LE) dosing duration within progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocols for assisted reproduction. A 3-day letrozole regimen significantly outperformed the standard 5-day regimen, producing higher follicular output rates, greater numbers of mature oocytes and blastocysts, and improved blastocyst formation rates, all while requiring lower total gonadotropin doses. The superior outcomes with shorter letrozole administration appear mechanistically related to enhanced follicular synchrony and more favorable endocrine conditions during the late follicular phase, including higher estradiol levels and lower progesterone levels on the trigger day. Consequently, patients receiving the 3-day letrozole protocol achieved significantly higher clinical pregnancy and blastocyst transfer rates despite requiring transfer of fewer embryos per cycle, suggesting improved overall reproductive efficiency in ovarian stimulation.

title: Endometrial compaction in patients with chronic endometritis does not predict pregnancy outcomes: a retrospective study
summary: Chronic endometritis (CE) is an inflammatory endometrial condition caused by microbial disturbances and altered immune environments that impairs pregnancy outcomes through multiple mechanisms. CE triggers progesterone resistance and diminished endometrial stromal cell differentiation, disrupting the normal hormonal response necessary for endometrial decidualization and receptivity; these effects are mediated by alterations in immune cell and cytokine expression that are detrimental to embryo implantation. While endometrial compaction—a measure of appropriate endometrial response to progesterone exposure—is strongly associated with improved clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, and live birth rates in patients without CE, this favorable association is abolished in CE patients even after antibiotic treatment with doxycycline hydrochloride. Importantly, among CE patients following antibiotic treatment, endometrial compaction was not significantly associated with biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, or live birth, suggesting that CE-induced inflammatory damage to endometrial receptivity persists despite microbiological cure. These findings indicate that CE represents a distinct pathophysiologic barrier to successful pregnancy that cannot be adequately predicted or overcome by endometrial compaction metrics alone, even after antimicrobial therapy.

title: Multicenter cohort study of the effect of Crinone® progesterone vaginal gel alone to prepare the endometrium for frozen–thawed blastocyst transfer in a hormone replacement cycle
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter cohort study evaluated Crinone progesterone vaginal gel as the sole luteal support agent for preparing the endometrium in frozen-thawed blastocyst transfer cycles using hormone replacement. The study enrolled patients receiving estradiol to suppress corpus luteum development, followed by Crinone administration once endometrial thickness reached ≥8 mm. The treatment achieved a clinical pregnancy rate of 41.7%, which was notably higher than national registry data (36.0%), with better outcomes in patients younger than 38 years (48.7%) compared to older patients (30.2%). Maternal age and blastocyst quality, classified by Gardner's criteria, were the primary factors associated with pregnancy success. Crinone demonstrated favorable safety and tolerability, with only minor adverse events including light genital bleeding (5.8% at day 19), abdominal pain, and isolated cases of threatened miscarriage, representing significant advantages over other vaginal progesterone formulations due to reduced vaginal discharge and administration discomfort. These findings support Crinone as an effective and safe monotherapy for endometrial preparation in frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: Ovarian Responses and Outcomes of In Vitro Fertilization Following Progesterone-Primed Ovarian Stimulation and Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Antagonist Protocols Using Follitropin Delta
summary: This study compared progesterone-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) and GnRH antagonist protocols using follitropin delta, a recombinant FSH preparation with dosing adjusted by anti-Müllerian hormone levels and body weight, for controlled ovarian stimulation in infertile women undergoing in vitro fertilization. Both protocols suppress premature luteinizing hormone surges to prevent ovulation, with PPOS using progestin to inhibit estradiol-responsive hypothalamic cells while GnRH antagonists directly suppress endogenous FSH and LH. The PPOS protocol produced significantly more blastocysts overall and particularly benefited women aged ≥40 years with higher oocyte retrieval, though it requires a freeze-all strategy due to early endometrial decidualization precluding fresh embryo transfer. Clinical and ongoing pregnancy rates were comparable between protocols, indicating both are effective approaches for assisted reproduction. GnRH agonist triggering combined with a freeze-all policy substantially reduced ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk. These findings suggest PPOS with follitropin delta represents a viable alternative to GnRH antagonist protocols, especially for older patients seeking improved oocyte yields while maintaining pregnancy outcomes and safety.

title: Protocols for Endometrial Preparation and Endometrial Pattern Before Embryo Transfer in Patients with Asherman Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Asherman syndrome causes endometrial fibrosis that destroys the distinction between functional and basal layers, impairing fertility. Treatment involves transcervical resection of adhesions combined with intrauterine balloon stenting, hyaluronic acid, estrogen, and dydrogesterone to restore endometrial function. The GnRHa+HRT protocol significantly improves clinical pregnancy and live birth rates compared to HRT alone by suppressing endogenous estrogen secretion through pituitary downregulation, which extends the window of implantation and enhances endometrial receptivity. Achievement of a triple-line endometrial pattern before embryo transfer emerges as the strongest predictor of successful pregnancy and live birth outcomes. Younger patient age (<37 years) and fewer transcervical resection procedures independently associate with improved pregnancy outcomes, while endometrial thickness measurements alone do not predict success.

title: Dual triggering for final oocyte maturation. A narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Dual triggering for final oocyte maturation combines human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH agonists) to synchronize the complex hormonal cascade required for oocyte maturation and ovulation. While hCG alone effectively triggers maturation by binding to LH receptors on granulosa cells, the addition of GnRH agonists amplifies this response by simultaneously releasing endogenous LH and FSH from the pituitary, potentially enhancing nuclear and cytoplasmic maturation through synergistic mechanisms. Multiple clinical studies demonstrate that dual triggering significantly improves reproductive outcomes compared to hCG alone, including higher numbers of retrieved and mature oocytes, improved fertilization and implantation rates, and substantially higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates across various patient populations. This approach proves particularly beneficial for patients with low ovarian response, high proportions of immature oocytes, and diminished ovarian reserve, while also reducing the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. The combination of hCG and GnRH agonist therefore represents an important advancement in assisted reproductive technology for optimizing oocyte maturation and improving fertility outcomes.

title: Effects of different granulocyte colony stimulating factor regimens on patients with euploid embryo transfer in recurrent implantation failure
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) administration strategies in patients with recurrent implantation failure undergoing euploid embryo transfer. G-CSF, synthesized by endothelial cells and macrophages in the decidua and placenta, suppresses detrimental immune responses while promoting T-regulatory cell activation and a tolerant Th2-biased immune milieu that facilitates embryo implantation and trophoblastic growth. A combined G-CSF regimen involving both intrauterine infusion and subcutaneous dosing (Group 2) achieved significantly higher clinical pregnancy rates (65.9%) and live birth rates (55.7%) compared to control (50.5% and 40.6%, respectively), with the combined approach presumably providing dual benefits of local endometrial priming through intrauterine infusion and systemic immune modulation through subcutaneous administration. Intrauterine G-CSF alone improved endometrial receptivity by increasing endometrial thickness and altering implantation-related gene expression, while subcutaneous G-CSF influenced uterine natural killer cells systemically. These findings suggest that combined G-CSF regimens enhance reproductive outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure by addressing both local endometrial function and systemic immune tolerance mechanisms.

title: Amlodipine in the HRT cycle for frozen embryo transfer to correct uterine artery resistance in women with prior implantation failure: a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated amlodipine, a calcium channel blocker, as a treatment to improve uterine perfusion in women with prior implantation failure undergoing frozen embryo transfer. The study demonstrates that amlodipine significantly reduces uterine artery pulsatility index (UtA-PI) and resistance index by blocking voltage-dependent L-type calcium channels, which decreases intracellular calcium and promotes vascular smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation. Elevated uterine artery PI is an independent risk factor for implantation failure, as inadequate blood flow impairs endometrial development, thickness, and receptivity—all critical for successful embryo implantation. While amlodipine successfully improved hemodynamic markers and showed safety during pregnancy with no increase in embryonic or fetal abnormalities, the medication did not significantly improve clinical pregnancy or implantation rates compared to placebo. These findings suggest that while amlodipine effectively corrects uterine blood flow resistance, additional factors beyond vascular optimization may be necessary to overcome recurrent implantation failure in this population.

title: The impact of hCG trigger versus dual trigger on reproductive outcomes in elderly infertile women: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

In elderly infertile women undergoing antagonist stimulation protocols, dual trigger (combining GnRH agonist and hCG) significantly outperforms traditional hCG trigger alone across multiple reproductive parameters. The dual trigger mechanism works by activating pituitary GnRH receptors to induce a synchronized release of endogenous luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone, while simultaneously antagonizing hCG's sustained action to reduce ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk and accelerate luteolysis. Compared to hCG trigger, dual trigger resulted in higher numbers of retrieved oocytes, increased 2PN fertilization rates, greater cleavage-stage embryo counts, and markedly improved pregnancy outcomes including clinical pregnancy rates (46.67% vs. 27.88% in fresh cycles) and cumulative live birth rates. These improvements were consistent across both fresh embryo transfer and frozen embryo transfer cycles, with the dual trigger group demonstrating superior implantation rates and live birth rates per patient without significantly increasing OHSS incidence. The findings support dual trigger as the optimal final oocyte maturation strategy for elderly women, where age-related oocyte quality decline necessitates enhanced triggering protocols to maximize reproductive success.

title: Clinical efficacy and safety of two highly purified human menopausal gonadotropins in women undergoing in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the clinical efficacy and safety of two highly purified human menopausal gonadotropin (hMG) preparations, Gynogen HP and Menopur, in women undergoing in vitro fertilization. Both products stimulate follicular growth and oocyte maturation through their combined follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone activity, with Menopur containing higher human chorionic gonadotropin levels for enhanced oocyte maturation support. Gynogen HP demonstrated non-inferiority to Menopur across most efficacy parameters, including oocyte retrieval (6.3 versus 6.7 oocytes), oocyte maturation rates (86.6% versus 88.3%), and embryo development, though Menopur showed higher fertilization rates (89.9% versus 81.2%) and clinical pregnancy rates (4.9 versus 3.4 per oocyte retrieved). Both preparations were well-tolerated with minimal adverse events, and the use of GnRH agonist protocols effectively reduced ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk, with only one case reported in the Menopur group and none in the Gynogen HP group. These findings support Gynogen HP as an effective alternative for controlled ovarian stimulation in assisted reproduction, potentially offering cost advantages for patients in resource-limited settings.

title: The Comparative Study of Platelet Rich Plasma (PRP) and Plasma Rich in Growth factor (PRGF) in Endometrial Thickness, Implantation Rate and Pregnancy Outcomes in IVF Patients
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study evaluates platelet-rich plasma (PRP) and plasma rich in growth factors (PRGF) as regenerative therapies for improving endometrial thickness and IVF outcomes in patients with thin endometrium. Both therapies enhance endometrial thickness and improve IVF outcomes, but PRGF demonstrates superior efficacy, increasing endometrial thickness by 1.6 mm compared to PRP's 1.2 mm increase, and achieving higher implantation rates (40% versus 28%) and clinical pregnancy rates (38% versus 24%). PRGF's enhanced effectiveness is attributed to its bioactive components, particularly transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1), which protect against endometrial fibrosis, and its ability to upregulate vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) expression and stimulate endometrial fibroblast proliferation, migration, and contractility through increased pro-collagen type I and hyaluronic acid secretion. These findings establish both PRP and PRGF as promising autologous therapies for endometrial optimization in assisted reproductive technology, with PRGF showing particular utility for treating repeated implantation failure and thin endometrium resistant to conventional therapies.

title: Enhancing Endometrial Receptivity Assessment Outcomes in Recurrent Implantation Failure: Intralipid Infusion and Acupuncture as Complementary Therapies
summary:
# Summary

This case study demonstrates successful treatment of recurrent implantation failure through a multimodal approach combining advanced reproductive technologies with complementary therapies. The patient presented with multiple contributing factors including low ovarian reserve, thin endometrial lining, intrauterine adhesions, and a displaced implantation window, which were systematically addressed using endometrial receptivity analysis (ERA) to optimize embryo transfer timing, interleukin-2-producing cell (NK cell) modulation via intralipid infusion and platelet-rich plasma (PRP), and acupuncture to enhance uterine blood flow and reduce stress. The GnRH antagonist protocol combined with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) stimulated ovulation and addressed male factor infertility, while human chorionic gonadotropin induced final egg maturation and progesterone supported uterine lining development and decidualization. This integrated treatment strategy resulted in successful pregnancy as confirmed by positive β-hCG testing, illustrating that personalized medicine combining conventional assisted reproductive technology with immunomodulatory and complementary interventions significantly improves outcomes in RIF patients compared to standard approaches alone.

title: Association of Hyaluronic Acid-Enriched Transfer Medium (HETM) with Clinical Pregnancy Outcome in Women Undergoing Frozen-Thawed Embryo Transfer (FET)
summary: Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a naturally occurring glycosaminoglycan found in reproductive fluids that enhances embryo implantation by binding to CD44 receptors on embryos, facilitating embryo-endometrial interactions. Hyaluronic acid-enriched transfer medium (HETM) has been developed to improve outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer procedures, particularly for women with recurrent implantation failure, with optimized exposure durations and HA concentrations potentially further improving implantation and clinical pregnancy rates. The efficacy of HETM is modulated by multiple factors including patient demographics, treatment protocols, embryo quality, age, and endometrial conditions, which may overshadow HA's benefits in some cases. While some prior studies on HA-enriched media showed limited advantage over standard media, current research anticipates improved outcomes in specific patient populations, particularly those with endometriosis-associated implantation dysfunction. The study will systematically evaluate HETM across various ovarian stimulation protocols and demographic groups to establish optimal dosing and application conditions for maximizing pregnancy success in assisted reproduction. Secondary outcomes including early pregnancy loss, preterm birth, and low birth weight will also be assessed to comprehensively evaluate the safety and efficacy profile of HETM in embryo transfer procedures.

title: Study Protocol: Association of Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) with Endometrium Thickness and Pregnancy Outcome in Women with Recurrent Implantation Failure (RIF)
summary: This study protocol investigates platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as a therapeutic intervention for women experiencing recurrent implantation failure (RIF) and thin endometrium. Thin endometrial lining is a critical limiting factor in fertility, as inadequate endometrium thickness directly reduces implantation and pregnancy rates. PRP functions through multiple mechanisms: it contains growth factors such as PDGF and cytokines that promote endometrial cell proliferation and enhance endometrial receptivity, while simultaneously regulating inflammatory processes that impair embryo implantation. Preliminary evidence demonstrates substantial clinical benefits, with PRP-treated patients achieving 21% implantation rates compared to 9.37% in control groups, alongside measurable increases in endometrium thickness. As an autologous biological material, PRP offers the additional advantages of minimal immune rejection risk and reduced infectious disease transmission risk. The study suggests that PRP successfully increases endometrium thickness and improves overall pregnancy outcomes in women with RIF, positioning it as a cost-effective and low-risk adjunctive therapy for addressing endometrial insufficiency in reproductive failure.

title: Comparison of Assisted Hatching Techniques and Their Impact on Clinical Pregnancy Outcomes with Optimization Strategies in In Vitro Fertilization
summary:
# Summary

Assisted hatching (AH) is an in vitro fertilization technique that improves implantation and clinical pregnancy rates by facilitating embryo escape from the zona pellucida, particularly benefiting patients with advanced maternal age, previous IVF failures, or thickened zona pellucida. Laser-assisted hatching (LAH) outperforms mechanical approaches, achieving 48% implantation rates and 52% clinical pregnancy rates compared to 26% and 37% in control groups, with 84% of LAH embryos successfully hatching before transfer. The technique addresses implantation failure, which contributes significantly to infertility by preventing embryo-uterine interaction during the window of implantation when the endometrium becomes progesterone-responsive and receptive. While AH reduces miscarriage rates slightly (18% for LAH versus 25% in controls), it shows limited benefits for fresh embryos in low-risk populations and does not significantly improve overall live birth rates. The procedure carries potential long-term risks including increased monozygotic twinning and developmental issues, suggesting AH should be reserved for high-risk cases such as repeated implantation failure and frozen-thawed embryo transfer.

title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of Platelet Rich Plasma Therapy in Enhancing Endometrial Receptivity for Women with Infertility: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Poor endometrial receptivity is a critical barrier to successful embryo implantation and a major cause of infertility and recurrent implantation failure. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) therapy has emerged as a promising treatment that improves endometrial receptivity, thickness, and pregnancy rates by delivering multiple growth factors—including platelet-derived growth factor, transforming growth factor-beta, vascular endothelial growth factor, and epidermal growth factor—which facilitate angiogenesis, tissue regeneration, and cellular proliferation. When injected into the uterine cavity, these growth factors enhance embryo-endometrium synchronization and promote the endometrial changes necessary for implantation, including increased blood flow and production of adhesion molecules. Beyond endometrial enhancement, PRP therapy also shows potential for treating poor ovarian reserve and improving oocyte quality when administered intraovarian. The review identifies additional factors that impair endometrial receptivity—including endometriosis, hormonal imbalances, and chronic endometritis—while confirming that progesterone and estrogen regulation of the endometrial molecular environment remain fundamental to successful implantation.

title: Association between hatching status and pregnancy outcomes in single blastocyst transfers: a retrospective cohort analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This retrospective cohort analysis demonstrates that blastocyst hatching status is a critical predictor of pregnancy outcomes in single embryo transfer cycles. Late-hatching blastocysts showed significantly superior clinical pregnancy rates (74.6%) and live birth rates (58.3%) compared to unhatched, early-hatching, and fully hatched embryos, with fully hatched blastocysts performing poorest. Embryo quality metrics, including inner cell mass and trophectoderm grades, as well as day-5 versus day-6 developmental timing, were independently associated with implantation success and live birth. The study also identified that optimal endometrial thickness (7-14 mm), maternal age, BMI, and blastocyst expansion level significantly influence pregnancy outcomes, while zona pellucida hardening can impair implantation but can be effectively addressed through laser-assisted hatching with 50% zona pellucida opening. These findings underscore that achieving optimal blastocyst expansion and late-hatching status, combined with favorable embryo morphology and endometrial conditions, represents the most promising approach for maximizing single embryo transfer success rates.

title: Impact of uterine cavity manipulation history on clinical and neonatal outcomes for in vitro fertilization-embryo transfers with donor sperm
summary: Prior hysteroscopic uterine cavity manipulation significantly enhances reproductive outcomes in patients undergoing in vitro fertilization with donor sperm. Compared to patients without uterine manipulation history, those with prior hysteroscopy demonstrated substantially higher implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and live birth rates, while simultaneously experiencing lower miscarriage rates. Multivariate analysis confirmed that hysteroscopy history independently predicted improved pregnancy outcomes, suggesting the procedure's beneficial effects persist beyond initial intervention. In contrast, other forms of uterine manipulation including curettage and complex uterine operations showed no significant association with clinical pregnancy outcomes, indicating that hysteroscopy may have unique mechanisms for enhancing endometrial receptivity or embryonic implantation. These findings suggest that diagnostic or therapeutic hysteroscopy prior to embryo transfer may provide meaningful clinical benefit for patients with compromised reproductive potential, potentially through improved uterine cavity integrity and endometrial function.

title: Efficacy of intravenous immunoglobulin in recurrent pregnancy loss: a retrospective analysis of patients with abnormal cellular immunity
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective analysis examines the role of abnormal cellular immunity in recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) and evaluates intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) as a therapeutic intervention. The study identifies elevated natural killer cell levels and activity, along with increased Th1/Th2 ratios, as key immune abnormalities associated with RPL and spontaneous abortion risk. IVIG treatment effectively suppresses these aberrant immune responses by downregulating NK cell levels and cytotoxicity while restoring favorable Th1/Th2 balance and modulating Th17/regulatory T cell populations. Among RPL patients with abnormal cellular immunity treated with IVIG, live birth rates improved significantly, with patients displaying both elevated NK cells and cytotoxicity achieving the highest success rates despite initial immune imbalance. Advanced maternal age and higher body mass index emerged as independent negative prognostic factors reducing live birth rates, whereas the number of previous miscarriages did not significantly influence outcomes. These findings support immune assessment and IVIG-based immunomodulation as effective strategies for managing RPL patients with documented cellular immune abnormalities.

title: IVF/ICSI treatment for patients with diminished ovarian reserve with or without Kuntai capsule pretreatment: a retrospective cohort study stratified by a controlled ovarian stimulation regimen
summary: This retrospective cohort study examined whether Kuntai capsule pretreatment, a traditional Chinese medicine composed of Rehmannia glutinosa, Coptis chinensis, Paeonia lactiflora, and other botanical components, improves IVF/ICSI outcomes in patients with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) across different controlled ovarian stimulation regimens. Although prior studies suggested Kuntai capsules could enhance ovarian reserve and improve ovarian response and embryo quality, this study found that pretreatment did not significantly improve the number of retrieved eggs, clinical pregnancy rates, or live birth rates in DOR patients. Notably, the Kuntai-treated group had lower baseline ovarian reserve markers (higher FSH and lower AMH), suggesting it was preferentially used in patients with poorer prognoses, and paradoxically showed reduced estradiol and progesterone levels on trigger day with increased oocyte failure rates in the progestin-primed ovarian stimulation regimen. However, Kuntai pretreatment did enhance the embryo utilization rate, indicating potential benefits for embryo quality despite lower overall egg retrieval numbers. These findings suggest that while Kuntai capsules may optimize embryo development in severely compromised ovarian reserve patients, they do not translate into improved fertility outcomes with current assisted reproductive techniques.

title: Cumulative Live Birth Rates in Women Undergoing Progestin-Primed Ovarian Stimulation Using Medroxyprogesterone Acetate, Dydrogesterone, and Progesterone: A Retrospective Analysis
summary: This retrospective analysis of 21,159 women compared three progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocols using medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA), dydrogesterone (DYG), and progesterone for assisted reproductive technology. All three progestins effectively suppress premature luteinizing hormone surges and endogenous gonadotropins through their different steroid receptor binding affinities, with MPA and DYG offering pharmacokinetic advantages including longer half-lives and stable plasma levels compared to natural progesterone. While MPA demonstrated superior embryo development metrics (higher oocyte retrieval, fertilization, and blastocyst formation rates), cumulative live birth rates after five frozen embryo transfer cycles were comparable across all three groups (MPA 87.6%, DYG 95.6%, progesterone 93.7%), with multivariable analysis showing no significant association between progestin type and live birth outcomes. Notably, outcomes varied by patient characteristics, with women aged ≥35 years and those with low antral follicle counts showing greater variation between progestin groups, while younger women and those with normal ovarian reserves achieved similar success rates regardless of progestin choice. These findings establish all three PPOS protocols as viable alternatives for ovarian stimulation with comparable reproductive efficacy, supporting individualized treatment selection based on patient-specific factors and clinical preferences.

title: The impact of inoculation with the inactivated COVID-19 vaccine on the outcomes of in vitro fertilization and embryo transfer: a cohort study of 1,258 women from Sichuan, China
summary: This cohort study of 1,258 women examined whether inactivated COVID-19 vaccination affects in vitro fertilization and embryo transfer outcomes in China. The inactivated vaccine did not significantly impact any measured parameter of assisted reproductive technology, including oocyte maturation and quality, fertilization rates, embryo development, implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or live birth rates, regardless of vaccination timing or number of doses administered. Similarly, COVID-19 mRNA vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 infection itself did not adversely affect ovarian reserve, ovarian stimulation response, follicle function, anti-Müllerian hormone levels, or endometrial receptivity. The findings provide reassurance that COVID-19 vaccination is compatible with fertility treatment, which is clinically important since SARS-CoV-2 infection during pregnancy increases risks for mechanical ventilation and preterm birth, while maternal vaccination offers passive immunization to the fetus.

title: Evaluation of gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist and antagonist protocols on pregnancy outcomes in POSEIDON groups 3 and 4: A randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial compared GnRH agonist and antagonist protocols for controlled ovarian stimulation in POSEIDON groups 3 and 4 patients (those with diminished ovarian reserve). The GnRH-antagonist protocol demonstrated significant pharmacological advantages, reducing stimulation duration (10.60 vs. 12.23 days) and total gonadotropin consumption (3225.67 vs. 4588.84 IU) compared to the agonist protocol while proving more cost-effective. Despite these practical benefits, both protocols yielded comparable reproductive outcomes, with no statistically significant differences in clinical pregnancy rates (15.8% vs. 17.8%), live birth rates (13.2% vs. 11.1%), implantation rates, or embryo quality. The agonist protocol produced higher estradiol levels on the trigger day, while the antagonist protocol showed elevated progesterone and LH levels. These findings suggest that for poor ovarian responders, the GnRH-antagonist protocol offers practical and economic advantages without compromising pregnancy outcomes, making it a preferable option despite the agonist protocol's historical use and slightly higher oocyte retrieval rates.

title: The Effects of Minimal Stimulation Protocol on Preimplantation Genetic Screening
summary:
# Summary

This study compared minimal stimulation protocols (MSP) with high-dose stimulation protocols (HSP) for assisted reproductive technology in women of advanced maternal age undergoing preimplantation genetic screening. While advanced age significantly reduces oocyte quality and increases chromosomal abnormalities, the research demonstrates that HSP yields substantially higher numbers of retrieved oocytes and metaphase II oocytes but produces no corresponding improvements in euploid embryo rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or live birth outcomes compared to MSP.
