The critical finding is that embryo aneuploidy is primarily determined by intrinsic oocyte quality and maternal age rather than stimulation intensity or oocyte yield, supporting the hypothesis that supraphysiological hormone levels from high-dose protocols may actually compromise endometrial receptivity without enhancing embryo competence. MSP achieves comparable euploidy rates and clinical outcomes while reducing ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk and preserving the natural hormonal environment, suggesting it represents a more effective and patient-friendly approach that prioritizes quality over quantity of oocytes.

title: Association of Pre-Implantation Uterine Artery Doppler with Clinical Pregnancy in Assisted Reproductive Technology: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that pre-implantation uterine artery Doppler parameters are significant predictors of clinical pregnancy success in assisted reproductive technology cycles. Women achieving clinical pregnancy exhibited lower uterine artery pulsatility index (PI) and higher peak systolic velocity (PSV), indicating reduced downstream vascular resistance and enhanced uterine perfusion. These improved hemodynamic parameters support critical endometrial changes including thickening, stromal transformation, and trophoblast invasion, all essential components of endometrial receptivity. The association was strongest when Doppler measurements were obtained during the menstrual cycle rather than on embryo transfer day, suggesting that timing of assessment influences predictive accuracy. Importantly, uterine receptivity requires the convergence of three factors: a viable embryo, hormonally synchronized endometrium, and adequate vascular supply, with impaired perfusion directly contributing to thin endometrial lining and lower pregnancy rates. These findings support the clinical utility of uterine artery Doppler as a non-invasive biomarker for assessing endometrial receptivity and optimizing outcomes in ART.

title: The Effect of Dual Trigger on In Vitro Fertilization/Intracytoplasmic
summary: This study demonstrates that dual trigger ovulation induction—combining a gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist (GnRH-a) with human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG)—significantly improves outcomes in in vitro fertilization cycles. The dual trigger mechanism works by leveraging GnRH-a's ability to stimulate both luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) surges, with FSH promoting LH receptor development on granulosa cells to enhance follicular responsiveness. Key findings show that dual trigger increases the quantity and maturation rate of retrieved oocytes, increases numbers of mature metaphase II oocytes and high-quality embryos, and reduces the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome compared to standard triggering protocols. Clinical outcomes are notably improved, with dual trigger associated with higher pregnancy and live birth rates. The technique appears particularly beneficial for patients with poor ovarian reserve or diminished oocyte maturation in previous cycles, though the optimal GnRH-a dosing for maximizing pregnancy rates requires further investigation. Overall, dual trigger represents an effective strategy to enhance both oocyte yield and embryo quality while mitigating iatrogenic complications in assisted reproduction.

title: Pregnancy Outcomes of Frozen-Thawed Blastocysts versus
summary: This study compares pregnancy outcomes between two embryo cryopreservation strategies in IVF: transferring frozen-thawed day-5 blastocysts (D5 group) versus thawing day-3 cleavage embryos, extending their culture to blastocyst stage, and then transferring (D3-5 group). While both approaches yielded comparable clinical outcomes—including positive hCG rates (~52-53%), clinical pregnancy rates (~45-48%), and live birth rates (~29-31%)—the D5 strategy demonstrated superior practical efficiency. The D5 group experienced zero cycle cancellations, whereas the D3-5 group had 65.3% cancellation rates due to failed blastocyst development, reflecting lower predictive value of cleavage-stage morphology for developmental potential. Blastocyst-stage culture before freezing enables natural embryo selection and avoids cryopreservation damage from insufficient cryoprotectant penetration and ice crystal formation that affects expanded blastocysts. Although the D3-5 approach doubles embryologist workload, artificial shrinkage techniques and optimized vitrification protocols can mitigate cryopreservation injury. Overall, direct transfer of frozen-thawed blastocysts emerges as the more efficient and patient-friendly option, reducing emotional and financial burden from cycle cancellations while maintaining equivalent reproductive outcomes.

title: Effect of acupuncture on endometrial receptivity in women with thin endometrium: study protocol for a single-centre, randomised, sham-controlled trial
summary: Thin endometrium represents a significant obstacle to successful pregnancy in assisted reproductive technology, as endometrial thickness directly correlates with clinical pregnancy and implantation rates. Current therapeutic approaches focus on estrogen supplementation and enhanced uterine perfusion via agents like low-dose aspirin and sildenafil citrate, though established risk factors including repeated intrauterine procedures, uterine infection, and clomiphene citrate use contribute to this condition. Preliminary evidence suggests acupuncture may improve endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms: increasing endometrial thickness and blood flow, promoting angiogenic activation via VEGFR2-dependent PI3K/AKT-ERK cascades, and upregulating receptivity markers including pinopodes, αvβ3, HOXA10, and HBEGF. Traditional Chinese medicine theory proposes that acupuncture, particularly deep needling of point BL33 to stimulate sacral nerves, restores Kidney Qi and circulation through the Chong and Ren meridians essential for female reproduction. This randomized sham-controlled trial protocol aims to rigorously evaluate acupuncture's efficacy in improving endometrial morphology and thickness, alongside emerging interventions such as granulocyte colony-stimulating factor infusion and stem cell therapy for women with thin endometrium undergoing fertility treatment.

title: Impact of spring festival on pregnancy outcomes in patients undergoing first embryo transfer: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that embryo transfer timing during the Spring Festival significantly impairs reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology (ART) cycles. Patients undergoing embryo transfer during the Spring Festival experienced substantially reduced clinical pregnancy, implantation, ongoing pregnancy, and live birth rates compared to non-festival periods (44.39% vs. 52.50%, 34.41% vs. 40.43%, 38.71% vs. 47.15%, and 37.33% vs. 44.82%, respectively), with the Spring Festival identified as an independent risk factor for poor prognosis. The detrimental effects are attributed to multiple behavioral and environmental disruptions during this holiday period, including overeating, excessive alcohol consumption, sleep disruption, and heightened emotional stress that collectively compromise self-management and pregnancy outcomes. The study further reveals broader seasonal patterns affecting ART success, with fertilization rates and sperm parameters showing significant correlation with variations in daylight duration and seasonal changes in temperature. Environmental factors such as light duration and temperature, alongside lifestyle-related factors including physical activity, emotional well-being, and sleep quality, were identified as important modulators of ART outcomes. These findings underscore the importance of optimizing the timing of embryo transfer to avoid major holiday periods and emphasize the multifactorial nature of seasonal influences on reproductive function.

title: The effect of ejaculatory abstinence time on in vitro fertilization outcomes
summary: This study evaluated how ejaculatory abstinence duration affects semen quality and in vitro fertilization outcomes. Longer periods of ejaculatory abstinence up to seven days significantly improved conventional semen analysis parameters, including semen volume, sperm concentration, and total motile sperm count, though sperm motility showed minimal improvement. Despite these improvements in semen quality metrics, increased ejaculatory abstinence had no significant effect on fertilization rates using either standard insemination or intracytoplasmic sperm injection, nor did it improve clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, or reduce miscarriage rates. These findings suggest that while ejaculatory abstinence duration substantially influences semen analysis results—which could affect male infertility diagnoses—this variation does not translate into improved reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology. The results indicate that standardizing ejaculatory abstinence recommendations for semen analysis interpretation may be more clinically relevant than optimizing abstinence duration specifically for IVF success.

title: Impact of race and ethnicity on in vitro fertilization outcomes in infertile women with polycystic ovary syndrome in the United States
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how race and ethnicity influence in vitro fertilization (IVF) outcomes in infertile women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Women with PCOS demonstrated significantly higher implantation, pregnancy, and live birth rates compared to non-PCOS controls, yet also exhibited higher rates of multiple pregnancies, pregnancy loss, and neonatal complications including lower birth weights, preterm delivery, and neonatal death. Critical racial and ethnic disparities emerged, with White women with PCOS achieving the highest live birth rates (49.5%), while African American and Asian women experienced substantially lower success rates and elevated pregnancy loss and neonatal mortality. African American women showed elevated serum estradiol and ovarian aromatase activity, higher AMH levels, greater oocyte retrieval, and increased BMI, yet paradoxically poorer reproductive outcomes, suggesting that biological and metabolic factors may not fully explain observed disparities. Obesity and elevated BMI were independently associated with poor pregnancy and neonatal outcomes across all groups. These findings underscore that while PCOS enhances certain fertility metrics in IVF, significant racial and ethnic inequities persist in live birth achievement and neonatal health outcomes, potentially reflecting complex interactions among genetic, metabolic, hormonal, and socioeconomic factors.

title: Does the weekend matter? evaluating the weekend effect on pregnancy outcomes in first-cycle fresh IVF/ICSI embryo transfers: a retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study of first-cycle fresh IVF/ICSI transfers reveals a significant "weekend effect" where oocyte retrieval timing relative to weekends substantially impacts reproductive outcomes. Oocyte retrievals performed near weekends—particularly in the near-weekend group—significantly reduced both clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates compared to midweek retrievals, with the effect being most pronounced for live birth outcomes. While weekend retrievals alone showed a trend toward lower pregnancy rates, the near-weekend group demonstrated statistically significant reductions in both endpoints. The study findings suggest that the timing of oocyte retrieval, which directly determines subsequent embryo transfer scheduling, plays an important role in determining IVF success, likely reflecting variations in laboratory conditions, staffing, or oocyte/embryo handling protocols associated with weekend-adjacent procedures. These results indicate that scheduling oocyte retrieval during midweek periods may optimize outcomes in fresh embryo transfer cycles. The mechanism underlying this weekend effect warrants further investigation to identify modifiable factors affecting laboratory performance and embryo quality during non-standard operating periods.

title: Correlation Between Hormonal Modulation and Pregnancy Outcomes: The Impact of Estrogen Priming and Endometrial Receptivity in Intrauterine Insemination
summary: This study demonstrates that estrogen priming significantly enhances pregnancy outcomes in intrauterine insemination by improving multiple aspects of endometrial function. Estrogen administration, particularly estradiol valerate during the early follicular phase, improves endometrial thickness and promotes endometrial proliferation, cellular differentiation, and vascularization—effects that are localized to the endometrium rather than affecting systemic follicular development. Enhanced endometrial receptivity, indicated by increased thickness (>9 mm) and a characteristic triple-line ultrasound pattern, correlates strongly with successful implantation and pregnancy rates, with the estrogen-primed group achieving approximately double the clinical pregnancy rate compared to controls. Mechanistically, exogenous estrogen extends the receptive phase of the endometrium and promotes synchronization between the embryo's developmental window and endometrial receptivity, facilitating both embryonic adhesion and invasion. The study identifies endometrial receptivity as a critical determinant of IUI success alongside other factors including maternal age, ovarian responsiveness, and sperm quality. Low-dose estradiol priming proved safe and well-tolerated, offering particular therapeutic benefit for women with thin endometrium or low ovarian reserve.

title: Progesterone Luteal Support in Natural Cycles for Unexplained Infertility: A Randomised Controlled Trial (The PiNC Trial)
summary: The PiNC Trial investigated whether luteal phase progesterone supplementation improves reproductive outcomes in women with unexplained infertility by targeting endometrial dysfunction. Progesterone is essential for endometrial secretory transformation and receptivity, produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation, and deficiencies in progesterone production or response can impair embryo implantation. In this randomized controlled trial, luteal phase progesterone supplementation increased live birth rates more than twofold (15.3% versus 7.0%) and showed trends toward improved clinical pregnancy rates and reduced miscarriage rates, though confidence intervals were wide. The mechanism may involve exogenous progesterone upregulating progesterone receptors through interactions with estradiol and androgens, potentially triggering positive feedback loops that enhance endogenous progesterone release and related factors like relaxin and oxytocin. The study highlights that progesterone resistance—a documented phenomenon in endometriosis and PCOS—may also underlie some cases of unexplained infertility, suggesting that simple vaginal progesterone supplementation could offer a noninvasive, cost-effective treatment for luteal phase defects in this population.

title: The effect of atosiban on pregnancy outcomes in different FET cycles: a single-center matched retrospective cohort study
summary: This single-center retrospective cohort study examined whether atosiban, an oxytocin receptor antagonist that reduces uterine contractions by blocking oxytocin signaling and lowering intracellular calcium levels, improves pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycles. The study identified that elevated estradiol during ovarian stimulation triggers increased oxytocin and prostaglandin F2α production, which stimulate excessive uterine contractions that may impair embryo implantation. While atosiban effectively reduces contraction frequency from 16/4 minutes to 6–2.6/4 minutes and enhances endometrial perfusion, it did not significantly improve live birth rates, clinical pregnancy rates, biochemical pregnancy rates, or reduce abortion or ectopic pregnancy rates in FET cycles. Key predictors of successful live birth included blastocyst-stage embryo transfer, greater endometrial thickness, younger maternal age, and fewer prior failed transfer attempts, while advanced maternal age reduced oocyte competence and pregnancy outcomes through multiple mechanisms. The findings suggest that despite atosiban's demonstrated ability to suppress pathologically elevated uterine contractions and enhance endometrial blood flow, its clinical benefit in unselected FET populations may be limited, indicating that embryo quality and endometrial receptivity remain dominant factors in determining implantation success.

title: Analysis of factors influencing clinical pregnancy rates in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of frozen-thawed embryo transfer (FET) cycles identified multiple independent predictors of clinical pregnancy outcomes. Female age emerged as the most critical factor, with younger age significantly improving pregnancy rates, while advancing maternal age reduced both oocyte quality and endometrial receptivity. Blastocyst-stage transfers substantially outperformed cleavage-stage transfers (61.14% versus 34.13% clinical pregnancy rates), attributed to improved embryo selection during extended culture and better alignment with the physiological implantation window. Embryo quality, reflected by the number of high-quality blastocysts and cleavage-stage embryos, along with elevated anti-Müllerian hormone levels indicating better ovarian reserve, independently predicted higher pregnancy success. Endometrial factors, particularly increased endometrial thickness on transfer day, also positively associated with clinical pregnancy, suggesting that optimal endometrial preparation alongside superior embryo selection are critical determinants of FET outcomes. These findings highlight that successful FET requires optimization of both gamete/embryo quality and uterine receptivity, with maternal age remaining the most influential variable across all analyses.

title: Associations between inactivated COVID-19 vaccination status and timing and fertility and pregnancy outcomes following frozen-thawed embryo transfer: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study examined how inactivated COVID-19 vaccination timing affects fertility outcomes in women undergoing frozen-thawed embryo transfer. The results demonstrate that inactivated COVID-19 vaccination significantly reduces clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and ongoing pregnancy rates compared to no vaccination, with the most pronounced effects observed in women vaccinated before ovarian stimulation, particularly when the interval between vaccination and stimulation was ≤90 days. The proposed mechanism involves vaccination-induced inflammatory responses that interfere with folliculogenesis and oocyte maturation, with outcomes improving when vaccination occurred >90 days before ovarian stimulation. Notably, while vaccination reduced reproductive outcomes, it was associated with lower gestational diabetes mellitus incidence in those who achieved pregnancy, and pre-pregnancy vaccination enhanced fetal immune regulation, suggesting potential protective benefits during gestation despite initial reproductive impacts.

title: Ovarian sensitivity index-based nomogram for predicting clinical pregnancy outcomes in patients with diminished ovarian reserve undergoing in vitro fertilization or intracytoplasmic sperm injection
summary:
# Summary

Diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) is characterized by reduced oocyte quantity and quality, reflected in decreased anti-Müllerian hormone and antral follicle count alongside elevated baseline follicle-stimulating hormone, resulting in significantly reduced pregnancy rates in patients undergoing IVF/ICSI. This study developed and validated a predictive nomogram incorporating the ovarian sensitivity index (OSI)—which measures ovarian responsiveness to gonadotropin stimulation—alongside patient age and controlled ovarian hyperstimulation protocol to predict clinical pregnancy outcomes in DOR patients. Multivariate analysis identified OSI, age, and COH protocol as independent predictors of pregnancy success, with the nomogram demonstrating good discriminative ability (AUC 0.744) in forecasting clinical pregnancy, live birth, and pregnancy complications. Among 448 DOR patients studied, 130 (29%) achieved clinical pregnancy, with 21.4% resulting in live births, 6.7% in miscarriage, and 0.89% in ectopic pregnancy. This nomogram provides clinicians with a quantitative tool for individualized counseling and treatment planning in DOR patients pursuing assisted reproduction, addressing a key clinical need where endometriosis and adenomyosis were excluded as confounding factors affecting reproductive outcomes.

title: Scoping Review of Letrozole in Assisted Reproductive Cycles: Efficacy and Outcomes Across Infertility Causes
summary:
# Summary

Letrozole, a selective aromatase inhibitor that suppresses estrogen production and increases follicle-stimulating hormone secretion, significantly enhances reproductive outcomes across multiple infertility etiologies. In assisted reproductive cycles, letrozole-only protocols achieve superior clinical pregnancy rates (50.57%) and live birth rates (45.58%), while combination protocols produce the highest ongoing pregnancy rates (58.3%) with the lowest miscarriage rates (14.86%). Letrozole's mechanism—reducing local estrogen levels, promoting follicular development, and preventing excessive luteinizing hormone elevation—makes it particularly effective for women with polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, substantially improving oocyte retrieval, endometrial receptivity, and implantation rates compared to conventional treatments. Additionally, letrozole reduces the total gonadotropin dose required, minimizes the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, and avoids the endometrial thinning and hyperandrogenism associated with clomiphene citrate. These findings position letrozole as a first-line treatment for ovulation induction and a valuable adjuvant in assisted reproductive technology protocols, with particular benefits for women with hormone-sensitive conditions seeking both fertility outcomes and reduced estrogen exposure.

title: Retrospective cohort study on cumulative live birth rate of in vitro fertilization/intracytoplasmic sperm injection after multiple complete cycles in patients with diminished ovarian reserve
summary: This retrospective cohort study examines cumulative live birth rates in diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) patients undergoing multiple IVF/ICSI cycles, with outcomes strongly influenced by patient age and ovarian reserve markers. Diminished ovarian reserve, defined by low anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH <1.1 ng/mL) and reduced antral follicle count, results from delayed childbirth and environmental factors, leading to decreased fertility and the need for assisted reproduction. Key findings demonstrate that younger patients (age <40 years) achieve satisfactory cumulative live birth rates with multiple treatment cycles, with at least 45% obtaining live births after repeated cycles, while patients aged 40 or above show dramatically reduced cumulative live birth rates that plateau at ≤2% per additional cycle. Age at embryo transfer, number of oocytes retrieved per cycle, and baseline ovarian reserve markers (AMH and antral follicle count) emerged as significant predictors of success, with older age and lower ovarian reserve associated with treatment discontinuation and reduced live birth outcomes. The study suggests that multiple IVF/ICSI cycles can effectively reduce reproductive disparities for younger DOR patients, though the benefit diminishes substantially for women 40 years or older, where extended treatment beyond four cycles yields minimal additional benefit.

title: Live birth rates in natural compared to artificial frozen blastocyst transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study compared natural cycle frozen embryo transfer (NC-FET) with artificial cycle FET (AC-FET) and found significantly higher live birth rates in NC-FET (43% versus 30%), accompanied by lower rates of biochemical pregnancies and spontaneous abortions. The superior outcomes in NC-FET may reflect the physiological advantages of endogenous corpus luteum function, as AC-FET cycles rely on exogenous hormone supplementation with supraphysiological estradiol levels that may impair endometrial vascularization and increase pregnancy loss. While AC-FET produced slightly heavier singleton infants, NC-FET demonstrated better reproductive outcomes overall, with 85% singleton births and reduced obstetric complications. Patient selection factors significantly influenced success, with advanced maternal age, elevated BMI, male factor infertility, and idiopathic infertility associated with lower live birth odds, whereas transfer of two blastocysts substantially improved success rates. The findings suggest NC-FET may be particularly advantageous for young, ovulatory women with healthy BMI and frozen blastocysts available for transfer.

title: Efficacy and safety of recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor in patients with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss: a protocol for a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss (uRPL) is associated with immune dysregulation at the maternal-fetal interface, where maternal rejection of the embryo may prevent successful implantation and pregnancy continuation. Recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) is hypothesized to improve pregnancy outcomes through multiple immunomodulatory mechanisms, including promotion of trophoblast proliferation and invasion, favorable shifting of the Th1/Th2 balance toward immune tolerance, and expansion of regulatory T cells. Previous studies have demonstrated that G-CSF treatment improves both conception rates and live birth rates in patients with uRPL, though evidence for live birth outcomes remains somewhat inconclusive. This multicenter randomized controlled trial aims to evaluate the efficacy and safety of G-CSF in Chinese women with uRPL, building on evidence that microRNA polymorphisms influence both immune regulation and endometrial receptivity. The study will assess whether G-CSF-mediated modulation of maternal-fetal immune tolerance provides therapeutic benefit, potentially offering a mechanistically-informed approach beyond empirical treatments such as dydrogesterone and aspirin currently used in fertility clinics.

title: The association between pregnancy outcomes of frozen thawed embryo transfers and previous SARS-CoV-2 infections
summary: Previous SARS-CoV-2 infection is associated with significantly reduced pregnancy outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles, including lower implantation rates (31.1% versus 47.2%), clinical pregnancy rates (42.7% versus 54.8%), ongoing pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to uninfected controls. The mechanism underlying these adverse effects involves direct viral exposure to reproductive tissues, as SARS-CoV-2 can bind to ACE-2 receptors, TMPRSS-2 proteins, and CD147 that are expressed on oocytes, preimplantation embryos, and the endometrium at critical developmental stages including the trophectoderm. SARS-CoV-2 infection during controlled ovarian stimulation impairs oocyte and embryo quality, reducing top-quality embryo rates and blastocyst formation, effects that persist even after viral clearance as evidenced by IgG antibodies against SARS-CoV-2 in follicular fluid remaining associated with compromised reproductive outcomes months post-infection. Additionally, the COVID-19 cytokine storm induces a hypercoagulable state that creates a toxic endometrial environment, further impairing embryo implantation and development. Both female and male partner infections during ovarian stimulation reduce pregnancy rates, highlighting that SARS-CoV-2 negatively impacts human reproductive health through multifactorial mechanisms affecting gamete quality, embryo development, and endometrial receptivity.

title: Comparison of double blastocyst transfer versus sequential transfer on pregnancy outcomes in individuals with frozen embryo transfer and a history of recurrent implantation failure: An RCT
summary: This randomized controlled trial compared sequential embryo transfer (combining day-3 cleavage-stage and day-5 blastocyst embryo transfers) with double blastocyst transfer in individuals with recurrent implantation failure undergoing frozen embryo transfer. Sequential transfer aims to optimize endometrial receptivity by exposing the endometrium to embryos at different developmental stages, thereby triggering cytokine and growth factor release that enhances the embryo-endometrial interaction. However, the study found that both approaches yielded comparable outcomes across all measured parameters, including chemical pregnancy rates (~50%), clinical pregnancy rates, implantation rates (~29%), early miscarriage rates, and multiple pregnancy rates (~24%), indicating that sequential transfer does not significantly improve assisted reproductive technology outcomes compared to double blastocyst transfer. While some prior studies suggested sequential transfer improves implantation and clinical pregnancy rates with reduced early miscarriage risk, this trial demonstrates comparable efficacy between the two methods, suggesting that embryo quality and individual endometrial receptivity may be the primary determinants of success in recurrent implantation failure cases rather than the transfer strategy alone.

title: Effect of prior anti-tuberculosis treatment on assisted reproductive outcomes in infertile women: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Genital tuberculosis (TB) contributes to infertility through multiple mechanisms, including tubal obstruction, ovulatory dysfunction, and decreased endometrial receptivity. This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that anti-TB treatment significantly improves assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes in infertile women with prior pulmonary TB lesions, with treated patients achieving live birth rates comparable to non-TB controls despite having lower antral follicle counts and endometrial thickness. Women who received anti-TB treatment exhibited higher live birth rates and lower miscarriage rates (11.3% vs 19.1%) compared to untreated counterparts, and notably produced more retrieved oocytes and good-quality embryos. Uterine TB involvement was particularly detrimental, reducing fertilization rates, implantation rates, and pregnancy rates while increasing spontaneous abortion risk; conversely, isolated tubal TB showed pregnancy outcomes similar to non-TB groups. Patient age and body mass index emerged as independent predictors of pregnancy success, with younger patients and those with lower BMI demonstrating superior ART outcomes across all reproductive parameters.

title: Spontaneous ovulation, hormonal profiles, and the impact of progesterone timing variation on outcomes in natural proliferative phase frozen embryo transfer cycles with single euploid blastocyst transfer
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates a natural proliferative phase frozen embryo transfer (NPP-FET) protocol with flexible dydrogesterone initiation in patients undergoing single euploid blastocyst transfer. The protocol preserves spontaneous ovulation by initiating progesterone supplementation based on predefined criteria (follicle diameter ≥14 mm, serum estradiol >150 pg/mL, progesterone <1.5 ng/mL, and endometrial thickness ≥7 mm), rather than relying on artificial hormonal priming like hormone replacement therapy (HRT-FET). Spontaneous ovulation occurred in all 196 participants, with serum estradiol and luteinizing hormone peaking two days before ovulation and progesterone rising progressively thereafter, demonstrating physiological hormone dynamics. The NPP-FET protocol achieved clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, and clinical loss rates of 66.3%, 58.7%, and 11.5% respectively, with embryo morphology and biopsy day—but not progesterone timing variations—predicting pregnancy outcomes. These findings suggest that NPP-FET offers a scheduling-friendly alternative to HRT-FET while maintaining physiologically grounded endocrine function and competitive reproductive outcomes, potentially avoiding the increased risks of preeclampsia, placenta previa, and cesarean delivery associated with artificial hormone replacement protocols.

title: Serum LH levels before progesterone administration significantly affect pregnancy outcomes in hormone replacement therapy-frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

In hormone replacement therapy-frozen-thawed embryo transfer (HRT-FET) cycles, serum luteinizing hormone (LH) levels measured before progesterone administration significantly influence pregnancy outcomes. The study demonstrates that patients with LH levels in the 51-75th percentile range exhibit higher live birth rates and lower miscarriage rates compared to both lower and higher percentile groups, while those with suppressed LH levels show reduced live birth rates and increased miscarriage risk. LH exerts these effects through multiple mechanisms, including binding to LH/chorionic gonadotropin receptors in both ovarian and uterine tissues to enhance luteal function and influence endometrial receptivity. Notably, these associations were pronounced in non-pituitary down-regulation groups but absent in patients receiving GnRH agonist pretreatment, which pharmacologically suppresses LH secretion. These findings suggest that optimizing serum LH levels to intermediate ranges represents a clinically relevant consideration for improving pregnancy outcomes in HRT-FET cycles, particularly in protocols that do not include pituitary suppression.

title: A comparative analysis of progestin-primed ovarian stimulation versus GnRH antagonists protocols pertaining to stimulation parameters and embryological outcomes in patients with endometrioma
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, particularly endometrioma, impairs reproductive outcomes through chronic inflammation and oxidative stress that directly compromise oocyte quality and ovarian response. Endometriomas release reactive oxygen species and inflammatory cytokines that degrade follicular quality, and this inflammatory burden is further exacerbated when high sex steroid levels during conventional ovarian stimulation aggravate the endometriotic microenvironment. The progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocol addresses these challenges by suppressing pituitary activity with progestins, which reduces inflammatory molecules in follicular fluid and restores folliculogenesis, resulting in superior embryological outcomes including higher numbers of usable cleavage-stage embryos and mature oocytes compared to GnRH antagonist protocols. Although GnRH antagonist protocols require higher total gonadotropin doses and longer treatment duration, PPOS produces similar live birth rates when combined with frozen embryo transfer, which itself improves outcomes by reducing miscarriage rates and increasing live births. This comparison demonstrates that in endometrioma patients, PPOS offers a therapeutically advantageous approach by simultaneously managing the inflammatory pathology of endometriosis while optimizing embryo development, particularly when followed by frozen embryo transfer to allow endometrial recovery.

title: A valuable predictive model for optimizing the timing of oocyte retrieval: a retrospective analysis of oocyte retrieval time in 49,961 oocyte pickup (OPU) cycles
summary: This large retrospective analysis of nearly 50,000 oocyte retrieval cycles identified multiple clinical and biochemical factors that significantly influence the optimal timing between final maturation trigger and oocyte retrieval. Female age, antral follicle count, controlled ovarian hyperstimulation protocol, follicle size on trigger day, and hormone levels (estradiol, progesterone, and luteinizing hormone) emerged as the key predictive variables, with younger age and higher hormone levels associated with longer optimal retrieval intervals. The choice of stimulation protocol substantially affected retrieval timing, with long agonist protocols requiring longer intervals compared to short agonist protocols, while mild stimulation and GnRH antagonist protocols required shorter intervals. Trigger method also mattered, with GnRH agonist and hCG triggers associated with shorter intervals compared to dual triggers. Importantly, optimizing this trigger-to-retrieval interval directly impacts assisted reproductive technology outcomes by influencing oocyte maturation rates and fertilization success. The study provides a predictive framework showing that hormone-triggered cascades—including meiosis resumption, cumulus cell expansion, and luteinization—require individualized timing windows that depend on patient characteristics and stimulation approach.

title: Sperm Selection Using Microfluidic Techniques Significantly Decreases Sperm DNA Fragmentation (SDF), Enhancing Reproductive Outcomes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: Microfluidic-based sperm selection significantly improves reproductive outcomes by reducing sperm DNA fragmentation and enhancing sperm quality parameters compared to conventional techniques like density gradient centrifugation and swim-up methods. By eliminating centrifugation steps and sample manipulation, microfluidic chips lower sperm DNA fragmentation (mean difference = −9.98, p < 0.00001) while simultaneously improving progressive motility, total motility, and morphology. The reduction in sperm DNA fragmentation is clinically important, as elevated fragmentation is associated with poor embryonic quality and increased miscarriage risk. Microfluidic selection—particularly devices like ZyMot and FertileChip—substantially improves fertility rates, with significant increases in fertilization rate, implantation rate (OR = 4.51), clinical pregnancy rate (OR = 1.73), and live birth rate (OR = 1.59–1.65) in intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) cycles. These techniques mimic natural sperm selection by utilizing active swimming or passive filtration through microchannels rather than relying on harsh centrifugation, thereby reducing oxidative stress-induced DNA damage. The evidence suggests microfluidic sperm selection offers particular benefit for male factor infertility and cases with elevated sperm DNA fragmentation.

title: An Artificial Intelligence-Based Model to Predict Pregnancy After Intrauterine Insemination: A Retrospective Analysis of 9501 Cycles
summary: This retrospective study of 9,501 IUI cycles developed a machine learning model to predict pregnancy outcomes by analyzing clinical and semen parameters. A linear support vector machine (SVM) classifier outperformed seven other machine learning models, achieving an area under the curve of 0.78 when using only the top four predictive features. Pre-wash sperm concentration emerged as the strongest predictor of pregnancy success, followed by ovarian stimulation protocol type, cycle length, and maternal age, while paternal age proved to be the least informative variable. IUI was evaluated as a first-line treatment for multiple conditions including unexplained infertility, mild endometriosis, ovulation dysfunction, and moderate male factor infertility, with pregnancy rates varying from 7% with oral stimulation agents to 12% with recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone. The study demonstrated that feature selection substantially improved model interpretability and clinical applicability, as models utilizing fewer, highly predictive features are more practical for clinical decision-making than those requiring comprehensive datasets. These findings highlight that semen quality at baseline, before sperm processing, retains significant prognostic value for predicting IUI success.

title: Comparison of the outcomes of patients with non-male factor infertility and poor ovarian response undergoing in vitro fertilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: In women with non-male factor infertility and poor ovarian response, this study compared conventional in vitro fertilization (IVF) and intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) outcomes. Although the ICSI group demonstrated superior embryological metrics, including higher two-pronuclear fertilization rates and higher-quality embryos, the IVF group achieved higher overall fertilization rates (89.31% vs. 83.06%). Critically, no significant differences were observed between the two approaches in clinical outcomes, including clinical pregnancy rates, ongoing pregnancy rates, live birth rates, or cumulative live birth rates. The findings suggest that while ICSI can prevent fertilization failure in cases with low oocyte yield and may enhance embryo quality in advanced maternal age, the insemination method itself does not significantly affect reproductive success in this patient population. This contrasts with ICSI's established efficacy in severe male factor infertility but aligns with evidence that ICSI is only marginally less effective than IVF in other infertility etiologies such as endometriosis and unexplained infertility. The results indicate that for non-male factor poor responders, conventional IVF remains a viable first-line approach.

title: Gonadotropin dose selection for repeat IVF cycles in POSEIDON Groups 1 and 2
summary:
# Gonadotropin Dose Selection for Repeat IVF Cycles in POSEIDON Groups 1 and 2

This study evaluated whether increasing gonadotropin (Gn) doses improves reproductive outcomes in POSEIDON Groups 1 and 2 patients (younger and older women with unexpectedly poor ovarian response) undergoing repeat IVF cycles. The Additive group, which received higher initial and total Gn doses in their second cycle, produced more retrieved oocytes, metaphase II oocytes, and higher fertilization rates compared to the Control group; however, these quantitative improvements did not translate into superior pregnancy outcomes. Notably, the Control group achieved a slightly higher cumulative live birth rate (31.5% versus 28.9%, not statistically significant) despite using lower Gn doses, and experienced higher fresh embryo transfer success and multiple pregnancy rates. The study concluded that increasing Gn doses under the GnRH antagonist protocol in POSEIDON Groups 1 and 2 increases treatment costs without improving cumulative live birth rates, suggesting that higher initial Gn doses do not optimize reproductive outcomes in these poor-prognosis patients. These findings indicate that dose escalation alone may not overcome the reduced FSH sensitivity characteristic of these patient groups, and that other treatment strategies warrant investigation.

title: One-year cumulative live birth rate associated with the number of oocytes in ovarian stimulation with follitropin delta: a pooled analysis of four randomized controlled trials
summary:
# Summary

This pooled analysis of four randomized controlled trials involving 1,746 patients examined the relationship between oocyte retrieval number and one-year cumulative live birth rates (CLBR) following ovarian stimulation with follitropin delta using a GnRH antagonist protocol. The study found that CLBR increased progressively with the number of oocytes retrieved, plateauing between 21–25 oocytes, with rates exceeding 60% at >15 oocytes retrieved. However, fresh cycle live birth rates declined beyond 14 oocytes, whereas CLBR continued to improve through subsequent frozen embryo transfer cycles, demonstrating the clinical benefit of cryopreservation strategies including vitrification and blastocyst-stage culture. Age was a significant factor, with CLBR decreasing from 57.1% in patients <35 years to 35.8% in patients ≥38 years, though older patients demonstrated continued CLBR improvement from 15 oocytes onward, contrasting with younger patients who plateaued at lower oocyte numbers. Anti-Müllerian hormone levels did not significantly predict CLBR outcomes. These findings suggest that optimal oocyte retrieval targets should be individualized by age, with younger patients requiring fewer oocytes to minimize ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk while maintaining favorable cumulative reproductive outcomes.

title: Age does not affect maximal endometrial thickness achieved in frozen embryo transfer cycles: a SARTCORS study
summary:
# Summary

This large retrospective study from SARTCORS databases examined how maternal age affects endometrial thickness and pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer cycles, comparing gestational carriers (GC) with non-oocyte donor (NOD) recipients. While age demonstrated minimal direct effects on maximal endometrial thickness achieved—with thickness remaining largely stable across age groups despite statistically weak associations—advancing maternal age significantly reduced clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates in both GC and NOD cycles, independent of endometrial thickness changes. Notably, NOD recipients consistently achieved slightly lower endometrial thickness and had substantially lower pregnancy rates compared to gestational carriers, suggesting that an age-related endometrial factor beyond thickness measurements contributes to reduced fertility in older recipients. Thin endometrium (less than 8 mm) correlated with reduced implantation rates and adverse neonatal outcomes including preterm delivery, lower birthweight, and cesarean delivery, though most cycles achieved adequate thickness. These findings indicate that reproductive aging affects pregnancy success through mechanisms beyond simple endometrial thickness changes, implicating age-related alterations in endometrial receptivity and transcriptomic profiles as important contributors to declining fertility with advanced maternal age.

title: Reproductive Outcomes of Transferring Blastocysts Derived From Frozen–Thawed Cleavage Embryos: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis evaluates frozen-thawed blastocysts derived from cleavage-stage embryos (FT-CDB) compared to direct frozen blastocyst transfers in assisted reproductive technology. Participants receiving FT-CDB transfers demonstrated significantly higher odds of clinical pregnancy (OR 1.24) and live birth (OR 1.31), with reduced pregnancy failure rates, though cryosurvival and implantation rates were comparable between groups. The benefits of blastocyst-stage culture include improved embryo selection through extended culture and better synchronization with endometrial receptivity, while cryopreservation enables cumulative pregnancy cycles and prevents ovarian hyperstimulation. However, the FT-CDB approach carries important limitations including potential high cancellation rates, reduced developmental potential from multiple freeze-thaw cycles, and possible selection bias favoring morphologically superior cleavage-stage embryos. Clinical factors including endometriosis, male factor, and recurrent implantation failure influence outcomes, though maternal age, BMI, and endometrial thickness did not significantly affect FT-CDB transfer superiority. The heterogeneity in study results and vitrification protocols suggests that while FT-CDB transfer shows promise for improving reproductive outcomes, patient selection and embryo quality assessment remain critical considerations for clinical implementation.

title: Comparative analysis of hCG and dual-trigger protocols in IVF for advanced maternal age women: a single‑center retrospective cohort study based on propensity score matching
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study compared human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) and dual-trigger (hCG plus GnRH-agonist) protocols for final oocyte maturation in advanced maternal age women undergoing in vitro fertilization. While hCG alone carries a high risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome due to its prolonged luteotropic effects and lacks FSH activity necessary for optimal oocyte maturation, the dual-trigger protocol combines hCG with GnRH-agonist to enhance oocyte maturation rates, embryo quality, and reduce ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk. The dual-trigger group achieved higher fertilization rates, required lower gonadotropin antagonist doses, and showed a shorter time to live birth compared to the hCG group. Importantly, both protocols yielded comparable cumulative live birth rates and clinical outcomes in fresh and frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles, suggesting that the dual-trigger protocol may be preferable due to improved laboratory parameters and efficiency while maintaining safety and efficacy in this challenging population with age-related decline in ovarian reserve.

title: Pregnancy and neonatal outcomes of borderline blastocysts: a novel evaluation model based on inner cell mass and trophectoderm parameters
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a modified blastocyst grading system that improves consistency and inter-observer agreement in embryo assessment by incorporating quantitative parameters including inner cell mass (ICM) morphometry and trophectoderm (TE) cell counting, addressing the higher variability inherent in purely visual assessment methods. The grading system categorized blastocysts into groups A, B, and C based on ICM and TE characteristics, with group C embryos (ICM grade C with <60 μm diameter or TE grade C with <5 cells) demonstrating significantly lower clinical pregnancy rates (9.1%) and live birth rates (5.5%) compared to groups A and B (pregnancy rates 34.9% and 25.3%; live birth rates 25.6% and 25.3%, respectively). Importantly, borderline blastocysts retained potential for implantation and live birth without causing adverse neonatal outcomes, as gestational age, birth weight, and malformation rates did not differ among groups. Day 5 blastocyst transfers were associated with higher live birth rates than day 6 transfers, while maternal age within the 22-38 year range and TE grade were not independent predictors of outcome. These findings suggest that the modified grading system enables more reliable embryo selection and reduces unnecessary repeat treatment cycles for patients with only borderline-quality blastocysts available for transfer.

title: Growth Hormone Therapy in Recurrent Implantation Failure: Stratification by FSH Receptor Polymorphism (Asn680Ser) Reveals Genotype-Specific Benefits
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that growth hormone (GH) therapy effectively treats recurrent implantation failure by elevating endometrial leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) and improving embryo development, with benefits highly dependent on FSH receptor (FSHR) genotype stratification. Patients carrying the FSHR Ser/Ser genotype, which confers FSH resistance and typically requires higher hormone doses, showed markedly superior responses to GH supplementation, achieving a 50% cumulative live birth rate compared to 13% without treatment, along with increased fertilization rates, embryo numbers, and blastulation rates. In contrast, patients with FSH-sensitive genotypes (Ser/Asn and Asn/Asn) showed minimal benefit from GH supplementation, suggesting a targeted therapeutic approach. The mechanisms underlying GH's benefits involve its multi-level actions on follicular development, oocyte maturation, and endometrial receptivity through direct receptor signaling in granulosa cells, theca cells, and endometrial tissue, as well as indirect modulation of the insulin-like growth factor system and interaction with critical receptors (FSH receptor, luteinizing hormone receptor, bone morphogenetic protein receptor 1B, vascular endothelial growth factor). Notably, elevated LIF levels in GH-treated Ser/Ser patients correlated with improved implantation outcomes, identifying LIF as a key endometrial biomarker linking GH action to successful implantation. This genotype-stratified approach suggests that FSHR polymorphism assessment could enable personalized selection of patients most likely to benefit from GH co-treatment in assisted reproduction.

title: Sequential embryo transfer efficacy in enhancing pregnancy outcomes: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Sequential embryo transfer (SEQET) significantly improves reproductive outcomes by enhancing the availability of embryos at various developmental stages within the implantation window, thereby increasing clinical pregnancy rates, chemical pregnancy rates, implantation rates, and live births compared to conventional blastocyst transfer alone. The mechanism underlying these improvements centers on optimizing endometrial receptivity, which is critical since approximately 60% of implantation failures are attributed to endometrial factors rather than embryonic quality. Synchronization between embryo transfer timing and the endometrial receptive window, coupled with the embryo's inherent ability to stimulate endometrial receptivity, represents the key biological principle driving SEQET's efficacy. Additional strategies to enhance endometrial receptivity include hysteroscopic treatment of intrauterine pathologies, myomectomy, endometrial injury, low-dose aspirin, vaginal sildenafil, and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) administration to improve uterine blood flow. By transferring cleavage-stage embryos on day 2 followed by morula or blastocyst-stage embryos on day 5, SEQET ensures optimal embryo-endometrium synchronization and substantially improves pregnancy and implantation outcomes. This approach is particularly relevant for patients with repeated implantation failure, offering a mechanism-based strategy to overcome implantation defects attributable to endometrial dysfunction.

title: Acupuncture as an adjunct therapy for enhancing endometrial receptivity in female infertility: a literature review
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial receptivity, the ability of the endometrium to accept and support embryo implantation, is critical for successful pregnancy and is often compromised in infertile women, contributing to recurrent implantation failure, pregnancy loss, and adverse outcomes including preeclampsia and intrauterine growth restriction. This literature review demonstrates that acupuncture and electroacupuncture improve endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms, including enhanced uterine blood flow, increased endometrial thickness, improved pinopode formation, and activation of signaling pathways such as PI3K/AKT and LIF/STAT3, which collectively upregulate key receptivity markers including HOXA10, leukemia inhibitory factor, and integrin αvβ3. Clinical evidence shows that acupuncture combined with embryo transfer increases clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates, positioning it as a promising adjunct therapy for women with defective endometrial receptivity caused by conditions such as PCOS, adenomyosis, polyps, or thin endometrium. The mechanisms underlying acupuncture's benefits appear distinct from conventional Western medical interventions such as estrogen, progesterone, growth hormone, and platelet-rich plasma, suggesting potential complementary therapeutic value in addressing the multifactorial nature of endometrial dysfunction in infertility.

title: The importance of measuring the endometrial cavity length in deciding the ideal place for embryo transfer: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that endometrial cavity length (ECL) is a critical determinant of optimal embryo transfer positioning and clinical pregnancy rates in assisted reproductive technology. The researchers identified that the ideal distance from the embryo to the uterine fundus (measured as air bubble-fundus distance, BFD) varies significantly based on ECL: for shorter cavities (ECL < 32 mm), the optimal BFD is 11 mm with pregnancy rates increasing 13.5% per unit increase below this threshold, while for longer cavities (ECL ≥ 32 mm), the optimal BFD is 15 mm with pregnancy rates increasing 3.99% per unit increase below this point. By stratifying patients according to endometrial cavity length and adjusting embryo transfer depth accordingly, the study found that relative positioning methodology substantially improves transfer accuracy and clinical outcomes. These findings challenge the existing debate in the field by highlighting that previous conflicting results likely stemmed from failure to account for individual variation in uterine cavity dimensions when determining embryo placement depth. The study underscores that personalizing embryo transfer positioning based on measured endometrial anatomy enhances the precision of assisted reproduction procedures and significantly impacts pregnancy success rates.

title: Efficacy of hormone pre‐treatment before ART to improve reproductive outcomes in infertile women with endometriosis: Network meta‐analysis of randomized controlled trials
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting 20-50% of infertile women, impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms including tubal adhesions, poor folliculogenesis, and reduced endometrial receptivity. This network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluated hormone pre-treatments before assisted reproductive technology, finding that GnRH agonists—which suppress pituitary function, reduce ovarian steroidogenesis, and decrease inflammatory cytokines and oxidative stress—improve implantation rates when administered in long (1 month) or ultralong (3 months) protocols. Interestingly, while GnRH agonists enhance implantation, they paradoxically reduce live birth rates, whereas progestin pre-treatment (including dienogest and medroxyprogesterone acetate), which inhibits inflammatory cytokines like interleukin-8 and reduces endometriotic lesions, similarly shows reduced live birth rates compared to no pre-treatment. Dienogest therapy specifically decreases developing follicles, retrieved oocytes, fertilized oocytes, and blastocyst formation, suggesting that short-term progestin pre-treatment may impair ovarian response without conferring clinical benefit. These findings suggest that although hormone pre-treatments can modify endometriosis-associated pathology through anti-inflammatory mechanisms, in vitro fertilization without pharmacological pre-conditioning may remain the most effective approach to achieve live births in endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: The effect of autologous platelet-rich plasma intrauterine perfusion on pregnancy outcomes of frozen-thawed embryo transfer in patients with chronic endometritis
summary:
# Platelet-Rich Plasma Treatment for Chronic Endometritis and Frozen-Thawed Embryo Transfer Outcomes

Chronic endometritis creates a persistent pro-inflammatory state characterized by aberrant plasma cell infiltration, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, and altered immune microenvironment that impairs endometrial receptivity and leads to infertility, recurrent implantation failure, and recurrent pregnancy loss. Autologous platelet-rich plasma intrauterine perfusion, administered following antibiotic therapy with doxycycline, effectively treats chronic endometritis by reducing pro-inflammatory markers such as interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor, and matrix metalloproteinases while promoting endometrial repair and receptivity through multiple growth factors including platelet-derived growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor, and insulin-like growth factor. Platelet-rich plasma treatment significantly improves clinical outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles, with patients receiving PRP demonstrating higher clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and increased endometrial thickness compared to controls, with multiple PRP perfusions yielding superior outcomes to single treatments. The therapeutic mechanisms involve platelet-derived immunomodulation that reduces leukocyte infiltration, shifts the Th1/Th2 balance toward Th2 dominance, and upregulates genes associated with endometrial receptivity. These findings support a combined treatment strategy of antibiotic therapy followed by adjunctive PRP intrauterine perfusion for improving reproductive outcomes in patients with chronic endometritis undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: Comparison of Letrozole Versus Clomiphene Citrate Combined With Human Menopausal Gonadotropin in Women With Clomiphene-Resistant Anovulatory Infertility Undergoing Intrauterine Insemination: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: This retrospective cohort study compared letrozole versus clomiphene citrate (CC), each combined with human menopausal gonadotropin (HMG), for treating clomiphene-resistant anovulatory infertility in women undergoing intrauterine insemination. Both medications work through distinct mechanisms: CC blocks estrogen receptors on the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis to stimulate follicle-stimulating hormone release, while letrozole inhibits aromatase to reduce estrogen synthesis and enhance follicular responsiveness to FSH. The letrozole plus HMG combination achieved a significantly higher pregnancy rate of 54.7% compared to CC plus HMG at 46%, despite similar follicular development outcomes between groups. Notably, letrozole was associated with minimal adverse effects on endometrial thickness and cervical mucus production, whereas CC's anti-estrogenic effects reduced endometrial receptivity and cervical mucus quality, potentially limiting conception despite successful ovulation induction. Both drug combinations effectively reduced the required gonadotropin dose and decreased ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk compared to HMG monotherapy. The study suggests that letrozole combined with HMG represents a superior first-line approach for clomiphene-resistant anovulatory infertility, particularly in preserving endometrial function critical for implantation.

title: Adjusting key performance indicators in ART laboratories according to controlled ovarian stimulation protocol
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates how different controlled ovarian stimulation (COS) protocols affect key performance indicators (KPIs) in assisted reproductive technology. The flexible prolonged luteal GnRH agonist long protocol (FPL GnRH-a) achieved the highest implantation rate (46.6%) and blastocyst development outcomes, while the micro-stimulation protocol yielded significantly lower fertilization and implantation rates (13.1%). The short luteal phase GnRH agonist protocol (LPS GnRH-a) produced superior good blastocyst development rates (30.4%) but unexpectedly lower implantation rates than FPL, suggesting a disconnect between embryo quality and implantation potential. Female age emerged as a critical variable, with all KPIs declining steadily in women over 35 years, and COS protocol selection should be individualized based on age, body mass index, and ovarian reserve. These findings demonstrate that adjusting laboratory standards and embryo evaluation criteria according to the specific COS protocol employed is essential for optimizing assisted reproductive outcomes.

title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Single-Dose GnRH Agonist on the Day of Frozen Embryo Transfer in Artificial Cycles: Preliminary Evidence from Randomized Trials
summary:
# Summary

A single-dose GnRH agonist administered on the day of frozen embryo transfer in artificial cycles significantly improves reproductive outcomes, increasing clinical pregnancy rates (56.5% vs. 47.4%) and live birth rates (34.3% vs. 23%) compared to control cycles. The therapeutic mechanism involves GnRH receptors expressed on both the endometrium and pre-implantation embryos, where agonist stimulation upregulates critical implantation molecules including integrin αvβ3 and HOXA10, while enhancing embryonic development and hatching potential. In artificial hormone replacement cycles that lack endogenous corpus luteum formation, GnRH agonist supplementation promotes an LH surge that supports corpus luteum function and endogenous progesterone production during early pregnancy. Additionally, GnRH agonist suppression reduces uterine estrogenic and inflammatory activity, offering particular benefits for patients with endometriosis or adenomyosis experiencing recurrent implantation failure. This meta-analysis demonstrates that luteal phase GnRH agonist support represents an evidence-based strategy to enhance endometrial receptivity and embryonic potential during frozen embryo transfer cycles, with no increase in miscarriage rates observed across included studies.

title: The impact of completely shrunken blastocysts on clinical outcomes in vitrified-warmed single blastocyst transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the clinical outcomes of completely shrunken blastocysts (CSBs) in vitrified-warmed single embryo transfer cycles, revealing that blastocyst re-expansion post-thawing is the critical determinant of reproductive success. Completely shrunken blastocysts that fail to re-expand within 2-4 hours showed significantly lower clinical pregnancy rates (28.8% vs. 61.5%), ongoing pregnancy rates (22.1% vs. 52.9%), and live birth rates (20.2% vs. 50.0%) compared to re-expanded blastocysts, despite retaining some implantation potential particularly when derived from day 5 embryos. The mechanism underlying these differences involves cryo-injury during vitrification-warming, which impairs trophectoderm cell function and prevents the Na/K-ATPase-driven ionic gradient necessary for blastocoel cavity re-expansion through tight junction sealing. Day 5 blastocyst formation was a strong positive predictor of pregnancy outcomes, with day 5 blastocysts showing 3-fold higher pregnancy rates than day 6 blastocysts, likely due to superior embryo quality and better synchronization with endometrial receptivity. Artificial shrinkage prior to vitrification emerged as a protective strategy, reducing ice crystal formation and improving post-thaw survival rates. These findings underscore the importance of blastocyst expansion status as both a marker of cryo-injury and a predictor of implantation potential in frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: Incidence and risk factors of monozygotic twinning following ART: analysis of 154 671 live births resulting from single embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

This large study of over 154,000 live births from single embryo transfer procedures identified blastocyst-stage embryo transfer as the primary risk factor for monozygotic twinning (MZT) in assisted reproductive technology (ART), with approximately two-fold increased odds compared to cleavage-stage transfer. Fresh embryo transfers significantly elevated MZT risk, likely due to the hyperstimulated endometrial environment created by elevated estrogen and progesterone levels following ovarian stimulation, which may promote embryonic splitting. Conversely, vitrified-thaw embryo transfers reduced MZT risk by thirteen percent relative to fresh transfers, suggesting that freezing and thawing procedures may stabilize the embryo and reduce splitting susceptibility. The mechanisms underlying increased MZT with blastocyst culture appear to involve extended culture conditions affecting zona pellucida hardening and destabilization of intracellular bonds through variations in calcium, glucose-induced apoptosis, and growth factor alterations. While single embryo transfer remains the key strategy for preventing dizygotic multiple pregnancies in ART, this study demonstrates that MZT persists as an important adverse outcome risk, with declining incidence over the 2019–2021 period potentially reflecting improvements in laboratory conditions and culture media optimization.

title: Ovarian stimulation with follitropin delta for in vitro fertilization: a multicentre, randomized, assessor-blind comparison with follitropin alfa using conventional dosing regimens (ADAPT-1 trial)
summary:
# Summary

The ADAPT-1 trial compared follitropin delta, a recombinant FSH with individualized dosing based on bodyweight and anti-Müllerian hormone levels, with conventional follitropin alfa for ovarian stimulation in assisted reproduction. Both medications produced equivalent ovarian responses, with mean oocyte retrieval of 9.9 in each group, comparable serum hormone profiles, and similar follicular development. Clinical outcomes were virtually identical between treatments, including fertilization rates, blastocyst development, implantation rates, and clinical pregnancy rates (31.6% vs. 31.0%), while safety profiles were comparable with low rates of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and minor adverse events such as headache and pelvic discomfort. Notably, follitropin delta required a lower mean total gonadotropin dose than follitropin alfa, demonstrating clinical equivalence while potentially offering improved dosing efficiency through individualization based on patient characteristics.

title: Impact of gonadotropin genetic profile and ovarian reserve on controlled ovarian stimulation: data from prospective cohort of the GENOCS trial
summary:
# Summary

The GENOCS trial investigated how gonadotropin receptor gene polymorphisms influence ovarian response during controlled ovarian stimulation in assisted reproductive technology. Five key genetic variants—FSHR p.N680S, FSHR c.-29G>A, FSHB c.-211G>T, LHCGR p.S312N, and LHβ V-LH p.W8R—were significantly associated with stimulation duration, with N alleles and G alleles generally linked to prolonged treatment. While individual SNPs modulated follicle-to-oocyte ratios and mature oocyte yield, their haplotype combinations showed substantial influence on follicular output rate and stimulation requirements, explaining variability in ovarian response among women with similar baseline ovarian reserves. Notably, none of the genetic variants significantly predicted total oocyte retrieval, embryo development, implantation rates, or pregnancy outcomes, suggesting that genetic polymorphisms primarily affect the kinetics and efficiency of ovarian stimulation rather than ultimate clinical success. These findings underscore that individual genetic backgrounds substantially contribute to ovarian response variability, though additional factors beyond these SNPs determine final reproductive outcomes.

title: Clinical pregnancy rates after two different methods of laser-assisted hatching applied to vitrified-warmed day-3 embryos or day-5 blastocysts
summary:
# Summary

Cryopreservation of embryos hardens the zona pellucida (ZP), a thick protein layer surrounding embryos, which impairs embryo hatching and subsequent implantation. Laser-assisted hatching (LAH) addresses this problem through two main approaches: thinning the ZP or creating a breach through it. The study compared these two LAH methods in vitrified-warmed day-3 embryos and day-5 blastocysts, finding comparable clinical pregnancy rates, implantation rates, and miscarriage rates between the breaching-only method and the combined thinning-and-breaching approach. Notably, thinning did not provide additional benefit when breaching was already performed, suggesting that breaching alone is sufficient to facilitate successful hatching and implantation. The mechanism involves embryo-secreted proteases that naturally thin the ZP during culture, and LAH artificially enhances this process; however, creating too narrow an opening in the ZP carries a slight risk of monozygotic twinning due to blastomere entrapment. These findings support breaching as an effective and simpler LAH strategy for improving outcomes with cryopreserved embryos.

title: Comparison of clinical pregnancy rates between hormone replacement therapy and modified natural cycle for endometrial preparation in frozen embryo transfer cycles: An RCT
summary: This randomized controlled trial compared two endometrial preparation protocols for frozen embryo transfer: hormone replacement cycles (HRC) using exogenous estrogen and progesterone, and modified natural cycles (mNC-FET) using the patient's endogenous hormonal production. Successful embryo implantation requires three critical components—a viable embryo, a receptive endometrium, and optimal transfer technique—with endometrial preparation method significantly influencing overall IVF success. In HRC, exogenous estrogen stimulates endometrial development while suppressing follicular growth, whereas mNC-FET relies on HCG-triggered ovulation and corpus luteum-derived progesterone for luteal phase support, requiring fewer monitoring visits. The mNC-FET group demonstrated a higher clinical pregnancy rate (37.5% versus 28.6%) with lower miscarriage rates (4.8% versus 11.1%), though blastocyst-stage embryos and increased endometrial thickness were independently associated with improved pregnancy outcomes across both groups. These findings suggest that modified natural cycles may offer a physiologically-based alternative to exogenous hormone protocols for endometrial preparation in frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: The role of tacrolimus in women with unexplained infertility: A narrative review
summary: This narrative review examines tacrolimus, a calcineurin inhibitor, as a potential therapeutic agent for women with unexplained infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and recurrent implantation failure. Tacrolimus works through the calcineurin-nuclear factor of activated T cells pathway to suppress excessive immune responses by inhibiting natural killer cells, natural killer T cells, and macrophages, while simultaneously converting mature dendritic cells to tolerogenic phenotypes that promote immune tolerance. The drug effectively modulates the Th1/Th2 cytokine balance by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-4, interferon gamma, IL-33/ST2) while upregulating anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10, leukocyte inhibitory factor), with higher IL-10 levels correlating positively with implantation rates. Additionally, tacrolimus normalizes progesterone receptor expression and stimulates extravillous trophoblast cells, thereby improving placentation and endometrial receptivity. Clinical evidence demonstrates that tacrolimus alone achieves live birth rates of 70% in women with elevated Th1/Th2 ratios, and when combined with low molecular weight heparin, significantly improves both live birth and clinical pregnancy rates while reducing spontaneous miscarriage risk. These findings suggest tacrolimus addresses immune-mediated reproductive failure by restoring endometrial immune homeostasis and enhancing placentation mechanisms.

title: Comparison of the efficacy of single vs. double autologous platelet-rich plasma intrauterine infusion on endometrial receptivity in thin endometrium patients: a prospective randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Thin endometrium is a significant clinical challenge associated with poor reproductive outcomes, including recurrent implantation failure and cycle cancellation, with pregnancy rates dropping to approximately 29% when endometrial thickness is ≤6 mm. This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that double intrauterine platelet-rich plasma (PRP) infusion substantially outperforms single infusion in treating thin endometrium, increasing endometrial thickness by 2.19 mm compared to 1.74 mm and improving hemodynamic parameters including resistance index and pulsatility index. Double PRP infusion resulted in superior clinical outcomes, with a 48.9% clinical pregnancy rate and 10% cycle cancellation rate versus 27% and 26% respectively in the single infusion group. The therapeutic mechanism relies on PRP's rich content of growth factors—particularly platelet-derived growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor, and insulin-like growth factor 1—which promote endometrial cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and antifibrosis, thereby enhancing endometrial receptivity. These findings suggest that repeated PRP administration offers a promising therapeutic approach for patients with thin endometrium who have failed conventional treatments such as increased estrogen dosing, sildenafil, or granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

title: Intraovarian platelet-rich plasma (PRP) infusion appeared to benefit low prognosis IVF patients; however, when compared to controls, no significant benefit could be confirmed
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Intraovarian platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection stimulates tissue growth and repair while promoting endometrial proliferation, and was evaluated as a potential treatment for patients with diminished ovarian reserve or primary ovarian insufficiency undergoing IVF. Within the PRP-treated group, intraovarian injection activated follicles and significantly increased euploid embryo yield, transferable embryo yield, and blastocyst yield, with particularly striking fivefold improvements in patients with anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels ≥1. However, when the PRP group was directly compared to untreated controls, no significant differences emerged in euploid yield, transferable embryo yield, or blastocyst production, suggesting that regression to the mean—natural improvement observed in repeat IVF cycles—likely explains the apparent benefits rather than a true PRP effect. Notably, patients with AMH <1 showed no improvement in embryo outcomes following PRP treatment, indicating that ovarian reserve status may determine treatment responsiveness. These findings suggest that while intraovarian PRP shows promise in low-prognosis IVF patients, the lack of control group differentiation limits clinical confidence in its efficacy as a standalone reproductive intervention.

title: Every second counts: the association of embryo transfer duration with live birth following 2267 single, euploid, frozen embryo transfer
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study of 2,267 single euploid frozen embryo transfers demonstrates that embryo transfer duration significantly impacts live birth outcomes, with the longest transfer times (4th quartile, 55–1028 seconds) substantially reducing pregnancy success compared to the fastest transfers. While embryo transfer duration did not affect biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, or miscarriage rates, the mean transfer time in cycles resulting in live birth was notably shorter (53.9 seconds) compared to unsuccessful cycles (59.9 seconds), and transfers exceeding 60 seconds showed reduced pregnancy rates. Longer transfer times were associated with anteverted uterine position, absence of ultrasound flash, and first transfer cycles, suggesting that mechanical factors such as prolonged embryo exposure to suboptimal conditions, transfer-related stress, and heightened uterine contractions may compromise implantation potential. The findings support optimizing embryo transfer technique through established practices including abdominal ultrasound guidance, soft catheters, proper catheter placement in the upper-middle uterine cavity, and swift, smooth transfer procedures to maximize reproductive success in frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: The Association Between Early Progesterone Rise and Serum Estradiol Levels as Well as Endometrial Thickness in IVF Cycles
summary:
# Summary
