This IVF study reveals a critical paradox in hormonal regulation: while estradiol on the hCG trigger day positively correlates with oocyte retrieval, maturation, and fertilization outcomes, elevated progesterone (≥2 ng/mL) on oocyte retrieval day impairs both fertilization and pregnancy rates despite promoting endometrial secretory transformation. Elevated progesterone induces premature endometrial maturation, creating histologically advanced but functionally unreceptive endometrium that closes the implantation window prematurely, an effect independent of oocyte quality. The optimal hormonal profile for successful pregnancy involves lower progesterone, higher estradiol and anti-müllerian hormone (AMH), lower BMI, and younger age—factors that collectively support proper endometrial thickness (≥7 mm) and receptivity. These findings underscore that successful IVF outcomes depend critically on maintaining appropriate E2/P4 balance during controlled ovarian stimulation to ensure both quality oocytes and receptive endometrium. Smoking and elevated BMI further compromise this balance by disrupting endometrial receptivity, while hormonal status remains the primary determinant of implantation success rather than pregnancy maintenance.

title: Administration of levofloxacin combined with metronidazole suppositories prior to in vitro fertilisation in women with chronic endometritis: a protocol of a single-centre, randomised, controlled clinical trial
summary: Chronic endometritis, characterized by disruption of endometrial microbial balance and associated with pathogens such as Chlamydia trachomatis, Streptococcus species, and Escherichia coli, represents a significant contributor to infertility, repeated implantation failure, and recurrent miscarriage in women undergoing assisted reproduction. This randomized controlled trial evaluates a novel treatment approach combining oral levofloxacin with metronidazole suppositories administered prior to in vitro fertilization, hypothesizing that this regimen will improve clinical pregnancy and live birth rates compared to standard antibiotic protocols. The suppository formulation offers distinct advantages over oral antibiotics by achieving high local drug concentrations in the endometrium through the anatomical continuity between the vagina and uterine cavity, while simultaneously reducing systemic side effects and gastrointestinal complications such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea that commonly limit treatment compliance. Standardized antibiotic treatment for chronic endometritis has demonstrated the capacity to enhance both clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates while reducing abortion risk, supporting the therapeutic rationale for this intervention. By combining the broad-spectrum coverage of levofloxacin and metronidazole with improved bioavailability and tolerability through vaginal administration, this protocol aims to optimize treatment outcomes in women with chronic endometritis undergoing fertility treatment.

title: Effectiveness and safety of follitropin delta in routine clinical practice in the Nordics and Switzerland (the NORSOS study): a prospective non-interventional study
summary:
# Summary

The NORSOS study evaluated follitropin delta, a recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone produced using a human cell line with a unique glycosylation profile, as a treatment for infertility in women undergoing in vitro fertilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection across Nordic countries and Switzerland. Follitropin delta dosing is individualized based on body weight and anti-Müllerian hormone levels using an algorithmic approach, which reduces extreme ovarian responses and the incidence of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome compared to conventional dosing. In this real-world prospective cohort, 47.9% of participants achieved the target response of 8–14 oocytes retrieved, with an overall ongoing pregnancy rate of 41.2%, demonstrating comparable efficacy to other FSH preparations while maintaining a favorable safety profile with only 4.0% reporting mild ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome cases. Patient satisfaction was high, with 98.4% of women satisfied with the injection pen delivery system. Age and anti-Müllerian hormone concentration significantly influenced oocyte retrieval outcomes, with younger women and those with higher hormone levels achieving higher oocyte numbers and pregnancy rates. The study confirms that individualized follitropin delta dosing achieves adequate ovarian responses in routine clinical practice with low immunogenicity potential and minimal adverse events including only minor headaches and pelvic discomfort.

title: Study on the relationship between endometrial thickness on the hCG day in the progestin-primed ovarian stimulation protocol and pregnancy outcomes in frozen–thawed embryo transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how endometrial thickness (EMT) on the human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) trigger day in progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) cycles predicts pregnancy outcomes in frozen-thawed embryo transfer. The researchers found that EMT ≥8 mm on the hCG day was significantly associated with better pregnancy outcomes, particularly higher clinical and ongoing pregnancy rates in blastocyst transfers, whereas thin endometrium (<8 mm) impaired embryo implantation and development. The PPOS protocol, which uses supraphysiological progestin doses during the follicular phase to prevent premature LH surges, paradoxically reduces endometrial receptivity while maintaining partial endometrial growth capacity through responsiveness to endogenous estrogen. For patients with thin endometrium in PPOS cycles, natural cycle endometrial preparation yielded superior results, whereas GnRH-agonist plus hormone replacement therapy improved outcomes primarily in patients with adequate EMT (≥8 mm). These findings suggest that EMT on the hCG day reflects inherent endometrial estrogen sensitivity and serves as a valuable predictor of implantation success, with the endometrial response varying considerably among individuals based on their intrinsic hormonal responsiveness.

title: A multicentric real-world observational study to describe the use and efficacy of follitropin delta for IVF/ICSI procedures in patients at risk of hypo-response
summary: This real-world observational study evaluated follitropin delta, a recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone derived from human cell lines, for ovarian stimulation in patients at risk of hypo-response to assisted reproductive technology (ART). The study focused on 35 patients with poor ovarian reserve, classified as POSEIDON groups 3 and 4, who had very low anti-Müllerian hormone levels (mean 0.7 ng/ml) and were dosed according to an individualized algorithm based on body weight and serum AMH levels. Follitropin delta successfully achieved the target ovarian response of 8–14 oocytes in 28.1% of cases, with a mean of 6.54 oocytes retrieved among those following the algorithm, and demonstrated superior outcomes compared to conventionally-dosed follitropin alfa and beta. The treatment was associated with a 20% ongoing pregnancy rate and 28.6% clinical pregnancy rate per started cycle, with all ongoing pregnancies occurring in patients who adhered to the dosing algorithm, while simultaneously reducing the need for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome prevention measures. No adverse drug reactions were reported, confirming the safety profile of follitropin delta in this challenging patient population. These findings suggest that individualized dosing of follitropin delta is particularly suitable for poor responders and those with diminished ovarian reserve, offering a promising approach to improve reproductive outcomes in ART.

title: Effect of the Peiyu granules on early miscarriage among women undergoing embryo transfer: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated Peiyu granules, a Chinese herbal medicine formulation, in women undergoing embryo transfer for assisted reproduction. Peiyu granules reduced early miscarriage rates from 22.4% to 12.8% by upregulating luteinizing hormone and progesterone while enhancing trophoblast cell invasion and proliferation to improve placental function. Among women who achieved clinical pregnancy, live birth rates were substantially higher in the Peiyu group (84.2% versus 73.7%), demonstrating that the intervention prevents pregnancy loss rather than improving initial conception rates. The herbal formula contains components with hormone-like properties: flavonoids from Semen Cuscutae and Herba Taxilli provide estrogen- and progesterone-like effects, while other constituents support endocrine and reproductive function. These findings suggest that Peiyu granules address endometrial dysfunction and early pregnancy complications by enhancing placentation and endocrine support, thereby protecting against spontaneous abortion in infertile women undergoing embryo transfer.

title: Elevated serum estradiol levels on embryo transfer day predict higher pregnancy success in HRT-FET cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that serum estradiol levels above 201 pg/mL on embryo transfer day are independently associated with significantly improved pregnancy outcomes in hormonally prepared frozen-thawed embryo transfer (HRT-FET) cycles, with clinical pregnancy rates of 62.6% versus 2.6% in patients below this threshold. Estrogen exerts its beneficial effects through multiple mechanisms: it promotes essential endometrial proliferation and glandular secretion via upregulation of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and establishes immune tolerance by increasing regulatory T cells while reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α and IFN-γ. Conversely, suboptimal estradiol levels below 201 pg/mL predispose to insufficient decidual transformation and excessive inflammation, resulting in markedly higher early pregnancy loss rates (87.5% versus 7.25%). Using decision tree analysis, the authors identified 201 pg/mL as a clinically relevant threshold, achieved 77.1% classification accuracy in predicting clinical pregnancy outcomes, and found this transfer-day measurement more predictive than estradiol levels measured on the first day of progesterone administration. These findings underscore that estradiol supplementation achieving adequate serum concentrations at the time of embryo transfer is crucial for optimizing endometrial receptivity and pregnancy success in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Day 6 vitrified blastocyst transfer on day 5 vs. day 6 of progesterone shows similar outcomes in HRT-FET cycles: a cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study of 746 hormone replacement therapy frozen embryo transfer (HRT-FET) cycles investigated whether transferring day 6 (D6) vitrified blastocysts on day 5 versus day 6 of progesterone exposure affects reproductive outcomes. The analysis found no significant differences in live birth rates, biochemical pregnancy rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or early pregnancy loss rates between the two transfer timing groups, with live birth rates of 23.5% and 21.9% respectively. Good-quality blastocysts emerged as the strongest positive predictor of success across all outcomes, while maternal age negatively predicted live birth rates, and transfer timing appeared less critical when embryo quality was high. Although some previous studies suggested advantages to transferring D6 blastocysts on day 5 of progesterone, this cohort demonstrates comparable outcomes between the two approaches in HRT-FET cycles, suggesting that endometrial thickness and embryo quality may be more important determinants of success than the specific timing of progesterone exposure.

title: Utility of GnRH Agonists before embryo transfer in women with
summary: Adenomyosis significantly impairs reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction, reducing clinical pregnancy rates by 28% and live birth rates by 41%, with mechanisms involving elevated estrogen receptors and estradiol-driven adenomyotic cell proliferation that causes endometrial gland invasion and myometrial changes. GnRH agonist pretreatment protocols show differential efficacy depending on duration: long GnRHa protocols substantially improve implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to ultra-long protocols or antagonist protocols, though the evidence base remains limited to few comparative studies. Ultra-long GnRHa protocols paradoxically reduce implantation and reproductive outcomes despite their theoretical benefits, while antagonist protocols demonstrate improved live birth rates (OR 2.59) compared to long GnRHa in some analyses. The location and extent of adenomyosis affect prognosis, with inner myometrial involvement causing a threefold increased miscarriage risk and diffuse adenomyosis reducing clinical pregnancy and live birth rates, whereas outer myometrial-only disease associates with better ongoing pregnancy rates. GnRHa treatment reduces uterine volume and pain symptoms, indicating adenomyosis severity, and pituitary downregulation before controlled ovarian stimulation appears beneficial for reproductive outcomes. Coexisting endometriosis is significantly more prevalent in women receiving ultra-long GnRHa protocols, suggesting complex disease phenotypes requiring individualized treatment approaches.

title: Does flying after embryo transfer impact
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated whether air travel immediately following embryo transfer affects IVF outcomes in patients undergoing fertility treatment, including those with endometriosis-related infertility. Comparing patients who traveled by air after embryo transfer to those who remained sedentary, the researchers found no significant differences in pregnancy rates (61.99% vs. 62.14%), implantation rates (34.58% vs. 38.65%), or miscarriage rates (28.51% vs. 32.50%) between groups. The study examined potential physiological stressors associated with flying, including cabin pressure changes, decreased humidity, and radiation exposure, and determined these factors posed no measurable risk to embryo implantation or early pregnancy viability. These findings support the safety of medical tourism for fertility treatment and allow patients to return home via air travel without compromising reproductive outcomes. The results suggest that embryo transfer success depends more fundamentally on embryo quality and uterine lining receptivity than on postoperative activity restrictions.

title: Impact of unilateral and bilateral salpingectomy on ovarian
summary:
# Summary

Hydrosalpinx significantly impairs reproductive outcomes by reducing embryo implantation rates, increasing miscarriage and ectopic pregnancy rates, and compromising endometrial receptivity through toxic fluid accumulation in the uterine cavity. Prophylactic laparoscopic salpingectomy effectively improves implantation, pregnancy, and ongoing pregnancy rates in patients with hydrosalpinx. Despite theoretical concerns about vascular injury during salpingectomy, prospective studies demonstrate that both unilateral and bilateral salpingectomy do not adversely affect ovarian reserve parameters including anti-Müllerian hormone levels, antral follicle counts, FSH, or estradiol levels. The anatomical proximity of the ovarian artery to the medial tubal artery creates surgical risk, with mesosalpinx vascular damage potentially disrupting ovarian blood supply and follicular development; however, careful surgical technique such as proximal tubal ligation or distal salpingostomy minimizes this risk. Age-specific considerations warrant attention, as salpingectomy in women aged 35-39 years may decrease antral follicle counts, and bilateral salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy could potentially shorten reproductive lifespan.

title: Comparison of efficacy, safety, and economy of recombinant and urinary follicle-stimulating hormone in women with a predicted normal response undergoing assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This study compared recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone (rFSH) and urinary FSH (uFSH) for controlled ovarian stimulation in women with predicted normal ovarian response undergoing assisted reproductive technology. Although both agents effectively stimulated multi-follicular development, rFSH demonstrated superior laboratory outcomes, producing significantly higher numbers of mature follicles, retrieved oocytes, and transferable embryos, along with elevated estradiol and progesterone levels on trigger day. However, rFSH required higher total doses, with uFSH patients needing increased starting doses and longer stimulation duration, reflecting the batch-to-batch variability inherent in urinary-derived hormones. Despite these laboratory differences, clinical outcomes were comparable between groups, with no significant differences in live birth rates, clinical pregnancy rates, cumulative delivery rates, or moderate-to-severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome incidence. The primary distinction was economic: rFSH substantially increased treatment costs compared to uFSH, suggesting that for women with normal ovarian reserve, the superior laboratory outcomes of rFSH do not translate into clinically meaningful reproductive advantages.

title: GnRH antagonist protocol versus long-acting GnRH agonist protocol on pregnancy outcomes in IVF/ICSI: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study compared two major controlled ovarian stimulation protocols used in IVF/ICSI treatment to evaluate their effects on pregnancy outcomes, particularly in patients with endometriosis and adenomyosis. The GnRH antagonist protocol offers practical advantages including shorter stimulation duration, reduced ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk, and milder ovarian stimulation with less treatment complexity, making it widely adopted in clinical practice. In contrast, the long-acting GnRH agonist protocol produces superior oocyte retrieval outcomes, generates more available and good-quality embryos, and improves endometrial receptivity through reduced LH fluctuation and lower preovulatory LH instability, leading to higher live birth rates. The GnRH antagonist protocol's association with lower LH levels during the mid-follicular phase impairs oocyte development, whereas the long-acting agonist maintains more stable hormonal conditions beneficial for embryo quality. After adjusting for confounding factors, both protocols showed similar biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, and miscarriage rates, suggesting that protocol selection should be individualized based on patient age, ovarian reserve, and specific conditions such as adenomyosis or advanced-stage endometriosis. Frozen embryo transfer following long-acting GnRH agonist pretreatment demonstrates superior outcomes compared to fresh transfer protocols.

title: Number of top-quality embryos transferred has no advantage in women over the age of 40 in FET cycles
summary: This study demonstrates that while the number of high-quality embryos transferred is generally an important predictor of clinical pregnancy and live birth rates in frozen embryo transfer cycles, this benefit is substantially diminished in women over 40 years of age. In younger women (≤30, 31-35, and 36-40 years), increasing the number of top-quality embryos significantly improved both clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates, but women over 40 showed no significant pregnancy outcome improvement with additional high-quality embryos. The decline in embryo quality and reproductive success with advancing maternal age is attributed to multiple factors: increasing rates of embryonic aneuploidy, decreased uterine receptivity, and reduced correlation between embryo morphology and euploidy status in older women. Key factors associated with better FET outcomes across all ages include adequate endometrial thickness, primary infertility diagnosis, fewer prior treatment attempts, and transfer of blastocyst-stage embryos with intact post-thaw morphology. These findings suggest that for women over 40, the limiting factor in achieving pregnancy is not embryo quantity but rather age-related oocyte and endometrial dysfunction, indicating that treatment strategies for this population should focus on improving egg quality rather than increasing embryo numbers.

title: Intrauterine infusion of oil-based medium in women with recurrent implantation failure before frozen embryo transfer: a randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine infusion of oil-based contrast medium, specifically ethiodized poppy seed oil, improves fertility outcomes in women with recurrent implantation failure undergoing frozen embryo transfer by modulating endometrial immunobiological properties. The oil-based medium exerts immunomodulatory effects through inhibition of inflammatory markers (CD138, CD38, and NF-κB), regulation of the TH1/TH2 axis, and modulation of dendritic cells and regulatory T cells, while simultaneously increasing endometrial thickness. In this randomized clinical trial, the oil-based medium group demonstrated significantly higher clinical pregnancy rates (56% vs. 37.4%) and implantation rates compared to controls, though differences in live birth and ongoing pregnancy rates were not statistically significant. Notably, women aged 35–39 years and those undergoing gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist hormone replacement therapy (GnRHa-HRT) showed enhanced clinical pregnancy rates with oil-based medium infusion, with GnRHa-HRT protocol further improving endometrial thickness and multiple reproductive outcomes. The treatment was well-tolerated with no adverse reactions reported, and some patients even achieved spontaneous pregnancy within weeks following infusion. These findings suggest oil-based medium infusion represents an effective and safe therapeutic approach for improving fertility outcomes in recurrent implantation failure, particularly when combined with appropriate endometrial preparation protocols.

title: Intrauterine application of Budesonide-hyaluronic acid gel in patients with recurrent implantation failure and total loss of junctional zone differentiation on magnetic resonance imaging
summary: This pilot study demonstrates that intrauterine application of budesonide-enriched hyaluronic acid (HA) gel following hysteroscopic sub-endometrial exploration significantly improves reproductive outcomes in women with recurrent implantation failure (RIF) and loss of junctional zone (JZ) differentiation on MRI. The junctional zone is critical for regulating uterine peristalsis, implantation, and placentation, and its abnormalities—particularly loss of differentiation—are associated with inflammatory states and implantation failure. The combination treatment leverages two synergistic mechanisms: hyaluronic acid promotes endometrial regeneration, reduces adhesions, and enhances endometrial-myometrial receptivity, while budesonide exerts potent anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects by suppressing natural killer cell cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion while promoting trophoblast proliferation and invasion. Among the 20 women treated, 18 (90%) achieved clinical pregnancy, with 17 resulting in live births, suggesting the approach effectively restores uterine receptivity in this difficult-to-treat population. The study indicates this targeted hysteroscopic delivery of regenerative and anti-inflammatory agents may also have potential applications in other inflammatory uterine conditions such as chronic endometritis and endometriosis.

title: Impact of euploid blastocyst developmental stage and morphological grading on pregnancy outcomes in young recurrent pregnancy loss patients: association with parental chromosomal status
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that blastocyst developmental stage and morphological quality significantly impact pregnancy outcomes in young recurrent pregnancy loss patients, with effects varying by parental chromosomal status. Among balanced chromosomal translocation (BCT) carriers, day 5 (D5) euploid blastocysts achieved substantially higher clinical pregnancy rates (83.33% vs. 62.86%) and live birth rates (75.00% vs. 51.43%) compared to day 6 (D6) blastocysts, suggesting that developmental timing is critical for this population. In contrast, normokaryotypic RPL patients showed minimal differences between D5 and D6 blastocysts, indicating that morphological grading—particularly good-quality blastocysts—was the dominant predictor of success in this group. Advanced maternal age associates with reduced embryonic euploidy and metabolic dysregulation, while BCT carriers exhibited markedly reduced euploidy rates (26%) compared to normokaryotypic individuals (40%), highlighting chromosomal status as a key modulator of embryo viability. These findings underscore that optimizing assisted reproductive outcomes requires tailoring embryo selection strategies to parental karyotype, as developmental stage and morphology exert differential effects depending on chromosomal status.

title: Impact of Transfer Timing on Day-6 Blastocyst Pregnancy Outcomes in HRT Cycles of 2021–2024
summary: This study examines how transfer timing of Day 6 blastocysts in hormone replacement therapy (HRT) frozen embryo transfer cycles affects pregnancy outcomes, addressing the clinical challenge that Day 6 blastocysts typically show lower pregnancy rates than Day 5 blastocysts due to their slower developmental speed and increased vulnerability to DNA damage from extended in vitro culture. The research demonstrates that transferring Day 6 blastocysts on day 6 of progesterone administration achieves a clinical pregnancy rate of 45.80%, compared to 42.44% when transferred on day 7, though this difference was not independently significant—suggesting that timing optimization provides modest benefits. Critical to success is synchronization between embryonic development and endometrial receptivity, which can be enhanced through prolonged progesterone-induced endometrial transformation to reduce uterine contractions and improve endometrial receptivity. The study identifies several factors negatively associated with clinical pregnancy: advancing female and male age, secondary infertility status, and increased number of transfer cycles, while AMH and antral follicle count positively correlate with pregnancy success. The findings suggest that while Day 6 blastocyst transfer in HRT cycles yields reasonable pregnancy rates, the inherent developmental disadvantage of slower-developing embryos—their association with aneuploidy and reduced implantation potential—remains a limiting factor that cannot be fully overcome by timing adjustments alone.

title: Is live birth rate decline from frozen cycles performed during Chinese new year holiday season: A single center retrospective cohort
summary: This retrospective cohort study examined whether the Chinese New Year holiday affects live birth rates in assisted reproductive technology cycles, comparing frozen embryo transfers (FET) during the holiday period with non-holiday cycles. Contrary to previous findings showing adverse effects on fresh embryo transfers, the study found no significant difference in live birth outcomes between frozen cycles performed during Chinese New Year and non-holiday periods, with rates of approximately 39-40% in both groups. The protective effect of FET during this stressful period may result from the reduced psychological vulnerability of frozen cycles compared to fresh cycles, as frozen transfers involve shorter stimulation phases and endometrium preparation that are less sensitive to psychosocial stress factors. Multiple clinical factors influenced outcomes independently, including advanced maternal and paternal age, elevated FSH levels, and lower AMH levels all decreasing success rates, while GnRH antagonist and follicular phase long-acting GnRH agonist protocols improved outcomes. Notably, the study observed seasonal variations in oocyte retrieval patterns and embryo transfer timing, with February transfers showing higher live birth rates (51.6%) compared to March (40.8%), though holiday timing did not significantly impact FET success. These findings suggest that frozen embryo transfer programs provide a more resilient approach to reproductive timing during culturally significant stress periods, with outcomes determined primarily by patient characteristics and protocol selection rather than sociocultural factors.

title: Autologous platelet-rich plasma ovarian injection was associated with increased oocyte acquisition in patients with poor ovarian response: a retrospective propensity-matched study
summary: This retrospective propensity-matched study demonstrates that autologous platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection into the ovaries significantly improves oocyte retrieval in patients with poor ovarian response, a clinically important finding for assisted reproductive technology. Intra-ovarian PRP administration increased the number of follicles ≥14 mm, retrieved oocytes, and normally fertilized zygotes compared to controls, while reducing cycles without transferable embryos. The therapeutic mechanism involves multiple growth factors present in PRP—including platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β), and sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P)—which promote tissue regeneration, angiogenesis, and protection of ovarian cells from oxidative stress. Additionally, PRP-derived cytokines facilitate critical reproductive processes including follicular maturation, ovulation, and luteinization, while factors like growth differentiation factor 9 (GDF-9) support oocyte maturation and prevent premature ovarian failure. Although embryo development and clinical pregnancy outcomes showed trends favoring the PRP group, these differences did not reach statistical significance, suggesting that PRP's primary benefit lies in improving ovarian response and oocyte quantity in poor responders. The study identifies anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) and gonadotropin dosage as independent predictors of oocyte retrieval success alongside PRP treatment.

title: Time to pregnancy in young women with high response to controlled ovarian stimulation undergoing fresh transfer vs. a freeze-all strategy after in vitro fertilization treatment—a nationwide register-based cohort study
summary: This nationwide register-based cohort study compared fresh embryo transfer versus freeze-all strategy in young women (≤37 years) with high ovarian response (≥15 oocytes retrieved) undergoing IVF treatment. Although fresh embryo transfer achieved pregnancy approximately 6–8 weeks sooner than freeze-all strategy, the freeze-all approach yielded superior reproductive outcomes, including higher clinical pregnancy rates (66.2% vs. 59%), live birth rates (56% vs. 51.1%), and cumulative live birth rates (77.1% vs. 73.5%), while reducing miscarriage rates and moderate-to-severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS; 4.75% vs. 0.9%). The study identified that high oocyte retrieval associated with supraphysiological estradiol levels, premature progesterone elevation, increased immature oocytes, and prolonged stimulation protocols compromised endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation potential in fresh cycles. Freeze-all strategy mitigated these detrimental effects of high ovarian response by allowing endometrial recovery and reducing OHSS risk, making it the preferred approach despite extended time to pregnancy. The findings suggest that women with high ovarian response benefit from cryopreservation despite the psychological burden of delayed conception.

title: Progesterone Vaginal Gel or Combined Medication for Luteal-Phase Support of Frozen-Thawed Embryo Transfer Cycles: A Single-Centre, Chinese, Randomized, Open-Label, Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

Progesterone plays a critical role in embryo-endometrial synchrony, endometrial receptivity, and implantation during frozen embryo transfer cycles, which lack corpus luteum-derived progesterone production. This randomized pilot study compared vaginal progesterone (VPG) monotherapy versus combined vaginal and oral progesterone (VPG + OPG) for luteal-phase support, finding that VPG 90 mg plus OPG 20 mg daily improved the ongoing pregnancy rate (29.1% versus 18.8%) and showed numerical advantages in implantation and clinical pregnancy rates compared to VPG alone. Higher total progesterone doses were associated with better pregnancy outcomes, including reduced pregnancy loss and increased β-hCG positivity, suggesting that total progesterone dosage rather than route of administration is key to optimizing pregnancy success in frozen embryo transfer cycles. Both regimens had comparable safety profiles with minimal treatment-emergent adverse events. These findings support the use of combination progesterone therapy as an effective luteal-phase support strategy to enhance reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: Protocol-Specific Outcomes of GnRH Agonist Use in Luteal Phase Support During Frozen Embryo Transfer Cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated GnRH agonist administration during luteal phase support in frozen embryo transfer cycles and found that agonist use significantly increased implantation rates (61.6% vs 48.4%) compared to control, though live birth rates showed only a non-significant trend toward improvement. The mechanism involves GnRH agonist receptor activation in the endometrium and trophoblasts, which triggers signaling cascades affecting trophoblast invasion and enhances endogenous LH levels, corpus luteum support, and progesterone output, while elevated circulating steroids facilitate implantation and hCG secretion. However, when stratified by transfer protocol, GnRH agonist addition provided no significant benefit in either medicated or ovulation induction cycles, with similar pregnancy and live birth rates across treatment groups regardless of agonist use. Embryo quality emerged as the only significant predictor of pregnancy outcomes, and miscarriage rates remained comparable between groups. These findings suggest that while GnRH agonists theoretically enhance implantation through multiple biological pathways, the clinical benefit may be limited depending on the specific FET protocol employed.

title: Effect of a luteal phase rescue protocol on live birth rates in frozen embryo transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated a luteal phase rescue protocol for frozen embryo transfer cycles, focusing on optimizing progesterone supplementation to improve reproductive outcomes. The research compared patients with low serum progesterone levels (<11 ng/ml) on Day 4 of endometrial preparation who received additional subcutaneous progesterone supplementation (rescue group) versus standard hormone replacement therapy alone (control group). The rescue group achieved significantly higher live birth rates (36.9% versus 24.7%), demonstrating that subcutaneous progesterone rapidly increases serum levels above critical thresholds (>10.6 ng/ml), which are essential for endometrial secretory transformation and receptivity. While both groups achieved comparable progesterone levels by Day 12 after embryo transfer, the timing and adequacy of progesterone exposure proved crucial for implantation success. These findings highlight the importance of individualized luteal phase support strategies, particularly for patients with suboptimal initial progesterone levels, as combined administration routes optimize both systemic and local uterine progesterone exposure while reducing side effects compared to vaginal-only approaches.

title: Optimizing the preparation of thin endometria in frozen-thawed embryo transfer: a retrospective cohort analysis
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort analysis investigated optimal preparation strategies for thin endometria (≤7 mm) during frozen-thawed embryo transfer. Thin endometrium impairs endometrial tolerance and significantly reduces embryo implantation rates and pregnancy outcomes, with aging contributing through reduced endometrial blood flow, fewer hormone receptors, and decreased vascular endothelial growth factor expression. The study compared natural cycle (NC) and hormone replacement therapy (HRT) protocols, finding that HRT protocols more effectively stimulate endometrial growth through exogenous estrogen administration and promote endometrial epithelial cell proliferation, while NC protocols produce endogenous estrogens but require frequent monitoring and have higher cancellation rates. For patients with endometrial thickness of 6–7 mm on trigger day, the HRT protocol achieved significantly higher clinical and biochemical pregnancy rates compared to NC protocol. The authors recommend HRT protocols for patients with thin endometrium (EMT <7 mm) undergoing frozen-thawed embryo transfer, as this approach optimizes endometrial receptivity while accounting for age-related endometrial changes that compromise fertility outcomes.

title: GnRH agonist pretreatment in hormonal endometrial preparation: a comparison of two protocols for frozen embryo transfer outcomes
summary:
# Summary

GnRH agonist (GnRHa) pretreatment followed by hormone replacement therapy (GnRHa-HRT) significantly improves live birth rates in frozen embryo transfer cycles compared to conventional HRT, particularly benefiting patients with stage III-IV endometriosis undergoing single-embryo transfer. The mechanism involves GnRHa suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, reducing basal estrogen levels and preventing the negative feedback that impairs luteal function and endometrial development. GnRHa activation of GnRHa receptors on the endometrium downregulates the TGF-β/Smad3 pathway, reducing pathological extracellular matrix deposition and fibrous adhesion formation characteristic of endometriosis. Simultaneously, GnRHa enhances endometrial receptivity by promoting key transcription factors (LIF, MEIS1, HOXA10) and increasing integrin expression, pinopode formation, and ciliation, while modulating the intrauterine immune environment through downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-11) and NK cell cytotoxicity. These combined effects result in an endometrium with improved receptivity, higher implantation rates, and better pregnancy outcomes, though the benefit is less pronounced in double-embryo transfer cycles and diminishes with advanced maternal age.

title: Tubal occlusion in patients with hydrosalpinx modifies the uterine environment and improves in vitro fertilization outcomes within 7 months
summary:
# Summary

Hydrosalpinx severely impairs in vitro fertilization outcomes by promoting a pro-inflammatory endometrial environment, but laparoscopic tubal occlusion reverses this effect through immune remodeling of the uterine cavity. The study demonstrates that tubal occlusion suppresses multiple inflammatory pathways (including T-cell leukemia virus infection, natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and complement cascades) while upregulating CXCL14 expression in endometrial epithelial cells. CXCL14 orchestrates a shift toward M2 macrophage polarization, establishing a low-inflammatory microenvironment conducive to embryo implantation. Critically, oocyte retrieval must occur within seven months after occlusion to optimize pregnancy and live birth rates, as delayed retrieval beyond this window significantly reduces reproductive outcomes. Tubal occlusion achieves superior results compared to salpingectomy while preserving ovarian reserve, making it an effective less-invasive intervention for hydrosalpinx-associated infertility.

title: Pregnancy and obstetric-neonatal outcomes of patients with thin endometrium using three different endometrial preparation protocols in frozen embryo transfer cycles: a historical cohort of 2671 patients
summary:
# Summary

This large historical cohort study of 2,671 patients examined three endometrial preparation protocols for frozen embryo transfer in women with thin endometrium (≤8 mm). Thin endometrium was strongly associated with reduced clinical pregnancy rates, increased miscarriage risk, and worse obstetric-neonatal outcomes, though outcomes did not significantly differ among the three protocols when endometrial thickness fell below 7 mm. Natural cycle protocols demonstrated advantages over artificial cycles, including significantly lower biochemical pregnancy loss rates (3.9% versus 8.6%) and reduced overall obstetric-neonatal complications, while artificial cycles were linked to higher rates of hypertensive disorders and preeclampsia. The study highlights that endometrial thickness independently predicts reproductive success in frozen embryo transfer, but when thickness is severely compromised (<7 mm), the choice of endometrial preparation strategy becomes less critical than addressing the underlying endometrial insufficiency itself.

title: Success Rates of Assisted Reproduction in Couples with Poor Ovarian Response and Oligospermia
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates assisted reproduction outcomes in couples facing the combined challenge of poor ovarian response (POR) and oligozoospermia, conditions that substantially reduce fertility success. Oligozoospermia significantly impairs intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) fertilization and cleavage rates in a dose-dependent manner, with sperm concentration below 5 million/mL showing the most severe effects, and is strongly associated with increased chromosomal abnormalities in sperm. Concurrently, poor ovarian response—linked to advanced maternal age and diminished ovarian reserve—leads to fewer retrieved oocytes, higher cycle cancellation rates, and a 50% lower cumulative delivery rate compared to normal responders. The research demonstrates that frozen embryo transfer and blastocyst-stage transfers improve clinical pregnancy rates across all groups, while tailored management strategies including customized stimulation protocols, advanced laboratory methods, and psychological support enhance outcomes. Emerging therapies such as stem cell and mitochondrial interventions show promise as future treatment options for this challenging dual-factor infertility presentation.

title: Impact of Previous-day versus Same-day Warming of Cleavage-stage Embryos on Reproductive Outcomes: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined frozen embryo transfer outcomes in infertility treatment, comparing reproductive results when cryopreserved cleavage-stage embryos were warmed and cultured either same-day or the previous day before transfer. While extending culture of warmed cleavage-stage embryos to blastocyst stage (2 additional days) has been associated with improved pregnancy and perinatal outcomes in previous studies, this analysis found no significant clinical differences in live birth rates, clinical pregnancy rates, implantation rates, or miscarriage rates between day 3 and day 4 embryo transfers. However, day 3 embryo transfers demonstrated superior cryosurvival rates compared to day 4 transfers, though this did not translate to improved reproductive outcomes. The study suggests that the timing of warming and culture extension of cleavage-stage embryos does not meaningfully impact fertility success when using hormone replacement therapy for endometrial preparation, offering clinicians flexibility in embryo management protocols during frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: Impact of GnRH Agonist Pretreatment on Frozen-Thawed Embryo Transfer Outcomes of Overweight/Obese Women Undergoing Hormone Replacement Therapy
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that GnRH agonist (GnRH-a) pretreatment significantly improves frozen-thawed embryo transfer outcomes in overweight and obese women undergoing hormone replacement therapy. Overweight and obesity impair endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms, including disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, reduced ovarian response, and diminished embryo quality, all of which compromise pregnancy success. GnRH-a pretreatment enhances endometrial function and thickness, facilitating better endometrial-embryonic synchronization by suppressing endogenous hormonal fluctuations. Women receiving GnRH-a plus hormone replacement therapy achieved significantly higher rates of positive hCG tests (77.18% versus 68.65%), clinical pregnancy (68.09% versus 60.48%), implantation (52.41% versus 47.47%), and live birth (55.84% versus 49.35%) compared to hormone replacement therapy alone, with no difference in miscarriage rates between groups. The benefits of GnRH-a pretreatment were particularly pronounced in overweight women and those with dyslipidemia, suggesting that this protocol effectively counteracts the reproductive impairments associated with metabolic dysfunction.

title: Clinical Outcomes of In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) Versus Intrauterine Insemination (IUI) in Infertile Patients: Toward Evidence-Based Fertility Planning
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study demonstrates that in vitro fertilization (IVF) significantly outperforms intrauterine insemination (IUI) for treating infertility, with IVF achieving a 46% pregnancy rate and 40.7% live birth rate compared to IUI's 33.3% and 27.3% respectively. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) levels emerged as strong predictors of fertility outcomes, with higher AMH (>3.5 ng/mL) and lower FSH correlating with improved pregnancy success. Patient age and duration of infertility were critical determinants, with women under 30 years and those with shorter infertility histories showing substantially better outcomes in both treatment modalities. While IVF demonstrated superior efficacy for treating severe conditions including blocked fallopian tubes, male factor infertility, and unexplained infertility, it carried increased risks of multiple pregnancies (15.9%) and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (2.7%). The findings support personalized treatment planning based on hormonal profiles and patient characteristics to optimize fertility outcomes while considering the financial burden and procedural risks associated with IVF.

title: Effectiveness of Hysteroscopic Surgery for Intrauterine Lesions on Pregnancy Rates in Patients With Primary Infertility in Bahrain
summary:
# Summary

This Bahraini study demonstrates that hysteroscopic surgery significantly improves pregnancy rates and gestational outcomes in women with primary infertility caused by intrauterine lesions. Multiple anatomical abnormalities—including endometrial polyps, submucosal fibroids, uterine septa, intrauterine adhesions, and endometriosis—impair embryo implantation by distorting the uterine cavity and reducing endometrial receptivity. Polypectomy achieved the highest live birth rate (37.9%) and the most term pregnancies (47.8%), while myomectomy yielded the highest pregnancy rate (85.7%). Hysteroscopic procedures restored normal uterine anatomy and enhanced endometrial receptivity, with younger age and lower BMI associated with better outcomes. The findings support incorporating hysteroscopic evaluation and treatment into routine infertility workup to address correctable uterine cavity abnormalities that otherwise compromise fertility.

title: Elective single morula transfer achieved better clinical outcomes than two cleavage embryos transfer in fresh IVF-ET cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elective single morula transfer (eSMT) on Day 4 achieves comparable clinical pregnancy rates to Day 3 double cleavage-stage embryo transfer while substantially reducing adverse pregnancy outcomes. The eSMT approach significantly lowered preterm delivery rates (9.78% vs. 21.51%) and twin pregnancy rates, resulting in higher singleton live birth rates (58.97% vs. 43.59%). Morula-stage embryos represent a more developmentally advanced selection criterion, exhibiting better symmetric division patterns, lower chromosomal abnormality rates, and superior post-cryopreservation survival compared to cleavage-stage embryos. By transferring a single high-potential morula, eSMT maintains pregnancy efficacy while mitigating the maternal and neonatal risks associated with multiple pregnancies, including gestational hypertension, low birth weight, and preterm birth. These findings establish eSMT as an emerging clinical strategy in assisted reproductive technology that optimizes both maternal safety and perinatal outcomes.

title: Optimal management of large endometriomas (≥ 4 cm) before IVF: ethanol sclerotherapy, cystectomy, or no intervention?
summary: Endometriosis causes infertility through multiple mechanisms including anatomical pelvic alterations, impaired folliculogenesis, granulosa cell dysfunction, immune dysregulation, and reduced oocyte quality, while abnormal inflammation, local estrogen production, and progesterone resistance diminish endometrial receptivity. Large endometriomas (≥4 cm) further compromise IVF outcomes by reducing oocyte retrieval numbers, impairing oocyte quality, and decreasing implantation chances through chronic inflammatory effects on ovarian reserve. This study compared three management approaches before IVF: ethanol sclerotherapy (EST), laparoscopic cystectomy, and observation. Ethanol sclerotherapy demonstrated superior results, improving cumulative live birth rates (65% versus 30.8% with observation and 29% with cystectomy), oocyte yield, and embryo quality while preserving ovarian reserve and reducing cycle cancellation to 10%. In contrast, laparoscopic cystectomy significantly reduced anti-müllerian hormone levels and increased cycle cancellation rates to 35.5%, resulting in fewer retrieved oocytes and lower embryo numbers despite higher fresh embryo transfer pregnancy rates when cycles proceeded. The findings support ethanol sclerotherapy as the optimal pre-IVF management strategy for large endometriomas, as it effectively treats the cysts while minimizing harm to ovarian reserve and improving reproductive outcomes.

title: Cesarean section history affects the outcomes of frozen embryo transfer in IVF/ICSI: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study demonstrates that prior cesarean section (CS) significantly compromises frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes in assisted reproductive technology, with cesarean scar defects (CSD) serving as a key mechanistic pathway. Patients with previous CS history showed substantially reduced biochemical pregnancy rates (42.3% vs. 52.4%), clinical pregnancy rates (36.6% vs. 47.4%), implantation rates, and live birth rates (27.32% vs. 36.85%) compared to those with vaginal delivery history. The adverse effects stem from multiple mechanisms: CS creates myometrial defects and disrupts normal endometrial contractile waves, while CSDs trigger local inflammation, leukopenia, decreased angiogenesis, and delayed endometrial maturation that collectively impair endometrial receptivity. Additionally, accumulation of mucus and blood at the scar site directly interferes with embryo transfer and implantation processes. Notably, CSD presence also increased premature birth rates (30.8% vs. 11.3%) and perinatal complications in resulting pregnancies, though CSD size did not significantly influence live birth or clinical pregnancy rates. These findings underscore that cesarean section history has lasting detrimental effects on subsequent IVF/ICSI-FET success through scar-related endometrial dysfunction and mechanical interference.

title: Analysis of factors associated with IVF/ICSI clinical outcomes in advanced maternal age patients with normal ovarian reserve under the POSEIDON criteria
summary:
# Summary

This study examined IVF/ICSI outcomes in advanced maternal age patients (≥35 years) with normal ovarian reserve using POSEIDON criteria, identifying maternal age and endometrial thickness as critical prognostic factors. Patients under 38 years demonstrated significantly higher rates of hCG positivity (55.8% vs. 34.2%), embryo implantation (35.2% vs. 19.2%), clinical pregnancy (47.8% vs. 29.7%), and live birth (37.3% vs. 20.0%), alongside lower miscarriage rates (12.6% vs. 28.3%) compared to those ≥38 years. Optimal endometrial thickness of 11–11.65 mm substantially improved reproductive outcomes, whereas thin endometrium (≤7 mm) or excessively thick endometrium (≥11.65 mm) reduced implantation and pregnancy rates. Advanced maternal age drives oocyte aneuploidy and chromosomal abnormalities (>60% in patients ≥38 years), thereby impairing fertility and increasing adverse reproductive outcomes. The study highlights that infertility type, gonadotropin dosage, and endometrial receptivity independently influence live birth rates, establishing endometrial thickness and patient age as modifiable and non-modifiable determinants of assisted reproductive success in this population.

title: Prevalence of dysmorphic uterus in infertile women by 3D-TVUS: a retrospective longitudinal cohort study
summary: This retrospective longitudinal study demonstrates that dysmorphic uterus, a congenital uterine malformation (CUM), is substantially more prevalent in infertile women (27.3%) than previously reported in the literature when diagnosed using three-dimensional transvaginal ultrasound (3D-TVUS) combined with standardized CUME diagnostic criteria. Dysmorphic uteri are characterized by reduced T-angles, smaller lateral indentation angles, and greater lateral indentation depths, and are significantly associated with endometrial polyps, suggesting abnormal myometrial architecture may alter uterine contractility and vascular patterns. While hysteroscopic metroplasty improved reproductive outcomes in women with dysmorphic uteri—achieving live birth rates and clinical pregnancy rates comparable to women with normal uterine anatomy—septate uterus resection did not improve live birth rates according to current evidence. The findings underscore that routine high-resolution 3D-TVUS imaging should be incorporated into infertility evaluations, and a diagnosis of CMU should lower the threshold for diagnostic hysteroscopy to identify and treat coexisting endometrial pathology. However, the study authors acknowledge the need for prospective validation of the polyp-CMU association, mechanistic studies exploring shared pathophysiological pathways, and a well-designed randomized controlled trial to definitively establish the efficacy of hysteroscopic metroplasty for dysmorphic uterus.

title: FSH priming and hormonal modulation of oocyte competence in in vitro maturation for infertility treatment: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis evaluates FSH priming as a hormonal modulation strategy to enhance oocyte competence during in vitro maturation (IVM) for infertility treatment. FSH, a key regulator of oocyte folliculogenesis, promotes cumulus-oocyte communication through gap junction function and upregulation of maturation-associated genes, thereby enhancing both nuclear and cytoplasmic maturation. FSH priming significantly increases metaphase II (MII) maturation rates compared to non-stimulated controls, with particularly pronounced effects in polycystic ovary syndrome patients. However, despite improved oocyte maturation rates, FSH priming does not translate to enhanced fertilization rates, cleavage rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or implantation rates, suggesting that incomplete cytoplasmic maturation or suboptimal endometrial receptivity may limit downstream success. IVM offers distinct advantages over standard ovarian stimulation by reducing hormonal exposure and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk, while alternative approaches such as dual FSH/hCG priming, the CAPA-IVM system, and optimized culture media may further improve developmental competence and pregnancy outcomes in these cycles.

title: Effect of early rescue ICSI in multiple cycles of couples with successful fertilization in the initial cycle of conventional IVF
summary:
# Summary

Total fertilization failure in conventional IVF, driven by male factors such as sperm abnormalities and female-related factors including oocyte competence deficiencies, leads to cycle cancellation and psychological distress for patients undergoing multiple treatment attempts. Early rescue intracytoplasmic sperm injection (R-ICSI) addresses this problem by identifying fertilization signs within 5-6 hours post-insemination, preventing oocyte aging and polyploidy formation while improving embryo availability and quality. In couples with prior successful conventional IVF cycles, early R-ICSI significantly increases clinical pregnancy rates, ongoing pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to conventional IVF alone, with superior outcomes particularly evident in second cycles. The procedure's efficacy relies on calcium signaling-mediated oocyte activation and early embryonic development, though outcomes in couples undergoing multiple cycles (≥3) show reduced 2PN and embryo availability rates, suggesting that cumulative treatment effects may influence successive cycle responses. These findings demonstrate that early R-ICSI is a highly effective salvage strategy for couples experiencing fertilization failure in subsequent IVF cycles, substantially reducing cycle cancellation rates and improving reproductive outcomes.

title: Predicting the optimal timing for triggering in controlled ovarian stimulation: mature oocytes retrieval predictor
summary:
# Summary

This study developed the Follicle-to-mature Oocyte Index (FmOI) prediction model to optimize trigger timing during controlled ovarian stimulation (COS) for assisted reproductive technology. The model uses baseline serum FSH levels, the number of follicles ≥14 mm, and total gonadotropin dose—factors that reflect ovarian reserve and sensitivity to exogenous stimulation—to predict the number of mature oocytes (MII) that will be retrieved. The FmOI prediction model demonstrated high predictive accuracy (concordance index 0.98 for follitropin alfa and 0.87 for follitropin delta) and, when applied clinically, resulted in higher cumulative live birth rates in the follitropin alfa group with non-inferiority in the follitropin delta group compared to control. By accurately predicting mature oocyte yield, this model enables individualized trigger timing decisions, thereby optimizing outcomes in ART cycles while accounting for variation in ovarian response across different patient populations and FSH formulations.

title: The impact of seasonal variations on IVF pregnancy outcomes: a retrospective cohort study in Jinan, China
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study from Jinan, China examined how seasonal environmental variations affect IVF outcomes, revealing that seasonal factors significantly influence reproductive success through multiple physiological mechanisms. Seasonal temperature and light exposure patterns modulate key reproductive hormones—with pituitary hormones peaking in late summer while effector hormones remain elevated in winter and spring—and melatonin, which is regulated by light exposure, critically regulates endometrial morphology, embryo implantation, and pregnancy maintenance. The analysis found that spring cycles demonstrated paradoxically higher live birth rates (51.6%) and full-term delivery rates compared to winter, yet showed substantially elevated miscarriage risk (15.3% versus 10.1%), while trigger-day sunlight intensity exhibited a dose-dependent inverse association with pregnancy success, with each unit increase in solar exposure reducing clinical pregnancy probability by 2.2%. Winter cycles demonstrated the highest fertilization rates (62.0%), and autumn produced the highest Day 3 embryo counts, suggesting that early spermatogenesis and oogenesis are particularly sensitive to seasonal temperature fluctuations. Notably, trigger-day temperature inversely correlated with clinical pregnancy rates, while extreme heat was associated with hormonal imbalance, compromised oocyte quality, and mitochondrial dysfunction. These findings suggest that seasonal microenvironmental factors during ovarian stimulation significantly modulate IVF success trajectories, with implications for treatment timing optimization.

title: Effect of day 3 cell number on the pregnancy and neonatal outcomes of vitrified-warmed single blastocyst transfer
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined how day 3 embryo cell number affects pregnancy and neonatal outcomes following vitrified-warmed single blastocyst transfer in frozen embryo transfer cycles. Embryos with fewer than 7 cells on day 3 demonstrated significantly lower clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates compared to embryos with 7–9, 10–13, or greater than 13 cells, with the 7–9 cell group showing optimal implantation potential and the highest proportion of chromosomal euploidy. The <7-cell group also exhibited poorer morphological characteristics, including higher fragmentation rates (>25%) and lower blastomere evenness, whereas the >13-cell group produced predominantly high-quality blastocysts (97.62%) with minimal fragmentation. Interestingly, embryos in the >13-cell group were associated with a significantly higher proportion of male neonates compared to the <7-cell group. These findings support using day 3 cell number as a morphological predictor of implantation potential and suggest that embryos with abnormally slow developmental rates (fewer cells) have reduced competence for establishing successful pregnancies and live births.

title: Interplay of endocrine and psychological factors in IVF/ICSI outcomes: a prospective cohort analysis
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study identified multiple interacting endocrine and psychological factors influencing IVF/ICSI pregnancy outcomes. Perceived stress emerged as the sole significant psychological predictor, with higher stress levels directly reducing pregnancy rates, while depression, anxiety, and sleep quality showed no meaningful associations. Key endocrine parameters—including basal luteinizing hormone (LH) levels, endometrial thickness at embryo transfer, embryo quantity, and embryo quality—demonstrated strong positive correlations with clinical pregnancy success, with optimal outcomes occurring at endometrial thickness ≥12 mm and low basal LH <2 IU/L. Additionally, both female and male partner age significantly influenced outcomes, with advancing paternal age beyond 40 years associated with increased sperm DNA fragmentation and reduced pregnancy rates. The findings highlight that while infertility challenges both endocrine function and psychological well-being, stress management may be more clinically relevant than treating other mood disorders in optimizing assisted reproductive outcomes.

title: Nomogram-Based Prediction of Live Birth in GnRH Antagonist Protocol Fresh IVF/ICSI Cycles
summary:
# Summary

GnRH antagonist protocols for controlled ovarian stimulation in fresh IVF/ICSI cycles effectively inhibit premature luteinizing hormone surges and reduce gonadotropin consumption and ovarian hyperstimulation risks compared to traditional agonist regimens. However, fresh cycle outcomes under antagonist protocols remain suboptimal, primarily due to endometrial receptivity alterations rather than embryonic factors. To improve prognostication, the study developed a predictive nomogram incorporating six key clinical parameters: serum progesterone on post-ovulation day 9, number of embryos transferred, progesterone-to-follicle ratio, triggering-day progesterone levels, progesterone-to-oocyte ratio, and creatinine concentrations. Live birth success was associated with higher basal FSH levels, lower triggering-day progesterone and estradiol levels, increased high-quality embryo rates, and higher progesterone and estradiol levels six days post-transfer. This clinically accessible predictive tool enables more accurate estimation of reproductive success in antagonist-based IVF/ICSI cycles while supporting informed decision-making regarding fresh versus deferred embryo transfer strategies.

title: The auxiliary effect of oral nutritional supplements on fertility in women with diminished ovarian reserve: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that oral nutritional supplements, including vitamins, coenzyme Q10, and DHEA, provide meaningful improvements in fertility outcomes for women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR). Supplementation reduces follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) levels while elevating anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and increasing antral follicle counts and retrieved oocyte numbers, ultimately improving clinical pregnancy rates by 70%. Coenzyme Q10 emerges as particularly effective through its potent antioxidant activity and mitochondrial metabolic regulation, alleviating oxidative stress-induced cell damage and improving oocyte quality more effectively than alternative supplements. Supplementation duration exceeding two months yields superior results, and combination therapies prove more beneficial than single agents for modulating hormonal markers and follicle counts. Individual micronutrients such as folate, zinc, iron, selenium, and calcium contribute to enhanced oocyte quality and endometrial receptivity. These findings establish nutritional supplementation as a simple, low-risk adjuvant therapy capable of delaying ovarian aging and improving in vitro fertilization outcomes in women with DOR.

title: Effectiveness of hormone add-on strategies in ovarian stimulation for women with poor ovarian response: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

Poor ovarian response, characterized by decreased anti-Müllerian hormone levels, reduced antral follicle counts, elevated follicle-stimulating hormone, and compromised oocyte quality, significantly impairs reproductive potential and increases the risk of aneuploidy and miscarriage. A network meta-analysis of hormone add-on strategies for controlled ovarian stimulation identified human growth hormone as the most promising adjuvant, improving both clinical pregnancy rates and the number of retrieved metaphase II oocytes compared to estrogen supplementation. Testosterone pretreatment showed potential for improving live birth rates, while letrozole reduced gonadotropin requirements and stimulation duration. Although dehydroepiandrosterone, testosterone, and human growth hormone demonstrated benefits for reproductive outcomes in women with poor ovarian response, the evidence quality remains low to very low, suggesting that current hormone add-on strategies offer only modest improvements and warrant further investigation to optimize assisted reproductive outcomes.

title: Impact of intrauterine infusion of human chorionic gonadotropin and hyaluronic acid enriched medium on pregnancy outcomes in frozen-thawed cycles with recurrent implantation failure: An RCT
summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated whether combined intrauterine human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) infusion and hyaluronic acid-enriched embryo transfer medium could improve pregnancy outcomes in frozen-thawed cycles complicated by recurrent implantation failure. hCG enhances endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms including promotion of angiogenesis, increased uterine blood flow, modulation of cytokine production, and involvement in decidualization, implantation, and tissue remodeling. Hyaluronic acid, a key extracellular matrix component, improves embryo attachment and implantation by increasing cell-cell and cell-matrix adhesion, thereby facilitating embryo aggregation and endometrial adhesion. However, the combined intervention did not significantly improve overall pregnancy rates compared to control groups, though subset analysis revealed potential benefits in specific populations such as those using cleavage-stage embryos or receiving multiple embryo transfers. Success of embryo implantation fundamentally depends on synchronized embryo-endometrium interaction, which requires addressing multiple contributing factors to recurrent implantation failure including embryonic, endometrial, genetic, and immunological abnormalities.

title: Impact of Systematic Follicular Flushing on Egg Retrieval and Embryo Quality in IVF-ICSI Cycles: A Controlled Study?
summary:
# Summary

Systematic follicular flushing during oocyte retrieval in IVF-ICSI cycles significantly increases oocyte yield by recovering an additional 38% of cumulus-oocyte complexes, particularly during the second through fifth flush attempts, without compromising oocyte morphological quality or embryo development. However, follicular flushing is associated with a modest increase in slow cleavage rates and reduced oocyte maturation, particularly when ovulation is triggered by Ovitrelle alone, though this effect is not observed with double trigger (Decapeptyl plus Ovitrelle) or Decapeptyl alone protocols. Despite these minor developmental variations, the improved oocyte recovery through flushing does not translate into enhanced clinical outcomes, as pregnancy rates per transfer and miscarriage rates remain comparable between flushing and non-flushing groups. This controlled study suggests that while systematic follicular flushing optimizes oocyte collection efficiency in standard IVF-ICSI cycles, its clinical benefit must be weighed against increased procedure duration and slight reductions in oocyte maturation depending on the ovulatory trigger used.

title: The efficacy of adjuvant therapies in improving endometrial function of women undergoing assisted reproductive technology: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary: This network meta-analysis evaluated adjuvant therapies for improving endometrial function in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology, identifying endometrial thickness and clinical pregnancy rates as critical outcomes. Three interventions—sildenafil, platelet-rich plasma (PRP), and granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (G-CSF)—emerged as the most effective, significantly improving endometrial thickness with effect sizes ranging from 1.27 to 1.66 millimeters. PRP demonstrated the strongest enhancement of biochemical pregnancy rates (odds ratio 4.28), while aspirin, G-CSF, growth hormone, and PRP all improved clinical pregnancy rates compared to control groups, with PRP showing the largest effect (odds ratio 2.66). The analysis confirms that adequate endometrial thickness and endometrial receptivity are essential prerequisites for successful embryo implantation and pregnancy maintenance, and these therapeutic adjuvants appear particularly beneficial for women with thin endometrium. Notably, these findings underscore the importance of optimizing endometrial characteristics alongside embryo quality in assisted reproduction, offering evidence-based options to enhance ART success rates across patient populations.

title: Influence of Medical Versus Surgical Evacuation of Early Miscarriage Loss on Reproductive Outcomes of Women in Subsequent FET Cycle
summary: This study compared medical versus surgical management of early miscarriage on subsequent frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes. Surgical evacuation, including dilatation and curettage, significantly reduced endometrial thickness and was associated with lower live birth rates, reduced positive pregnancy test rates, and decreased clinical pregnancy rates compared to medical management. The adverse effects of surgical evacuation persisted in subsequent FET cycles, with patients demonstrating thinner endometrial lining that correlated with diminished reproductive success. Medical management, conversely, maintained higher live birth rates, particularly in women under 35 years of age. Additionally, surgical evacuation was linked to increased cesarean section rates in non-preimplantation genetic testing FET cycles. These findings suggest that the mechanical trauma from surgical evacuation compromises future reproductive outcomes by damaging endometrial integrity, supporting medical management as the preferable approach for early miscarriage care in women planning subsequent assisted reproduction.

title: Development and Validation of a Combined Model Integrating Early Pregnancy Ultrasound Radiomics and Clinical Features to Predict Live Birth Following Single Vitrified‐Warmed Blastocyst Transfer
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a predictive model combining early pregnancy ultrasound radiomics with clinical features to forecast live birth success following single vitrified-warmed blastocyst transfer. The integrated model demonstrated superior predictive performance (AUC = 0.806 in training, 0.718 in testing) compared to radiomics-only or clinical-only approaches, utilizing an AdaBoost classifier to extract high-dimensional quantitative features from gestational sac ultrasound images alongside key clinical variables including maternal age, endometrial thickness, and a radiomics-derived risk score. Maternal age and endometrial thickness emerged as the strongest independent clinical predictors of live birth, while early ultrasound parameters such as gestational sac size, yolk sac dimensions, and fetal cardiac activity correlated strongly with pregnancy viability and miscarriage risk. The researchers constructed a practical nomogram incorporating these three predictive factors (maternal age, endometrial thickness, and the radiomics risk score) to facilitate clinical decision-making, with decision curve analysis and calibration curves confirming the combined model's excellent clinical utility and agreement between predicted and observed outcomes.

title: Analysis of related factors of biochemical pregnancy loss in single blastocyst transfer in the hormone replacement protocol frozen-thawed cycle
summary:
# Summary

This study analyzed risk factors for biochemical pregnancy loss (BPL) in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles using hormone replacement therapy (HRT), focusing on single blastocyst transfers. Multiple maternal, paternal, and embryological factors were associated with BPL, including increased female and male age, elevated body mass index, higher serum estradiol levels, and poor blastocyst quality. Critically, insufficient endometrial thickness emerged as the strongest independent predictor of biochemical pregnancy loss, with endometrial thickness below 10 mm significantly increasing BPL risk, while blastocysts graded CB or BC and Day 6 transfers showed markedly higher loss rates compared to higher-quality embryos. Protective factors included greater antral follicle count, higher luteinizing hormone levels, and increased endometrial thickness prior to endometrial transformation. These findings underscore the importance of optimizing endometrial preparation and selecting high-quality blastocysts for transfer to improve pregnancy outcomes in HRT-FET cycles.

title: Efficacy and safety of nutritional supplements in female infertility: a network meta-analysis
summary: This network meta-analysis evaluated the efficacy and safety of nutritional supplements for treating female infertility, which is predominantly caused by ovulatory dysfunction, often associated with polycystic ovary syndrome. Multiple supplements demonstrated beneficial effects on reproductive outcomes: curcumin substantially increased oocyte retrieval, fertilization rate, and metaphase II oocytes; astaxanthin improved good-quality embryo numbers; and several supplements enhanced clinical pregnancy rates, with the combination of probiotics and vitamin D showing the most significant improvement. Vitamin D alone, coenzyme Q10, and the combination of vitamin D with vitamin E also substantially increased clinical pregnancy rates compared to placebo. Importantly, the safety analysis found no significant associations between nutritional supplementation and miscarriage risk, supporting the therapeutic potential of these interventions in regulating the ovarian environment and enhancing pregnancy outcomes in infertile women.

title: Progestin primed ovarian stimulation yields comparable outcomes to the GnRH antagonist protocol for controlled ovarian hyperstimulation
summary:
# Summary

This study compares progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) with GnRH antagonist protocols for controlled ovarian hyperstimulation in assisted reproductive technology, demonstrating that both approaches yield comparable clinical outcomes. The biological challenge in ovarian stimulation is preventing premature luteinizing hormone (LH) surges, which trigger early ovulation and reduce oocyte retrieval; traditional GnRH agonists and antagonists prevent these surges but carry drawbacks including cost, injection burden, and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk. PPOS, introduced in 2015, uses orally administered progestins and medroxyprogesterone acetate to suppress LH surges while maintaining oocyte competence, offering a more accessible alternative with comparable efficacy to GnRH antagonists. The study found no significant differences between PPOS and antagonist protocols in oocyte yield, embryo quality, fertilization rates, or pregnancy outcomes, with live birth rates of 25.33% and 30.64% respectively (p=0.261), and multiple regression analysis confirmed that stimulation protocol type was not associated with live birth rates. Patient factors such as maternal age, infertility duration, ovarian reserve markers (AMH, antral follicle count), and embryo quality emerged as stronger predictors of reproductive success than protocol choice, indicating that PPOS represents a valid, cost-effective alternative for ovarian stimulation in infertility treatment.

title: Impact of platelet-rich plasma intrauterine perfusion on endometrial receptivity and pregnancy outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure and thin endometrium
summary: Intrauterine platelet-rich plasma (PRP) perfusion significantly improves endometrial receptivity and pregnancy outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure and thin endometrium. PRP treatment increased endometrial thickness and enhanced uterine blood flow by reducing vascular resistance indices across multiple uterine arteries—including the uterine, arcuate, and spiral arteries. The treatment also favorably modified the endocrine environment, increasing estradiol levels while reducing follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, collectively supporting endometrial function. Consequently, PRP-treated patients demonstrated substantially higher implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, ongoing pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to conventional treatment controls. These findings suggest that PRP intrauterine perfusion addresses the underlying pathophysiology of thin endometrium and impaired uterine hemodynamics, offering a promising therapeutic intervention for improving reproductive outcomes in this challenging patient population.

title: GnRH-a Pretreatment Doses and Frozen-Thawed Blastocyst Transfer Outcomes in PCOS Patients: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist (GnRH-a) pretreatment at different doses combined with hormone replacement therapy (HRT) for frozen-thawed blastocyst transfer in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients.
