PCOS causes anovulation, hyperandrogenism, and insulin resistance, leading to ovulatory dysfunction and impaired endometrial receptivity that reduces pregnancy rates and increases miscarriage risk. While GnRH-a downregulation can suppress spontaneous ovulation, stabilize endometrial development, and regulate inflammatory mediators like IL-6 and IL-11, this retrospective cohort study found that neither 1.5 mg nor 3.75 mg GnRH-a doses significantly improved embryo implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or early miscarriage rates compared to HRT alone in 555 FET cycles. The analysis identified embryo number transferred—particularly double-embryo transfer—as the only significant factor associated with clinical pregnancy outcomes. These findings suggest that GnRH-a pretreatment may not substantially benefit PCOS patients undergoing FET, and a simplified HRT-only protocol could reduce medical costs and drug exposure while maintaining comparable reproductive outcomes.

title: Sperm Selection Using Cumulus Cell Column Improves Sperm DNA Integrity, Embryo Morphokinetics, and Clinical Outcomes Following ICSI: A Randomized Clinical Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized clinical trial demonstrates that cumulus cell column (CCC) sperm selection significantly enhances reproductive outcomes in intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) by leveraging the natural biological functions of cumulus cells. The cumulus cells select for mature, high-quality sperm by identifying those capable of binding hyaluronic acid—sperm characterized by normal morphology, minimal DNA fragmentation, and functional capacity for acrosome reaction and hyperactivated motility. Sperm selected through this method showed reduced DNA fragmentation (from 37.08% to 23.36%) and produced embryos with accelerated developmental kinetics and fewer cleavage abnormalities, resulting in markedly improved clinical outcomes including higher implantation rates (58% vs. 28.4%), chemical pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to controls. The mechanism involves cumulus cell secretion of progesterone, which activates the CatSper calcium channel and prepares sperm for fertilization, while cumulus cells simultaneously remove harmful factors including excess cholesterol, reactive oxygen species, and immature surface proteins. These findings underscore the importance of selecting sperm with intact DNA integrity and proper chromatin condensation, as high sperm DNA fragmentation impairs embryo morphokinetic parameters and increases chromosomal abnormality risk in developing embryos.

title: Comparative Embryological Outcomes of Vitrified and Fresh Oocytes in Patients with Diminished Ovarian Reserve
summary:
# Summary

This study compared embryological outcomes between vitrified and fresh oocytes in patients with diminished ovarian reserve (POSEIDON groups 3 and 4, defined by low anti-Müllerian hormone and/or antral follicle count). Oocyte vitrification achieved a high survival rate of 93.1% and demonstrated comparable fertilization rates, cleavage rates, embryo quality, and developmental potential to fresh oocytes within the same patients. The strategy of oocyte accumulation through multiple vitrification cycles effectively increased cumulative oocyte yield and embryo numbers, with no significant differences in aneuploidy rates or congenital anomalies between vitrified and fresh groups. Notably, vitrified oocytes mitigated age-related declines in outcomes, as embryological results remained consistent across age groups in the vitrified cohort while younger patients showed superior oocyte yield in the fresh group. Overall, the findings support oocyte vitrification as a reliable strategy for patients with diminished ovarian reserve, enabling accumulation of adequate oocytes to improve chances of obtaining good-quality embryos and achieving pregnancy.

title: Intravenous immunoglobulin and prednisolone to women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss after assisted reproductive technology treatment: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
summary: This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluated whether combined prednisolone and intravenous immunoglobulin (P&IVIg) improves pregnancy outcomes in women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss after assisted reproductive technology treatment. The rationale for this combination therapy targets immune-mediated mechanisms implicated in recurrent pregnancy loss: prednisolone suppresses natural killer cell cytotoxicity, reduces Th1 levels, promotes Th2 and regulatory T cells, and enhances trophoblast outgrowth, while intravenous immunoglobulin inhibits natural killer cell activity and promotes regulatory T cell expansion. Although overall pregnancy rates did not significantly improve with P&IVIg treatment (25.0% versus 15.0% in placebo), among women who achieved pregnancy, the treatment group showed higher live birth rates and appeared to reduce pregnancy loss risk, suggesting the combination may support implantation and early pregnancy maintenance rather than enhance initial endometrial receptivity. The findings indicate that immunomodulatory treatment during the peri-implantation period may selectively benefit a subset of patients with immune-mediated recurrent pregnancy loss, though the trial did not achieve statistical significance for its primary outcome in the overall cohort.

title: Effect of adding gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists for luteal support in antagonist cycles on pregnancy outcomes
summary: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRHa) improve pregnancy and live birth rates when added as luteal support during antagonist-protocol in vitro fertilization cycles, achieving clinical pregnancy rates of 59.54% compared to 53.80% in conventional treatment. The mechanistic basis involves GnRHa binding to GnRH receptors on granulosa lutein cells and endometrial tissue, where it stimulates luteinizing hormone secretion, upregulates progesterone synthesis enzymes (including a 68% increase in 3β-HSD activity), and activates calcium signaling pathways that enhance luteal function and endometrial receptivity. Antagonist protocols inherently induce luteal phase deficiency through suppression of endogenous hormone secretion by supraphysiological estrogen and progesterone levels, which GnRHa supplementation effectively counteracts, particularly benefiting patients with progesterone levels below 1.0 ng/ml on the day of hCG administration. GnRHa also improves endometrial receptivity by modulating DNA methylation of Hoxa10 and may enhance placental function through GnRH receptor expression in trophoblast cells. Safety data demonstrates that GnRHa luteal support does not significantly increase risks of early miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, or preterm birth, with only transient side effects such as hot flashes and headaches observed.

title: Effectiveness of Controlled Ovarian Stimulation for Oocyte Preservation in Oncologic Patients: Insights from DuoStim Protocol
summary:
# Summary

Chemotherapy and radiotherapy cause significant ovarian toxicity in oncologic patients, leading to infertility and premature ovarian failure, making fertility preservation through oocyte cryopreservation essential before cancer treatment. This study evaluated controlled ovarian stimulation across different cycle phases and the DuoStim protocol—which performs two consecutive stimulations—in 203 oncologic cycles, with breast cancer (47.3%) and lymphomas (32.0%) being the most common indications. While the DuoStim group showed lower antral follicle counts and fewer oocytes per stimulation cycle compared to other protocols, cumulative oocyte retrieval from both stimulations achieved comparable mature oocyte yields to single-cycle approaches, making DuoStim a valuable strategy for poor responders who obtain insufficient oocytes initially. Multivariable analysis identified anti-Müllerian hormone and antral follicle count, but not age or basal FSH, as predictors of mature oocyte yield. The study demonstrates that controlled ovarian stimulation using GnRH antagonist protocols, with or without adjuvants like letrozole for breast cancer patients, effectively preserves fertility without delaying cancer treatment, and luteal phase stimulation offers comparable outcomes to follicular phase approaches.

title: Implantation Failure: Where to Look Up?
summary: Successful embryo implantation requires both a competent embryo and a receptive endometrium to establish productive cross-talk, which involves coordinated molecular interactions between integrins, selectins, and cadherins that facilitate embryo attachment and trophoblast invasion. Recurrent implantation failure is a multifactorial condition associated with compromised gamete and embryo quality, chromosomal abnormalities, impaired endometrial receptivity, and microbiota dysregulation. Maternal factors significantly impair implantation success, including advanced age (which reduces oocyte quality and increases chromosomal disorders through oxidative stress), obesity and metabolic dysfunction (which reduce endometrial receptivity), smoking (which impairs uterine blood flow and gamete quality), and high alcohol exposure. Balanced uterine immunity is essential for implantation and pregnancy maintenance; dysregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, TNF-α) relative to anti-inflammatory factors (IL-10, TGF-β) contributes to implantation failure, as does chronic endometritis caused by pathogenic microorganisms including Mycoplasma, Ureaplasma, Chlamydia, and pathogenic bacteria. Paternal factors also contribute through sperm quality parameters including DNA fragmentation, chromatin integrity, motility, and morphology, while environmental endocrine disruptors such as BPA and phthalates may pose emerging reproductive risks.

title: Effects of Maternal Age on Receptivity and Pregnancy Outcomes of Single Euploid Transfers: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Advanced maternal age impairs endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms, reducing endometrial thickness, creating unfavorable ultrasound patterns, and diminishing biochemical and clinical pregnancy rates in patients undergoing euploid embryo transfer. The aging endometrium exhibits reduced vascular perfusion, altered gene expression affecting immune tolerance and epithelial proliferation, and epigenetic changes including H3K27ac loss that collaborates with progesterone receptor signaling. Additionally, advanced maternal age compromises embryo quality itself, with older women producing euploid blastocysts exhibiting poorer morphology and slower development, which independently correlate with lower implantation rates. Notably, while advanced maternal age significantly impacts early implantation and pregnancy initiation, the senescent endometrium appears sufficiently functional to support pregnancy maintenance, with no demonstrated correlation to clinical miscarriage or live birth rates once clinical pregnancy is established. These findings demonstrate that age-related fertility decline results from both endometrial aging as an independent factor and embryonic compromises, though preimplantation genetic testing can mitigate aneuploidy confounders to reveal the true impact of uterine senescence on implantation.

title: Integrating Chinese Herbal Medicine in IVF: A Protocol for Randomized Controlled Trial of Gushen Antai Pill
summary:
# Summary

This protocol describes a randomized controlled trial investigating Gushen Antai Pill (GSATP), a traditional Chinese herbal medicine, as an adjunct to standard luteal phase support (LPS) in in vitro fertilization. Controlled ovarian stimulation causes luteal insufficiency in nearly all IVF patients, necessitating supplemental progesterone support; combining GSATP with progesterone-based LPS has demonstrated significantly higher ongoing pregnancy rates and reduced threatened miscarriage risk compared to LPS alone. According to traditional Chinese medicine theory, GSATP addresses threatened miscarriage by tonifying the kidneys and regulating liver and spleen function through its active herbal components, including Dipsaci and Cuscutae Semen for kidney support, alongside supplementary herbs like Rehmannia Glutinosa and agents that invigorate qi and calm the liver. Prior clinical studies have shown that progesterone combined with GSATP improves pregnancy rates, reduces miscarriage risk, and may enhance embryo implantation conditions. This trial will evaluate whether integrating GSATP into contemporary IVF protocols offers improved reproductive outcomes through combined mechanistic pathways of both conventional hormone therapy and traditional herbal intervention.

title: Association between latent tuberculosis infection and assisted reproductive outcomes: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examines how latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) affects reproductive outcomes in infertile patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART). Mycobacterium tuberculosis causes tuberculosis, which is a common cause of infertility through genital TB (GTB) that damages reproductive anatomy by causing tubal obstruction in 90% of cases, endometrial impairment in 50-60%, and ovarian dysfunction. The hormonal changes during pregnancy (elevated estrogen, progesterone, and human chorionic gonadotropin) suppress T-cell function and reactivate latent TB infection, disrupting endometrial receptivity through altered expression of adhesion molecules and immune factors like vascular endothelial growth factor and leukaemia inhibitory factor. Persistent immune activation in LTBI patients also disrupts immune tolerance, activates the coagulation system leading to microthrombus formation that compromises placental blood perfusion, and increases miscarriage risk. However, meta-analysis showed no significant differences in clinical pregnancy or live birth rates between LTBI and non-LTBI groups, though patients with imaging evidence of old TB lesions and positive interferon-gamma release assay results had significantly elevated miscarriage risk. Anti-TB treatment improves embryo quality, implantation rates, and pregnancy outcomes by restoring the ovarian microenvironment in LGTB-positive women.

title: Analysis of ultrasound parameters influencing endometrial receptivity and a pregnancy outcomes predictive model for patients undergoing in vitro fertilization and embryo transfer: a prospective study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study identifies critical ultrasound parameters predicting pregnancy outcomes in IVF-ET patients by analyzing the relationship between endometrial receptivity and vascular function. Endometrial receptivity, which is essential for blastocyst apposition, adhesion, and invasion, depends on synchronized endometrial thickening and robust vascularization. Lower resistance and pulsatility indices indicate improved endometrial arterial perfusion and predict successful pregnancy, whereas elevated indices reflect increased vascular resistance that compromises endometrial perfusion and reduces receptivity. Using machine learning analysis, the study identified eight key predictive variables including infertility etiology, baseline luteinizing hormone levels, oocyte quantity, uterine cavity volume, and multiple blood flow parameters; a Gradient Boosting model incorporating these factors along with embryonic characteristics demonstrated superior predictive accuracy for ongoing pregnancy. Enhanced endometrial blood flow grading, higher MII oocyte yields, elevated baseline LH levels, and reduced subendometrial perfusion indices collectively correlate with greater pregnancy likelihood, while impaired endometrial angiogenesis conversely leads to recurrent implantation failure and pregnancy loss.

title: Effect of Different Female Factors on Pregnancy Rates in Artificial Insemination With Donor Sperm Cycles: A Prospective Observational Study
summary: This prospective observational study examined how female factors influence pregnancy rates in artificial insemination with donor sperm (AID) cycles, a treatment approach for male factor infertility. The key finding was that duration of infertility showed a strong negative association with both clinical and ongoing pregnancy rates, with longer infertility duration predicting lower success. Interestingly, common female reproductive conditions including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) did not significantly affect pregnancy outcomes in AID cycles, though severe endometriosis was noted to reduce pregnancy rates in intrauterine insemination procedures. Other female parameters such as age, body mass index, and type of infertility showed weaker associations with AID success, and minimal to mild endometriosis did not impact outcomes in this donor sperm population. The findings suggest that in AID cycles, the duration of infertility is a more critical prognostic factor than specific female diagnoses, and that many female reproductive conditions may have less impact when donor sperm are used.

title: Cumulative live birth rates under three consecutive IVF/ICSI treatment cycles are reduced in women with endometriosis and/or adenomyosis diagnosed by ultrasonography
summary: Women with endometriosis and/or adenomyosis demonstrated significantly reduced cumulative live birth rates across three consecutive IVF/ICSI cycles, with a 15% lower chance of success compared to unaffected women. Both conditions are estrogen-dependent disorders that frequently coexist and share overlapping pathophysiology characterized by inflammation, local hyperestrogenism, progesterone resistance, and aberrant angiogenesis in ectopic endometrial tissue. These pathological features impair reproductive outcomes by compromising endometrial receptivity, reducing implantation rates, and increasing miscarriage rates, with adenomyosis additionally causing altered uterine function and persistent uterine dysfunction when untreated. The deleterious effects were most pronounced following fresh embryo transfers, whereas frozen embryo transfer outcomes were comparable between affected and unaffected women, suggesting that superphysiologic estrogen exposure during fresh cycles may exacerbate the underlying endometrial dysfunction. Women with multiple direct ultrasonographic features of adenomyosis (hyperechogenic islands, myometrial cysts, subendometrial lines) showed particularly compromised outcomes, with cumulative live birth rates of 70% versus 83.7% in healthy controls. These findings underscore the compounded negative impact of combined endometriosis and adenomyosis on assisted reproductive technology success and highlight the clinical importance of ultrasonographic diagnosis for counseling and treatment planning.

title: Assisted reproductive technology trends in Korea: Annual report for 2022
summary:
# Summary

This 2022 Korean assisted reproductive technology report reveals that delayed marriage has contributed to elevated maternal age at treatment, with the mean age of women undergoing IVF reaching 38.4 years, substantially older than international standards. Advancing maternal age significantly impairs reproductive outcomes across multiple parameters: it reduces oocyte retrieval numbers, diminishes clinical pregnancy rates for both fresh and frozen-thawed embryo transfers, and increases treatment discontinuation rates due to oocyte retrieval failure and embryo development failure. Despite these age-related challenges, frozen-thawed embryo transfers demonstrate superior pregnancy rates compared to fresh transfers (42.0% versus 30.2%), particularly benefiting older women through preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy. Female factor infertility, including ovulatory dysfunction and diminished ovarian reserve, represents the predominant etiology, with IVF cycles increasing 51.2% from 2019 to 2022, driven primarily by older women seeking treatment. National Health Insurance coverage improvements have enhanced access to infertility care, supporting the growing demand for both IVF and intrauterine insemination, the latter primarily indicated for unexplained infertility and mild male factor cases in younger women aged 30-39.

title: Effects of platelet-rich plasma as a new additive on human embryo development and clinical outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) serves as an effective culture medium additive that enhances multiple aspects of embryo development and reproductive outcomes by delivering high concentrations of growth factors including platelet-derived growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor, transforming growth factor, and epidermal growth factor. These growth factors promote cell proliferation and differentiation while reducing oxidative stress and DNA fragmentation, mechanisms that improve embryo quality, increase cleavage and blastocyst development rates, and enhance sperm parameters. Clinical application of PRP significantly increased implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and ongoing pregnancy rates compared to control groups, with particular benefits demonstrated in patients with tubal factor infertility and unexplained infertility. Additionally, PRP administration increased endometrial thickness and improved outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure and poor ovarian response, while maintaining safety with no observed fetal abnormalities.

title: The value of endometrial scratch injury in infertile women trying to conceive through non-in vitro fertilization cycles: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis of 32 randomized controlled trials demonstrates that endometrial scratch injury (ESI) significantly improves fertility outcomes in women attempting to conceive naturally or through non-IVF cycles. ESI improves clinical pregnancy rates (odds ratio 2.06), ongoing pregnancy and live birth rates (odds ratio 1.68-1.80), and chemical pregnancy rates (odds ratio 2.32) compared to controls. The mechanism underlying ESI's therapeutic effects involves deliberate trauma to the endometrium that alters cytokines and growth factors, modulates endometrial gene expression, promotes immune cell recruitment, and enhances local vascularization—collectively improving endometrial receptivity during the implantation window. While the procedure carries minor adverse effects including bleeding and pain, the evidence indicates ESI is a cost-effective and relatively safe treatment option for infertile women, particularly those with endometriosis-related infertility where endometrial dysfunction contributes to conception failure.

title: Pregnancy outcomes in women with anti-β2-glycoprotein I/human leukocyte antigen-DR autoantibodies receiving assisted reproductive technology: a prospective cohort study
summary: Women with anti-β2-glycoprotein I/human leukocyte antigen-DR (anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR) autoantibodies experience significantly reduced pregnancy outcomes in assisted reproductive technology, including higher rates of recurrent implantation failure and lower implantation rates per embryo. These autoantibodies, which are particularly prevalent in women with severe endometriosis and recurrent implantation failure, target β2GPI/HLA-DR antigens expressed on endometrial epithelial and decidual endothelial cells, where they disrupt placental function through mechanisms including endometrial thrombus formation, endothelial injury, and intrauterine inflammation. Low-dose aspirin combined with unfractionated heparin treatment substantially improves clinical outcomes, increasing clinical pregnancy rates threefold and live birth rates while reducing preeclampsia and preterm delivery in antibody-positive women. The therapeutic benefits of aspirin and heparin stem from their anti-inflammatory, anticoagulant, and antithrombotic properties, which restore uterine perfusion and counteract the pathogenic effects of autoantibody-induced thrombogenesis. These findings establish anti-β2GPI/HLA-DR autoantibodies as a clinically significant immunological factor contributing to reproductive failure, particularly in endometriosis-associated infertility, and support targeted immunomodulatory therapy as an evidence-based intervention for affected patients undergoing fertility treatment.

title: Comparative efficacy of r-hFSH Alfa + r-LH versus r-hFSH delta + hMG in poor ovarian responders
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study evaluated two gonadotropin protocols for treating poor ovarian responders undergoing assisted reproduction. The r-hFSH alfa + r-LH combination (Pergoveris) significantly outperformed the r-hFSH delta + hMG regimen (Rekovelle + Meropur) in stimulating oocyte quality and quantity, achieving higher numbers of retrieved oocytes, metaphase II oocytes, and resulting embryos, while requiring lower total gonadotropin doses. Despite these improvements in ovarian response, both protocols achieved equivalent clinical pregnancy rates, biochemical pregnancy rates, and miscarriage rates, indicating that superior oocyte retrieval did not translate to better fertility outcomes. The r-hFSH alfa + r-LH protocol also demonstrated cost advantages, yielding lower treatment costs per cycle and per mature oocyte retrieved. These findings suggest that for poor ovarian responders—characterized by low anti-Müllerian hormone levels, reduced antral follicle counts, or advanced maternal age—the Pergoveris regimen offers superior resource efficiency and oocyte yield without sacrificing pregnancy safety or success rates.

title: The influence of endometrial thickness on clinical pregnancy outcomes in early-follicular long-acting and midluteal short-acting GnRH agonist long protocols: a large retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This large retrospective study examines how endometrial thickness influences clinical pregnancy outcomes in assisted reproductive technology using two different GnRH agonist protocols. Endometrial thickness is established as a key marker of endometrial receptivity, with thin endometrium (<7 mm) significantly reducing implantation success due to insufficient glandular development, vascularization, and hormonal support. The early-follicular long-acting GnRH agonist protocol produced superior outcomes compared to the midluteal short-acting protocol, yielding higher clinical pregnancy and live birth rates alongside greater endometrial thickness and increased oocyte retrieval. Notably, the relationship between endometrial thickness and pregnancy outcomes differs by protocol: the early-follicular protocol exhibits a nonlinear association with a critical threshold around 10.6 mm for pregnancy rates and 15 mm for miscarriage rates, while the midluteal protocol shows a continuous linear relationship. When endometrial thickness reaches or exceeds 15 mm, both protocols achieve comparable outcomes with no further benefit, indicating that very thick endometrium does not adversely affect pregnancy success.

title: Characterization of infertile couples and outcomes of their first in vitro fertilization cycle at public fertility centers in Northern and Central Portugal: a five-year cohort study
summary: This five-year cohort study from Portuguese fertility centers examined 3,767 first IVF cycles to identify predictors of assisted reproductive technology success and characterize infertility etiologies. The analysis revealed that female age is the strongest predictor of ART success, with pregnancy rates declining significantly with advancing maternal age, while male factor infertility remains the most prevalent cause of infertility, occurring in isolation in 31.3% of cases. Modifiable risk factors such as tobacco use impair reproductive outcomes by diminishing ovarian reserve and reducing uterine receptivity, and obesity similarly decreases embryo quality and live birth rates while increasing miscarriage risk. Clinical outcomes were substantially influenced by treatment parameters: Day 5 blastocyst transfer achieved higher live birth rates (47%) compared to Day 3 embryo transfer (23%), natural cycles outperformed artificial cycles in live birth rates and miscarriage rates, and single embryo transfer yielded a 35.4% live birth rate. IVF effectively treats multiple infertility causes including endometriosis, tubal abnormalities, and ovulatory disorders, though multiple pregnancies carried substantially elevated prematurity risk at 70.7% compared to 12.5% for singletons. The cohort also demonstrated an increasing proportion of women aged 38 years or older seeking fertility treatment, underscoring the clinical importance of age-related counseling and optimization of treatment protocols.

title: Ovulation triggers in unassisted reproduction, an old question still unanswered: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the efficacy of ovulation triggers in unassisted reproduction for patients with anovulatory or unexplained infertility. Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) serves as the primary ovulation trigger, administered after follicle maturation (≥17-21mm), and is typically combined with ovulation-inducing agents such as clomiphene citrate and human menopausal gonadotropin. While hCG reliably triggers ovulation, the meta-analysis found weak and inconclusive associations between ovulation trigger use and clinical pregnancy outcomes, with odds ratios of 1.23 for clinical pregnancy, 0.92 for live birth, and 0.68 for miscarriage—all with 95% confidence intervals crossing unity. Although ovulation triggers probably increase the likelihood of ovulation occurring, the current evidence does not definitively support their use in improving pregnancy success rates. A major clinical concern is that hCG administration carries a significant risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, potentially limiting its therapeutic benefit. These findings highlight a persistent gap between the mechanistic success of triggering ovulation and demonstrable improvements in reproductive outcomes in unassisted reproduction.

title: Effect of assisted hatching on pregnancy outcomes following 1,678,872 single embryo transfers based on the Japan Assisted Reproductive Technology Registry
summary:
# Summary

This large registry study of 1,678,872 single embryo transfers examined whether assisted hatching (AH), a technique that facilitates embryo escape from the zona pellucida, improves pregnancy outcomes in assisted reproduction. After propensity-score weighting to account for confounding variables, AH showed marginal or no benefit overall, with clinical pregnancy rates of 29.6% versus 31.3% and live birth rates of 21.2% versus 22.4% in the AH and non-AH groups, respectively. Notably, AH increased risks of miscarriage (0.82% increase), multiple pregnancy including monozygotic twinning (0.23% increase), and placenta accreta spectrum, while reducing ectopic pregnancy and placenta previa incidence. The efficacy of AH varied substantially by embryo type and patient age: frozen-thawed blastocyst transfer in women under 35 years showed modest live birth improvements, whereas fresh cleavage-stage and fresh blastocyst transfers showed reduced outcomes with AH. The findings suggest AH may damage embryos directly or cause embryo-endometrial asynchrony through unnecessary manipulation, with effects potentially amplified in older women, and support selective rather than routine application of this technique in assisted reproduction.

title: GnRH agonist improves CLBR after one IVF cycle: a propensity score-matched and molecular mechanism study
summary:
# Summary

This propensity score-matched study demonstrates that GnRH agonist protocols achieve significantly higher cumulative live birth rates (81.54% versus 73.07%) and superior clinical pregnancy rates compared to GnRH antagonist protocols in assisted reproduction. The mechanistic advantage of GnRH agonists stems from their superior endometrial receptivity, characterized by greater endometrial thickness, higher serum estradiol levels, and enhanced decidualization of endometrial stromal cells. GnRH antagonists impair endometrial function by disrupting mesenchymal-epithelial transition (MET) in stromal cells through reduced E-cadherin expression and decreased production of critical decidual factors IGFBP1 and prolactin, which are normally secreted by decidualized cells to support embryo implantation. These molecular defects mirror abnormalities observed in patients with recurrent implantation failure, where reduced E-cadherin, HOXA10, and IGFBP1 expression compromise endometrial receptivity. The study reveals that GnRH antagonists' rapid blockade of GnRH receptors disrupts the hormonal balance necessary for proper endometrial preparation, ultimately reducing implantation and live birth rates despite comparable ovarian stimulation outcomes.

title: Abnormal endometrial peristalsis in frozen-thawed embryo transfer: risk factors and improvement of Atosiban treatment
summary:
# Summary

Abnormal endometrial peristaltic waves (EPWs) occur in approximately 30% of frozen-thawed embryo transfer patients and significantly impair implantation, with contraction frequencies exceeding two per minute associated with nearly 50% reduction in pregnancy rates. Multiple risk factors contribute to abnormal EPWs, including prolonged infertility duration, parity, tubal disease, male factor infertility, uterine anomalies, and use of hormone replacement therapy or GnRH agonist protocols, whereas prior cesarean delivery appears protective. The mechanism underlying excessive peristalsis involves supraphysiological estradiol levels and upregulated oxytocin receptor expression in artificial cycles, which fails to achieve the uterine relaxation normally promoted by endogenous progesterone and hCG in natural cycles. Atosiban, an oxytocin and vasopressin V1A receptor antagonist, improves clinical pregnancy rates by inhibiting these excessive contractions, with particular benefit observed in cleavage-stage embryo transfers (50.2% versus 41.4% without treatment). This work underscores the critical role of physiologic uterine peristalsis in endometrial receptivity and suggests that pharmacologic suppression of abnormal contractility through atosiban administration represents an effective strategy for improving reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: Do different male infertility factors impact embryological, cumulative pregnancy and neonatal outcomes in IVF/ICSI cycles? A retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study examined how different male infertility factors affect embryological development, pregnancy outcomes, and neonatal results in IVF/ICSI cycles. Severe oligoasthenozoospermia (OAT-S) and non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) significantly impaired reproductive outcomes by reducing fertilization rates, embryo utilization rates, blastocyst formation, and cumulative live birth rates compared to controls, with NOA additionally associated with increased pregnancy loss. The study identified oxidative stress-induced sperm DNA fragmentation and elevated mitochondrial DNA copy number in severely infertile men as potential mechanisms compromising embryonic development and genomic stability. Notably, while severe male infertility factors markedly increased fertilization failure and embryonic developmental impairment, they had no impact on obstetrical, perinatal, or neonatal outcomes, indicating that embryos surviving to transfer and implantation develop normally. These findings underscore the critical role of sperm motility and DNA integrity in early reproductive success while confirming ICSI's effectiveness in enabling biological paternity for men with severe male factor infertility.

title: Association of GnRH Agonist Pretreatment with Reproductive Outcomes in Women ≥35 years with Diminished Ovarian Reserve Undergoing Frozen Embryo Transfer: A Large Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: This large retrospective cohort study demonstrates that GnRH agonist (GnRH-a) pretreatment combined with hormone replacement therapy (HRT) significantly improves reproductive outcomes in women aged 35 and older with diminished ovarian reserve undergoing frozen embryo transfer. GnRH-a + HRT improved both live birth rates and clinical pregnancy rates compared to HRT alone, with a notably higher multiple pregnancy rate, while miscarriage rates remained comparable between groups. The mechanism underlying this benefit involves GnRH-a's suppression of spontaneous ovulation and premature LH surges, coupled with direct endometrial effects mediated by GnRH receptors that enhance receptivity through modulation of implantation-related molecules and immune-inflammatory pathways. Notably, age modified treatment outcomes, with patients 35 years and older showing the most substantial benefit from GnRH-a + HRT, an important finding given that advanced maternal age and endometrial aging involve loss of critical regulators like H3K27ac and progesterone receptor expression. Obstetric outcomes including gestational diabetes, hypertensive disorders, preterm birth, and fetal malformation rates were comparable between GnRH-a + HRT and HRT groups, indicating safe pregnancy outcomes. These findings suggest that GnRH-a pretreatment offers a clinically meaningful strategy to enhance reproductive success in older women with diminished ovarian reserve.

title: The management of slow ovarian response in PCOS patients and its impact on clinical pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Slow ovarian response (SOR), characterized by retarded follicular growth despite normal ovarian reserve, affects approximately 27% of PCOS patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology and is associated with prolonged treatment cycles, increased gonadotropin requirements, and higher body mass index. Although SOR patients demonstrate reduced oocyte retrieval and lower cumulative pregnancy and live birth rates compared to controls, embryo quality parameters including fertilization rate, transferable embryo rate, and high-quality embryo rate remain comparable between groups. Luteinizing hormone supplementation, particularly low-dose human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) co-administration with follicle-stimulating hormone, effectively improves ovarian response and follicular growth rates in SOR patients, with a follicular growth rate of 1.0 mm/day identified as the diagnostic threshold for SOR classification. hCG supplementation enhances oocyte retrieval and transferable embryo numbers without compromising endometrial development, making timely identification of SOR through assessment of follicular growth rate critical for determining whether to continue stimulation or modify treatment protocols. These findings suggest that targeted hCG supplementation may be an effective strategy to normalize ovarian responsiveness and improve reproductive outcomes in PCOS patients with SOR.

title: Delayed implantation might be an early signal for poor reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

Delayed implantation in assisted reproductive technology is strongly associated with poor reproductive outcomes, including significantly higher rates of biochemical pregnancy, ectopic pregnancy, and reduced ongoing pregnancy rates. This delayed timing correlates with substantially lower human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) levels produced by trophoblast cells on days 8, 10, and 12 post-transfer, as well as impaired hCG rising ratios, indicating compromised embryonic development and hormone production. Key risk factors for delayed implantation include non-preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidies (non-PGT-A) cycles, low-quality embryos, and various endometrial receptivity disorders including endometriosis, fibroids, and polyps, alongside embryo-specific issues such as slow blastulation and aneuploid embryos. Mechanistically, delayed implantation reflects desynchronization between embryo development and endometrial receptivity, where disrupted signaling pathways—such as reduced IGF-1 downregulating PI3K/AKT and triggering trophoblast apoptosis—decrease hCG secretion and embryo viability. Transfer of high-quality embryos and utilization of PGT-A cycles emerge as protective factors against delayed implantation. The study establishes that early hCG kinetics serve as a predictive biomarker for ongoing pregnancy success, with normal hCG rise rates of approximately 50% per day predicting live birth, whereas abnormal trajectories signal impending pregnancy loss or ectopic pregnancy risk.

title: GnRH agonist trigger in focus: does protocol choice between PPOS and antagonist strategies affect outcomes?
summary:
# Summary

This study compared progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) and GnRH antagonist protocols for ovarian stimulation in assisted reproduction, with particular focus on GnRH agonist triggering to prevent ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. While PPOS cycles resulted in shorter stimulation duration and lower progesterone levels on trigger day compared to antagonist cycles, both protocols achieved comparable oocyte yields, embryological outcomes, clinical pregnancy rates (63.6% vs 63.8%), and live birth rates (51.7% vs 52.2%). The PPOS protocol maintains adequate LH surge suppression through progestin-mediated mechanisms that reduce GnRH pulse frequency and suppress pituitary LH reserves, while GnRH antagonists achieve suppression through competitive inhibition at pituitary GnRH receptors. Notably, embryo quality emerged as the only significant predictor of live birth outcomes, independent of stimulation protocol choice, with poor and moderate-quality embryos showing substantially reduced likelihood of implantation success. These findings indicate that protocol selection between PPOS and antagonist strategies does not significantly impact reproductive outcomes when GnRH agonist triggering is employed, making both approaches clinically equivalent options for ovarian stimulation.

title: Endometrial hypoperfusion: the missing link in refractory thin endometrium
summary:
# Summary

Thin endometrium represents a critical challenge in reproductive medicine, fundamentally linked to implantation failure and poor pregnancy outcomes through deficient uterine blood supply. The article identifies endometrial hypoperfusion as the central mechanistic link, demonstrating that thin endometrium is characterized by impaired angiogenesis, reduced microvessel density, tissue hypoxia, and elevated vascular resistance indices on Doppler ultrasound. At the molecular level, thin endometrium exhibits decreased expression of angiogenic factors such as VEGF, β3-integrin, and leukemia inhibitory factor, while simultaneously displaying elevated hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF-1α and HIF-2α), creating a self-perpetuating cycle of vascular insufficiency and hypoxic injury that compromises endometrial growth and receptivity. Therapeutic strategies including vasoactive medications, platelet-rich plasma, and stem cell therapies show promise in improving endometrial thickness by targeting uterine blood supply. Pregnancies that do occur despite thin endometrium face significantly elevated risks for complications including miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, placental malperfusion, and preeclampsia, underscoring the critical role of adequate endometrial vascularization in successful implantation and pregnancy maintenance.

title: Expert transvaginal ultrasound is determinant for diagnosing pelvic conditions after recurrent implantation failure in IVF
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary:
# Summary

Recurrent implantation failure in IVF is a multifactorial condition associated with structural and functional uterine abnormalities including adenomyosis, deep infiltrating endometriosis, myomas, uterine malformations, and hydrosalpinx, as well as systemic factors such as immunological disorders, thrombophilic conditions, endometrial receptivity disorders, and embryo aneuploidy. Expert transvaginal ultrasound, particularly employing three-dimensional and power Doppler imaging modalities, substantially improves detection of pelvic pathologies in patients with recurrent implantation failure, identifying structural abnormalities in 129 of 152 cases compared to only 45 cases detected by standard IVF center ultrasound evaluation. Notable diagnostic discrepancies were found for adenomyosis (8.6% vs 39.5%), uterine malformations (0.7% vs 15.8%), and deep infiltrating endometriosis (6.6% vs 38.8%), while myomas demonstrated the highest diagnostic agreement between imaging centers. Adenomyosis and junctional zone involvement are particularly associated with increased miscarriage rates, and cavity-distorting myomas reduce clinical pregnancy rates, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis. These findings demonstrate that expert transvaginal ultrasound is critical for comprehensive evaluation of recurrent implantation failure cases, enabling detection of previously missed pathologies that may guide targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Effectiveness of preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy in improving clinical and neonatal outcomes for patients with recurrent pregnancy loss
summary: Embryonic chromosomal abnormalities, particularly aneuploidy, are the primary cause of early pregnancy loss, affecting 50–70% of affected embryos in patients with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL). Preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A) significantly improves both clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates across multiple patient populations, with particularly robust benefits in women under 38 years with a history of three or more miscarriages (CPR 74.70% vs. 42.13%) and in those with diminished ovarian reserve. PGT-A also substantially reduces miscarriage rates overall (9.09% vs. 22.06%) and improves the live birth-to-clinical pregnancy ratio. Euploid embryos demonstrate superior morphological quality, higher rates of high-quality blastocysts, and more favorable developmental dynamics, with a predominance of Day 5 blastocysts compared to aneuploid embryos. However, PGT-A efficacy varies by maternal age and miscarriage history, with limited benefit observed in patients with only two previous losses, and the biopsy process itself carries risk of embryo damage through misdiagnosis. Overall, PGT-A represents an effective intervention for improving reproductive outcomes in RPL patients, though patient selection based on age and clinical history optimizes therapeutic benefit.

title: ACCEPT Group Condensed Position Paper on the Management of Recurrent Pregnancy Loss
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

Recurrent pregnancy loss results from multiple distinct etiological pathways involving chromosomal, anatomical, endocrine, immunological, and male factor abnormalities. Aneuploidy, strongly linked to maternal age, is the most common cause of first trimester loss, while parental balanced translocations increase risk of unbalanced embryos; preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-SR) improves live birth rates in couples with chromosomal rearrangements. Uterine structural abnormalities—including Müllerian anomalies, intramural or submucosal fibroids, and intrauterine adhesions—significantly increase pregnancy loss risk and are amenable to hysteroscopic intervention, with lysis of intrauterine adhesions achieving live birth rates up to 71%. Endocrine factors such as thyroid disease (where levothyroxine reduces loss risk by up to 81%) and obesity substantially contribute to RPL, while acquired thrombophilias, particularly antiphospholipid syndrome with anti-cardiolipin antibodies, warrant anticoagulation with aspirin and heparin. Male factor contributions including obesity, smoking, and sperm DNA fragmentation are also significant, with lifestyle modification and advanced sperm selection techniques offering potential benefit.

title: Preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy versus no genetic testing in couples undergoing intracytoplasmic sperm injection for severe male infertility: multicentre, open label, randomised controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Severe male factor infertility, characterized by oligozoospermia, asthenozoospermia, teratozoospermia, or azoospermia, is strongly associated with elevated chromosomal abnormalities and aneuploidy rates (42.6%) in embryos. This multicenter randomized controlled trial evaluated whether preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A) combined with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) could improve reproductive outcomes compared to ICSI alone. PGT-A substantially reduced pregnancy loss across multiple categories—including biochemical pregnancy loss, first trimester loss, and total pregnancy loss at 12 months—and increased both implantation rates per embryo and cumulative live birth rates through multiple transfers. However, despite these improvements in embryo quality and early pregnancy viability, PGT-A did not translate into a significantly higher live birth rate per woman after the initial transfer, suggesting that benefits plateau when limited numbers of euploid embryos are available. These findings indicate that while PGT-A effectively selects chromosomally normal embryos with better early developmental potential, additional factors beyond aneuploidy—including morphokinetic patterns, gene expression, and cellular mechanisms—influence ultimate reproductive success in couples with severe male infertility.

title: Partner Ethnicity and Assisted Reproductive Technology Outcomes: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that partner ethnicity is independently associated with assisted reproductive technology outcomes, with non-White partners showing significantly reduced clinical pregnancy rates (5% lower), live birth rates (6% lower), and biochemical pregnancy rates (5% lower) compared to White partners. The disparities are particularly pronounced in same-ethnicity couples, with Black couples experiencing 24% lower live birth rates and Asian couples experiencing 42% lower live birth rates relative to White couples. These ethnic disparities appear to be mediated partly by differences in paternal factors, including advanced paternal age among Black partners (50.3% ≥40 years versus 34.5% in White partners) and documented reductions in semen parameters such as sperm concentration, motility, and DNA fragmentation in Black and Asian men. Additionally, female factors differed by ethnicity, with higher rates of ovulatory disorders and tubal disease among Black women, conditions associated with reduced live birth rates. The study also found lower utilization of assisted reproductive techniques such as preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy and elective single embryo transfer in non-White couples, factors that may further contribute to observed disparities in reproductive outcomes.

title: Pregnancy and perinatal outcomes after day 5 versus day 6 blastocyst‐stage embryo transfer: A systematic review and meta‐analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that day 5 (D5) blastocyst transfer significantly improves reproductive outcomes compared to day 6 (D6) transfer across multiple parameters. D5 blastocyst transfers yielded higher human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) positive rates, clinical pregnancy rates, ongoing pregnancy rates, and live birth rates, while concurrently reducing miscarriage rates by approximately 22%. These benefits were consistent across both single frozen blastocyst transfer (SFBT) cohorts with and without preimplantation genetic testing (PGT). Blastocyst morphological quality emerged as an important determinant, with lower morphological scores associated with reduced clinical pregnancy and live birth rates. Notably, perinatal outcomes including birth weight, gestational age, preterm birth rates, and sex ratios showed no significant differences between D5 and D6 transfers, indicating that earlier transfer timing does not compromise neonatal safety. The superior pregnancy outcomes with D5 transfer likely reflect better embryonic viability and developmental synchronization with endometrial receptivity, though blastocyst quality remains a critical factor modulating these benefits.

title: Live birth outcomes after euploid transfer: autologous vs. donor oocyte embryos in patients aged > 35 years
summary: This study compared live birth outcomes following euploid frozen embryo transfer in women over 35 years, contrasting autologous versus donor oocyte cycles. Maternal age at oocyte retrieval emerged as the primary determinant of live birth success, with autologous live birth rates declining from 55.8% (ages 35–37) to 43.4% (ages ≥45), whereas donor oocyte cycles maintained consistently higher rates independent of recipient age, demonstrating that oocyte age rather than uterine age drives age-related reproductive decline. The age-dependent decline in autologous cycles reflects both increased embryonic factors (aneuploidy, poor morphology, delayed blastulation) and oocyte-specific factors including compromised cytoplasmic competency, epigenetic modifications, and age-related ooplasmic defects that persist even in chromosomally normal embryos. Notably, women under 38 years requiring donor oocytes showed paradoxically lower live birth rates, likely indicating underlying poor oocyte quality or premature ovarian insufficiency affecting the uterine environment. These findings establish that among euploid transfers, chronological oocyte age fundamentally limits reproductive success through mechanisms beyond aneuploidy detection, with endometrial factors playing a secondary role in age-associated fertility decline.

title: Serum human chorionic gonadotropin can predict live birth 7 days after frozen-thawed blastocyst transfer
summary: This study establishes that serum human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) measured as early as day 7 after frozen-thawed blastocyst transfer can reliably predict live birth outcomes, with an optimal cutoff value of 31.05 mIU/mL achieving 96.1% sensitivity and 85.5% specificity. Multiple maternal and embryonic factors significantly influence live birth rates, including younger maternal age, absence of prior cesarean delivery, first-time frozen embryo transfer, and transfer of day 5 blastocysts with grade A trophectoderm and inner cell mass quality. The study developed specific hCG cutoff values for days 7 through 11 post-transfer, with day 10 showing the highest specificity (95.9% sensitivity, 85.9% specificity at 95.4 mIU/mL), enabling early and accurate prediction of pregnancy success. Prior cesarean delivery emerged as a significant negative predictor, suggesting that uterine history impacts endometrial receptivity and implantation outcomes. Notably, endometriosis was not identified as a significant differentiating factor in infertility causes among the study population. These findings provide clinicians with quantitative hCG thresholds to counsel patients on pregnancy viability within the first two weeks after blastocyst transfer.

title: Low-dose letrozole-HMG regimen reverses letrozole-induced endometrial impairment and improves frozen embryo transfer outcomes
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Letrozole monotherapy, while effective for ovulation induction, impairs endometrial receptivity through systemic estrogen suppression, resulting in thinner endometrium, reduced vascularization, and downregulation of cell adhesion and inflammatory pathways critical for embryo implantation. The addition of human menopausal gonadotropin (HMG) to letrozole, termed the LeH regimen, reverses these adverse effects by maintaining normal endometrial thickness, promoting pinopode development, and upregulating key adhesion molecules (CD6, CDH1, NCAM1, SDC3) and immune-regulatory proteins. Proteomic and bioinformatic analyses demonstrate that the LeH regimen restores cytokine-cytokine receptor interactions and activates anti-inflammatory pathways involving CCL11, CXCL1, CXCL5, IL1B, and IL4, creating a supportive local and systemic immune environment for implantation. Clinically, patients receiving the LeH regimen achieved significantly higher implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to letrozole monotherapy in frozen embryo transfer cycles. These findings establish that HMG co-administration effectively counteracts letrozole-induced endometrial impairment through multi-faceted mechanisms encompassing structural normalization, molecular pathway activation, and immune modulation, offering a superior treatment strategy for patients requiring ovulation induction before embryo transfer.

title: A prospective cohort study on progestin-primed ovarian stimulation vs. GnRH antagonist in IVF-ICSI cycles: effects on clinical pregnancy and neonatal outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study compared progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) with GnRH antagonist protocols in IVF-ICSI cycles, demonstrating that PPOS achieves superior embryological outcomes while maintaining equivalent reproductive and neonatal safety. The PPOS protocol, which uses medroxyprogesterone acetate to inhibit premature luteinizing hormone surges and the positive feedback loop of GnRH neurons, produced significantly higher numbers of mature oocytes, high-quality embryos, and blastocysts compared to the GnRH antagonist approach. Notably, PPOS showed higher initial implantation and clinical pregnancy rates (43.4% vs. 31.9%) and was associated with lower medication costs and fewer side effects. After propensity score matching of 1,950 patients per group, however, the two protocols yielded equivalent clinical pregnancy rates (46.26%), live birth rates, miscarriage rates, and comprehensive neonatal outcomes including birth weight and congenital anomaly incidence. These findings support PPOS as an effective and economical alternative to conventional GnRH antagonist protocols, particularly beneficial for patients with polycystic ovary syndrome or elevated body mass index, with reduced risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome.

title: Live birth in patients stimulated with r-hFSH or r-hFSH: r-hLH is strongly associated with cumulus cell derived gene expression models
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cumulus cell gene expression profiles serve as robust predictors of live birth outcomes in assisted reproductive technology, with the predictive biomarkers varying significantly based on the ovarian stimulation hormone protocol used. Cumulus cells, which surround and support oocytes during maturation, exhibit distinct gene expression patterns when patients are stimulated with recombinant human FSH alone (r-hFSH) versus FSH combined with LH (r-hFSH:r-hLH), with genes such as GOT1, HAS2, SASH1, and PTGS2 showing the strongest associations with successful live birth in r-hFSH stimulated patients, while GOT1 and HAS2 together achieved 88% accuracy for r-hFSH:r-hLH patients. The identified biomarkers are functionally involved in critical reproductive processes including metabolic function, cell signaling, prostaglandin synthesis, and extracellular matrix remodeling. A major clinical advantage of cumulus cell testing is that it represents a non-invasive approach using material normally discarded during oocyte processing, allowing fresh embryo transfer within one day without the need for blastocyst vitrification required by other selection methods. This cumulus cell-based test significantly improves live birth rates per transfer and reduces time-to-pregnancy compared to conventional embryo selection approaches, offering patients a less burdensome alternative to invasive genetic testing methods.

title: Effect of progesterone levels on the hCG trigger day and the progesterone-to-basal progesterone ratio on pregnancy outcomes in fresh IVF/ICSI cycles with GnRH antagonist protocol: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Progesterone is essential for endometrial receptivity and pregnancy success through multiple mechanisms, including downregulation of estrogen receptor-α to suppress oxytocin receptor expression, promotion of uterine smooth muscle quiescence, and modulation of vascular signaling pathways (VEGF-A–VEGFR2 and angiopoietin-2) critical for maternal-fetal blood flow. In fresh IVF/ICSI cycles using GnRH antagonist protocols, elevated progesterone levels on the human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) trigger day significantly impair pregnancy outcomes, with serum progesterone levels ≥1.06 ng/mL and progesterone-to-basal progesterone ratios ≥1.2 independently reducing clinical pregnancy and live birth rates. Conversely, patients achieving progesterone levels <1.06 ng/mL with a progesterone-to-basal ratio <1.2 demonstrated the highest success rates, with live birth rates of 43.6% and early miscarriage rates of 10.6%, substantially outperforming other progesterone cohorts. Women who achieved live birth were younger with superior ovarian reserve markers (higher AMH, more antral follicles), required lower gonadotropin doses, and notably had both lower trigger-day progesterone levels and thicker endometrial thickness. These findings establish that maintaining lower progesterone levels on the trigger day, reflected by a low progesterone-to-basal ratio, is a critical predictor of successful fertility outcomes in antagonist-based ovarian stimulation.

title: Case Report: Immediate vs. delayed azithromycin for chronic endometritis: a retrospective cohort study on cure rates and pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis (CE) is a persistent inflammatory condition caused by intrauterine microbial infection from organisms including Escherichia coli, Enterococcus faecalis, Mycoplasma, and Ureaplasma that impairs endometrial receptivity and disrupts the local immune microenvironment, leading to infertility, repeated implantation failure, and recurrent pregnancy loss. Azithromycin, a macrolide antibiotic with high tissue bioavailability and penetration, inhibits bacterial protein synthesis at the 50S ribosomal subunit and effectively treats CE with cure rates of 88.40% in immediate treatment and 93.63% in delayed treatment groups. The diagnosis of CE relies on immunohistochemical detection of CD138+ plasma cells in endometrial biopsies, with elevated counts during the proliferative phase. While immediate azithromycin treatment significantly reduces the time to embryo transfer and accelerates assisted reproductive technology processes, both immediate and delayed treatment approaches yielded comparable pregnancy outcomes, with no significant differences in biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, or ongoing pregnancy rates between groups. The study demonstrates that azithromycin provides an effective alternative to doxycycline for CE management and underscores the clinical importance of treating CE to restore endometrial function and optimize fertility outcomes in patients undergoing in vitro fertilization.

title: Double vitrification-warming cycles coupled with one blastocyst biopsy do not affect the chance of live birth after an euploid blastocyst transfer: a propensity score matching study
summary: This study demonstrates that performing preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidies (PGT-A) with a second vitrification-warming cycle on remaining embryos does not compromise reproductive outcomes compared to standard PGT-A protocols. PGT-A effectively reduces aneuploidy rates and early pregnancy losses by identifying and selecting euploid embryos for transfer, while enhancing clinical pregnancy and live birth rates in patients with repeated pregnancy loss, repeated implantation failure, or chromosomal abnormalities. The comparison between double vitrification-warming cycles with trophoblast biopsy (TBR group) and standard single vitrification with single biopsy (SVSB group) revealed no statistically significant differences in pregnancy outcomes, neonatal outcomes, or live birth rates, despite the TBR group achieving a lower aneuploidy rate and higher euploidy rate. Blastocyst-stage biopsy proved less detrimental to developmental potential than earlier zygote-stage sampling, and the survival rate of thawed blastocysts remained uncompromised after two vitrification cycles. These findings suggest that the additional embryo handling involved in double vitrification-warming cycles coupled with blastocyst biopsy is not detrimental, offering flexibility in PGT-A protocols for reproductive outcomes in patients requiring genetic screening.

title: Melatonin supplementation and outcomes of assisted reproductive technology: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that melatonin improves multiple parameters of oocyte and embryo quality during assisted reproductive technology, primarily through its potent antioxidant mechanisms. Melatonin, a hormone secreted by the pineal gland, downregulates reactive oxygen species and upregulates antioxidant enzymes, counteracting the oxidative stress induced by controlled ovarian stimulation and in vitro culture procedures. Melatonin supplementation significantly increased clinical pregnancy rates, MII oocyte numbers, top-quality embryo counts, and fertilization rates compared to placebo or no treatment. However, these benefits on embryo quality did not translate to significant improvements in live birth rates or final pregnancy outcomes overall. Notably, melatonin demonstrated selective efficacy in women with polycystic ovary syndrome or decreased ovarian reserve, improving oocyte quality in these populations, but showed no significant clinical pregnancy benefit across subgroups including those with PCOS, normal ovarian function, or prior poor fertilization histories. These findings suggest melatonin's antioxidant effects enhance gamete and embryo development, yet additional mechanisms may be necessary to improve ultimate reproductive success in assisted reproductive technology.

title: A comparison of progesterone via vaginal oil capsules versus pessaries for luteal phase support in assisted reproduction treatment: a multicentre cohort study of 42 291 cycles
summary: This large multicentre cohort study of 42,291 assisted reproductive treatment cycles demonstrates that vaginal oil capsules (Utrogestan) provide superior luteal phase support compared to pessaries (Cyclogest) across fresh IVF/ICSI and frozen embryo transfer cycles. Exogenous progesterone is essential during ART because ovarian stimulation causes supra-physiological estrogen levels that suppress endogenous progesterone production, while frozen cycles lack progesterone production entirely due to absence of a corpus luteum. Utrogestan achieved significantly higher clinical pregnancy rates (8% increase in fresh cycles, 7% in frozen cycles), higher live birth rates (11% and 9% increases respectively), and lower miscarriage rates (13% reduction in frozen cycles) compared to Cyclogest. These superior outcomes with Utrogestan are likely explained by differences in pharmacodynamics, including more stable plasma progesterone levels and greater bioavailability at the tissue level through the first uterine pass effect. The findings are particularly relevant for women with endometriosis or adenomyosis, who exhibit progesterone resistance and require higher serum progesterone thresholds for successful implantation and pregnancy maintenance.

title: Administration of ethiodized poppy seed oil-based contrast agent into the uterus enhances fertilization rate in mice inseminated with low sperm numbers
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that ethiodized poppy seed oil-based contrast media (OSCM), such as Lipiodol, significantly enhances fertility outcomes when administered into the uterus, particularly in conditions of low sperm count. In mice inseminated with suboptimal sperm numbers, Lipiodol administration increased fertilization rates, two-cell embryo production by threefold, and blastocyst development, resulting in a 4.1-fold larger average litter size compared to controls. The mechanism appears to involve multiple pathways: Lipiodol inhibits sperm phagocytosis by macrophages while modulating dendritic cell populations, and the iodine component supports thyroid hormone production, which regulates ovarian follicle growth and ovulation. These findings align with clinical evidence showing that hysterosalpingography with oil-based contrast media improves pregnancy and live birth rates compared to water-soluble alternatives, suggesting Lipiodol's pro-fertility effects work through both uterine and sperm-related mechanisms without compromising endometrial integrity.

title: Impact of Physical Activity on Ovarian Response: A Prospective Study Among In Vitro Fertilization Patients
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study examined the relationship between physical activity levels and ovarian response in in vitro fertilization patients. The findings demonstrate that regular physical activity is associated with improved clinical pregnancy rates and, in specific patient subgroups, enhanced oocyte retrieval and maturation. Notably, among patients with endometriosis or ovulatory disorders, high physical activity levels correlated with significantly greater numbers of mature oocytes compared to sedentary individuals. Conversely, a sedentary lifestyle increases obesity risk, which impairs ovarian function through multiple mechanisms including menstrual and ovulatory disorders, reduced oocyte quality, and diminished pregnancy rates. The study suggests that while moderate physical activity benefits metabolic health by improving insulin sensitivity and body weight control, extreme elite-level sport may paradoxically induce neuroendocrine and reproductive dysfunction. These findings highlight physical activity as a modifiable factor that may optimize reproductive outcomes, particularly in women with endometriosis or metabolic disorders, potentially by counteracting the proinflammatory and oxidative stress environment associated with reduced oocyte competence.

title: Clinical outcomes following GnRH antagonist protocol change to PPOS protocol versus GnRH antagonist protocol in patients with pre-elevated progesterone: a propensity score matching study
summary:
# Summary

This propensity-score matched study compared clinical outcomes between patients who switched from a GnRH antagonist protocol to a progesterone-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocol versus those who remained on the antagonist protocol, specifically in cases with elevated progesterone (≥0.9 ng/mL). The primary mechanism underlying the protocol switch addresses a critical problem: elevated progesterone during the antagonist cycle causes premature luteinization and prematurely exposes the endometrium to high progesterone levels, which damages endometrial receptivity and necessitates embryo freezing rather than fresh transfer. The antagonist-to-PPOS group achieved significantly superior embryo outcomes, producing more blastocysts (4.52 vs. 3.73), higher-quality blastocysts per person (3.28 vs. 2.45), and higher blastocyst formation rates (62.50% vs. 50.00%), despite retrieving slightly fewer oocytes. Importantly, clinical pregnancy rates in frozen embryo transfer cycles were comparable between groups, indicating that switching to PPOS did not compromise reproductive outcomes while improving embryo quality. The PPOS protocol's addition of progesterone in the early follicular phase suppresses the LH surge and prevents spontaneous ovulation, offering an economically advantageous alternative to antagonists while enabling better embryo development through higher LH levels on the hCG trigger day.

title: Effects of different ovulation induction protocols on pregnancy outcomes of fresh cycles in patients undergoing in vitro fertilization-embryo transfer with donor sperm
summary:
# Summary

This study compared three ovulation induction protocols for in vitro fertilization with donor sperm, examining their effects on pregnancy outcomes. The GnRH-agonist long protocol demonstrated superior clinical pregnancy and live birth rates compared to the GnRH antagonist protocol, which required the lowest gonadotropin doses but produced fewer eggs, lower fertilization rates, and thinner endometrial thickness on the day of human chorionic gonadotropin administration. GnRH-agonist protocols prevented premature luteinizing hormone elevation and reduced cycle cancellations, while the antagonist protocol lowered the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. Key clinical predictors of pregnancy success included thicker endometrium, higher numbers of high-quality embryos transferred, and younger patient age, with advanced age increasing miscarriage risk. These findings suggest that while GnRH antagonist protocols offer efficiency in medication use, GnRH-agonist long protocols may provide better reproductive outcomes for patients undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: A Proposed Model of a Pragmatic Surgical Approach in Women Affected by Uterine Fibroids Undergoing IVF: A “Real Practice” Experience
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids significantly impair fertility through multiple mechanisms, with estrogen and progesterone driving fibroid growth and submucosal or intramural fibroids compromising implantation by distorting the uterine cavity, triggering local inflammation, and reducing endometrial blood supply. The study demonstrates that surgical intervention through hysteroscopic myomectomy for submucosal fibroids and laparotomic myomectomy for intramural fibroids substantially improves reproductive outcomes in women undergoing IVF, with myomectomy increasing implantation rates (41% and 31% respectively versus 12.9% in untreated controls), clinical pregnancy rates (35.2% and 28.1%), and live birth rates while reducing miscarriage risk. Fibroid characteristics including size (≥3 cm), number, and FIGO classification type 3 correlate with poorer IVF outcomes, with larger fibroids (>30 cm³) causing focal deformation and vascular compression while multiple fibroids produce global myometrial dysfunction. The pragmatic surgical approach presented establishes a framework for fibroid management prior to fertility treatment, with laparoscopic myomectomy considered the gold standard for most cases and open myomectomy reserved for complex scenarios. Emerging pharmacological agents including ulipristal acetate, vitamin D, and epigallocatechin gallate are under investigation as adjunctive treatments. This evidence-based strategy optimizes the endometrial environment and uterine function to enhance reproductive success in fibroid-affected women pursuing assisted reproduction.

title: The impact of operative hysteroscopy on pregnancy outcomes in patients with suspected uterine cavity lesions prior to the first frozen–thawed embryo transfer: a retrospective propensity-score matching cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Uterine cavity lesions significantly impair endometrial receptivity and implantation success, with nearly half of women experiencing recurrent implantation failure harboring such abnormalities. This study demonstrates that operative hysteroscopy, the gold standard for diagnosing and treating uterine pathologies including endometrial polyps, intrauterine adhesions, uterine septa, and scar diverticula, substantially improves reproductive outcomes in patients undergoing frozen-thawed embryo transfer. Using propensity-score matching to control for confounding variables, operative hysteroscopy increased clinical pregnancy rates by 54.3% and live birth rates by 44.2% compared to the control group (65.03% vs. 54.90% clinical pregnancy; 52.79% vs. 44.40% live birth). The hysteroscopy group also demonstrated higher biochemical pregnancy rates, indicating improved early embryonic development after treatment of intrauterine lesions. Adjunctive therapies including doxycycline for chronic endometritis and hormonal support with Femoston for adhesions and septa were employed to optimize endometrial conditions following hysteroscopic surgery. These findings support the clinical utility of pretreatment operative hysteroscopy in infertile patients with suspected uterine cavity abnormalities prior to assisted reproductive technology procedures.

title: Effect of grafting site on reproductive outcomes following cryopreserved ovarian tissue transplantation: a meta-analysis and systematic review
summary: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation and transplantation represents a critical fertility preservation method, particularly for prepubertal patients and those unable to delay cancer treatment, as it uniquely bypasses the need for ovarian stimulation or oocyte retrieval. Upon transplantation, the grafted tissue can restore reproductive endocrine function and enable natural conception; however, over fifty percent of follicles are lost during initial hypoxic-ischemic injury following revascularization. The transplantation site significantly influences follicle survival and reproductive outcomes, with orthotopic transplantation (to the remaining ovary or pelvic peritoneum) yielding substantially higher live birth rates than heterotopic approaches. Among orthotopic sites, transplantation to the remaining ovary produces superior live birth rates compared to pelvic peritoneal grafting, making it the preferred location for women seeking to preserve fertility. This meta-analysis demonstrates that grafting site selection is a critical determinant of success in ovarian tissue transplantation, with the remaining ovary emerging as the optimal target for maximizing reproductive potential in cancer survivors and other patients requiring fertility preservation.

title: Live Birth Outcomes After Extended or Repeated High‐Dose Medroxyprogesterone Acetate Therapy for Fertility‐Sparing Management of Endometrial Neoplasia: A Single‐Center Retrospective Case Series
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

High-dose medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) therapy effectively treats atypical endometrial hyperplasia and early-stage endometrioid carcinoma as a fertility-sparing alternative to hysterectomy, achieving complete response rates of 70-80% with subsequent live births in approximately one-third of patients who achieve remission within six months. However, extended or repeated MPA therapy significantly reduces live birth rates compared to standard therapy, with only 8-17% achieving pregnancy outcomes versus 33% in the standard group, likely due to impaired endometrial receptivity from frequent curettage and intrinsic progestin resistance mechanisms such as PI3K/AKT dysregulation or mismatch repair deficiency. Patient factors critically influencing reproductive success include prompt conception desire at initial consultation and achievement of complete response within six months, with median conception occurring 12 months after treatment remission. Successful fertility-sparing management requires careful patient selection, strict adherence to surveillance protocols with hysteroscopic monitoring at three and six months, willingness to accept definitive surgery if needed, and timely conception attempts. These findings underscore that while extended or repeated MPA therapy occasionally permits live birth, outcomes are substantially superior with early treatment response and rapid progression to conception.
