title: Real‐World Effectiveness and Safety of Recombinant Human Follicle‐Stimulating Hormone (rhFSH) in Infertile Women undergoing Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART): A Korean Nationwide Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This Korean nationwide cohort study evaluated the real-world effectiveness and safety of recombinant human follicle-stimulating hormone (rhFSH) preparations in infertile women undergoing assisted reproductive technology, analyzing 10,700 fresh IVF cycles. Follitropin demonstrated superior oocyte retrieval rates, with women receiving follitropin significantly more likely to retrieve ≥11 oocytes while paradoxically experiencing lower rates of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS), despite the well-established correlation between oocyte yield and OHSS risk. The live birth rate achieved 38.8%, with clinical pregnancy rates of 47.3% and no significant differences in pregnancy outcomes compared to other rhFSH preparations. GnRH antagonist protocols, which were more frequently used in the follitropin group, were associated with both higher oocyte retrieval and lower OHSS incidence, suggesting additive safety benefits. Follitropin achieved these outcomes with lower gonadotropin dosages than alternative rhFSH formulations, establishing an improved risk-benefit profile for ART treatment. These findings provide evidence supporting follitropin's effectiveness and favorable safety profile while demonstrating that different rhFSH preparations yield comparable overall ART outcomes in appropriately stratified patient populations.

title: Feasibility and efficacy of random-start progestin-primed ovarian stimulation: a multicenter pilot randomized controlled trial in oocyte donation
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter pilot randomized controlled trial evaluated the feasibility and efficacy of progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) initiated at different phases of the menstrual cycle for oocyte donation. Oocyte donors received corifollitropin alfa combined with oral desogestrel to suppress the luteinizing hormone surge, with final maturation triggered by triptorelin acetate. The study demonstrated that initiating PPOS at any menstrual cycle phase—early follicular, mid-follicular, late follicular, ovulatory, or luteal—produced comparable and non-inferior oocyte retrieval outcomes, with median yields ranging from 10-20 oocytes depending on cycle timing. While late follicular and ovulatory phase groups required fewer additional follitropin beta doses, all groups achieved similar stimulation duration and high donor satisfaction scores. The random-start PPOS protocol proved safe and effective, with minimal adverse events and potential benefits for improving donor convenience and clinical workflow in oocyte donation programs.

title: Stem cell therapy for intrauterine adhesions: a systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine adhesions (IUA), including Asherman's Syndrome, represent a significant cause of female infertility, often resulting from endometrial damage caused by surgical procedures such as dilatation and curettage, infections, or pregnancy-related complications. This meta-analysis demonstrates that stem cell therapy, particularly autologous approaches using menstrual blood-derived stem cells, bone marrow-derived cells, or adipose-derived stromal vascular fraction combined with scaffold matrices, significantly improves reproductive outcomes including cumulative live birth rates (0.40), clinical pregnancy rates (OR = 3.13), biochemical pregnancy rates (0.61), and implantation rates. The therapeutic benefits of mesenchymal stem cells derive from their remarkable capacity for self-renewal and their immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, pro-angiogenic, and anti-fibrotic properties, which facilitate both endometrial repair and regeneration of endometrial thickness. Notably, autologous cells with scaffold matrices demonstrated the highest live birth rates (0.57) and clinical pregnancy rates (0.62) compared to other stem cell interventions, while controlled trials showed stem cell therapy significantly improved live birth outcomes (OR = 2.25) compared to conventional treatments like hysteroscopic surgery and hormonal therapy alone. Emerging cell-free therapies such as mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles show additional promise, though stem cell interventions were associated with early spontaneous abortions (0.17) and ectopic pregnancies (0.06) as potential complications requiring clinical consideration.

title: Effect of the timing of surgical treatment of hydrosalpinx on in vitro fertilization/intracytoplasmic single-sperm injection pregnancy outcomes in patients with tubal factor infertility
summary:
# Summary

Hydrosalpinx causes tubal factor infertility through multiple mechanisms, including direct toxic effects on embryos, mechanical washing of the endometrium that reduces endometrial receptivity, and compression of ovarian blood vessels that impairs ovarian blood supply, thereby creating a pelvic inflammatory environment hostile to implantation. Laparoscopic salpingectomy effectively treats hydrosalpinx and improves clinical pregnancy rates; however, the timing of surgery relative to assisted reproductive technology cycles significantly impacts outcomes and costs. This study compared two treatment sequences: Group A underwent oocyte retrieval followed by embryo freezing and delayed salpingectomy before frozen embryo transfer, while Group B underwent salpingectomy first followed by controlled ovarian hyperstimulation and fresh embryo transfer. Although live birth rates were comparable between groups (57.9% versus 64.7%), Group B demonstrated substantially lower time and economic costs, fewer postoperative complications, and lower premature delivery rates, suggesting that performing salpingectomy prior to ovarian stimulation may be the more efficient treatment strategy. These findings indicate that timing of hydrosalpinx surgery should be carefully considered in infertility management to optimize both clinical outcomes and resource utilization.

title: Efficacy of Stop GnRH-Agonist/Antagonist versus GnRH-Antagonist
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates the Stop GnRH-agonist/GnRH-antagonist protocol as an alternative controlled ovarian stimulation approach for polycystic ovary syndrome patients undergoing IVF, a population at elevated risk for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome due to their increased luteinizing hormone levels and androgen production. The Stop protocol involves initial GnRH agonist pretreatment to downregulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and improve ovarian synchronization, followed by GnRH antagonist administration during stimulation, which stabilizes hormonal levels and reduces premature LH surges. The combined sequential approach produced comparable oocyte yields and embryo quality to conventional long GnRH-agonist protocols while maintaining flexibility in final follicular maturation triggers through dual trigger mechanisms. Although the Stop protocol required slightly longer stimulation duration, it did not increase total gonadotropin requirements and demonstrated enhanced endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation rates without significantly increasing OHSS incidence. These findings suggest the Stop GnRH-agonist/GnRH-antagonist protocol offers a viable, cost-effective alternative for PCOS patients, providing improved safety and reproductive outcomes compared to traditional approaches.

title: Pregnancy Outcome following Administration of Intravenous
summary:
# Summary

This study examines intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) therapy as a treatment for recurrent implantation failure (RIF) caused by immune dysregulation. The research demonstrates that immune dysfunction—characterized by disruptions in normal inflammatory responses—contributes to implantation failure, miscarriage, and other adverse pregnancy outcomes, while a T helper cell type 2 (Th2)-dominant immune response is essential for successful pregnancy. IVIg functions as an immunomodulatory agent through multiple mechanisms: it neutralizes pro-inflammatory cytokines and pathogenic autoantibodies, reduces Th1 cell activity to rebalance the Th1/Th2 ratio, enhances regulatory T cells (Tregs) that establish maternal-fetal tolerance, and inhibits natural killer cell activity and antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity. The study found that women receiving IVIg therapy showed significantly higher biochemical pregnancy rates compared to controls (38.2% versus 19%), with additional benefits including improved cumulative pregnancy rates and live birth rates, alongside reductions in implantation failures and miscarriages. These results suggest that IVIg treatment may substantially improve pregnancy outcomes in women with unexplained RIF by restoring appropriate immune balance necessary for successful implantation and fetal development.

title: Comparative efficacy of varicocelectomy and intrauterine insemination in varicocoele patients with mild semen abnormalities: An observational study
summary:
# Summary

Varicocele causes male infertility by impairing spermatogenesis through increased testicular temperature, venous stasis, and oxidative stress. This observational study compared microsurgical subinguinal varicocelectomy (MSV) with intrauterine insemination (IUI) in men with mild semen abnormalities, finding that MSV significantly improved multiple semen parameters including sperm concentration, motility, total motile sperm count, and DNA fragmentation index, while IUI did not enhance semen quality. MSV patients achieved substantially higher clinical pregnancy rates (35.23%) and live birth rates (31.75%) compared to the IUI group, with multivariate analysis confirming that varicocelectomy independently improved both outcomes relative to IUI. Secondary infertility status, lower female age, longer seminal volume, and shorter infertility duration were associated with better reproductive outcomes in both treatment groups. These findings demonstrate that surgical correction of varicocele offers superior long-term reproductive benefits compared to assisted reproductive techniques in men with mild semen abnormalities.

title: Zishen Yutai Pill increased live births in advanced maternal age women: a randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

Advanced maternal age impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms including diminished ovarian reserve, reduced endometrial receptivity, and altered hormonal profiles, all of which contribute to decreased live birth rates during assisted reproductive technology. In a randomized clinical trial, Zishen Yutai Pill (ZYP), a traditional herbal supplement containing constituents like Panax ginseng and Lycium barbarum, significantly improved live birth rates in women aged 35–42 undergoing IVF, increasing the rate from 19.0% in the placebo group to 23.3% in the treatment group. The pill enhanced reproductive outcomes by upregulating key implantation markers (HOXA10, ICAM1, and SPP1) to improve endometrial receptivity and by upregulating oocyte maturation factors (BMP15 and GDF9) to improve embryo quality. Secondary outcomes confirmed that ZYP increased biochemical pregnancy, implantation, and clinical pregnancy rates without increasing pregnancy loss or safety complications including ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, ectopic pregnancy, or adverse fetal/neonatal events. These findings support the therapeutic potential of ZYP as an adjunctive treatment to enhance reproductive outcomes in older women with age-related fertility decline.

title: Comparing ovulation induction strategies for unexplained infertility: protocol for a systematic review and network meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and network meta-analysis protocol compares ovulation induction strategies for treating unexplained infertility, a condition affecting approximately 30% of infertile couples despite normal ovulation, tubal patency, and semen parameters. The three primary pharmacological approaches—letrozole, clomiphene citrate, and gonadotropins (including recombinant FSH, urinary FSH, and hMG)—are all used alone or in combination with intrauterine insemination to improve live birth rates. While clomiphene citrate and gonadotropins effectively induce ovulation and increase pregnancy rates, clomiphene citrate impairs endometrial receptivity, whereas letrozole appears to avoid this adverse effect. Gonadotropins, though effective at improving pregnancy outcomes, carry higher risks of multiple gestation and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. The review will employ component network meta-analysis to estimate the marginal effects of individual pharmacological and procedural components across treatment regimens, with primary outcomes of live birth and secondary outcomes including miscarriage, multiple pregnancy, and ectopic pregnancy. This novel analytical approach will provide comprehensive evidence comparing different ovulation induction strategies to guide clinical decision-making in unexplained infertility management.

title: Impact of endometrial thickness before frozen-warmed blastocyst transfer on live birth rate in women with endometriosis
summary: This study investigated how endometrial thickness affects reproductive outcomes in women with endometriosis undergoing frozen-warmed blastocyst transfer. Endometrial thickness is directly associated with endometrial receptivity and clinical outcomes; thin endometrium significantly reduces embryo implantation and live birth rates, while endometrial thickness below 6 mm warrants investigation for underlying pathology such as intrauterine adhesions or Asherman syndrome. Women with endometriosis exhibit impaired endometrial receptivity driven by molecular alterations, immune dysregulation, chronic inflammation, progesterone resistance, and estrogen dominance, often complicated by coexisting adenomyosis, chronic endometritis, and endometrial polyps that further compromise implantation potential. Notably, the optimal endometrial thickness for live birth differs between groups: peak live birth rates occurred at 8 mm in women without endometriosis but at 10 mm in women with endometriosis, with significantly reduced outcomes in women with endometriosis having 8–10 mm thickness. Natural-cycle frozen embryo transfer, supported by corpus luteum presence, may improve outcomes by reducing pregnancy complications including early loss, preterm birth, and hypertensive disorders. The findings suggest that while endometrial thickness above 6 mm shows no specific threshold for live birth in women with endometriosis, careful endometrial assessment and appropriate cycle selection remain critical for optimizing reproductive success in this population.

title: Usefulness of embryo evaluation via artificial intelligence–based image analysis
summary: This study evaluates Life Whisperer (LW), an artificial intelligence-based image analysis system, for objective embryo selection in assisted reproductive technology. The LW score, a quantitative measure ranging from 0 to 10, demonstrates a strong positive association with fetal heartbeat presence and clinical pregnancy outcomes, with higher scores indicating greater viability potential. Embryos classified in the excellent LW group (scores 9.0–10) showed significantly higher rates of positive fetal heartbeat (64.3%) compared to the poor group (27.6%), demonstrating clear prognostic stratification. The key advantage of LW over traditional Gardner classification is its high reproducibility and objectivity through automated quantitative scoring, eliminating the interobserver variability inherent in subjective morphological grading. These findings establish LW as a clinically useful tool that enables selection of embryos with higher potential for successful pregnancy and live birth, providing reproducible and quantifiable assessment from a single blastocyst image.

title: Exploring determinants of cumulative live birth rates in IVF: insights from the EELI study in Lebanon
summary:
# Endometriosis Reproductive Outcomes and Endometrial Function

The EELI study in Lebanon identified three major factors significantly influencing cumulative live birth rates in IVF: maternal age, oocyte yield, and embryo transfer stage. Maternal age demonstrated an inverse relationship with success, with women aged 35 years and older showing substantially reduced odds of live birth, a pattern similarly observed with paternal age in men over 45. Oocyte retrieval emerged as a strong predictor, with retrieval of more than 15 oocytes resulting in cumulative live birth rates exceeding 60%, while blastocyst-stage Day 5 embryo transfers were associated with markedly higher live birth odds compared to cleavage-stage Day 3 transfers. The freeze-all strategy yielded superior cumulative live birth rates (51.4%) compared to fresh embryo transfer protocols (41.7% by the fourth transfer), likely reflecting improved endometrial receptivity and reduced ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk. Beyond reproductive factors, endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and advanced age were identified as significant contributors to infertility risk, while higher educational attainment was unexpectedly associated with lower infertility odds.

title: Impact of advanced paternal age on reproductive outcomes in preimplantation genetic testing cycles of young female: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This study demonstrates that advanced paternal age (≥40 years) significantly impairs reproductive outcomes in preimplantation genetic testing cycles through elevated sperm DNA fragmentation (DFI). Men aged 40 years or older exhibited markedly higher DFI levels, reduced sperm concentration, and lower total sperm counts compared to younger men, with strong positive correlation between paternal age and DFI. Elevated sperm DFI independently reduced the likelihood of high-quality blastocyst formation and clinical pregnancy achievement, mediating the detrimental effects of advanced paternal age on both blastocyst development rates (57.3% in the ≥40-year group versus 68.6% in younger men) and live birth outcomes. Notably, advanced paternal age did not directly alter blastocyst ploidy status, indicating that impaired reproductive success operates through mechanisms other than chromosomal abnormalities. The findings underscore sperm DNA integrity as a critical factor for reproductive success and suggest that sperm DFI assessment may be valuable for counseling older men undergoing assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Progestin-primed ovarian stimulation protocol in patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

The progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocol represents an effective alternative to traditional controlled ovarian hyperstimulation methods by leveraging progestin's ability to inhibit the estradiol-induced luteinizing hormone surge and prevent premature ovulation. By suppressing hypothalamic GnRH pulses and reducing LH pulse frequency and amplitude through early follicular phase progestin administration, PPOS maintains oocyte maturation while achieving significantly lower rates of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome compared to GnRH agonist protocols. Clinical outcomes with PPOS are comparable or superior to conventional protocols, with multiple studies demonstrating higher oocyte retrieval rates, improved embryo quality, increased clinical pregnancy rates, and equivalent neonatal outcomes without increased birth defects. The protocol offers particular advantages for patients with poor ovarian response and polycystic ovary syndrome, while providing economic benefits through lower medication costs than GnRH analogs. However, the timing and concentration of progestin administration require optimization, as excessive progestin levels may impair granulosa cell function and potentially reduce oocyte maturity rates through disruption of mitochondrial metabolism.

title: Shorter interval between consecutive testicular sperm aspirations and clinical pregnancy after intracytoplasmic sperm injection in obstructive azoospermia: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study of 751 men with obstructive azoospermia undergoing testicular sperm aspiration (TESA) combined with intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) demonstrates that shorter intervals between consecutive TESA procedures significantly improve clinical pregnancy outcomes. Patients with intervals of three months or less achieved a pregnancy rate of 56.0% compared to 47.9% for longer intervals, with this benefit primarily mediated through improved embryo quality—shorter intervals increased usable and good-quality embryo production by approximately 0.496 and 0.393 embryos, respectively. The mechanism appears to involve preservation of sperm quality and yield through minimization of testicular inactivity, while an approximate three-month interval balances adequate tissue recovery and resolution of acute inflammation against the functional decline from prolonged inactivity. Multivariate analysis confirmed TESA interval as an independent predictor of pregnancy probability, with approximately 60% of its effect on pregnancy likelihood operating through enhanced embryo quality. The study carefully controlled for female factors known to affect reproductive outcomes by excluding women over 38 years, those with endometriosis or adenomyosis, and those with diminished ovarian reserve, ensuring that observed improvements reflected male factor optimization rather than female partner characteristics.

title: Uterine biophysical profile after intrauterine instillation of autologous blood cell derivative (ABCD) for thin endometrium in frozen embryo transfer cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates autologous blood cell derivative (ABCD) as a treatment for thin endometrium in frozen embryo transfer cycles by measuring uterine biophysical profile (UBP) improvements. ABCD, which releases growth factors and cytokines from concentrated platelets, significantly enhanced endometrial development by increasing endometrial thickness, uterine blood flow, and overall UBP scores while reducing uterine artery pulsatility index. Key UBP parameters including endometrial thickness (10–14 mm), trilaminar endometrial pattern, abundant Zone III blood flow, and reduced Doppler pulsatility index were strongly associated with higher pregnancy rates, with a UBP score threshold of 20 demonstrating 95% sensitivity for predicting successful outcomes. Treatment with ABCD resulted in approximately 27% positive β-hCG, 18% clinical pregnancy, and 12% live birth rates in patients who previously experienced cycle cancellations due to thin endometrium. The findings suggest that ABCD-induced improvements in endometrial morphology and hemodynamics can enhance endometrial receptivity and implantation potential in otherwise unsuccessful frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: Reproductive and obstetric outcomes following frozen embryo transfer: letrozole combined with human menopausal gonadotropin versus hormone replacement cycle
summary: This study compared frozen embryo transfer outcomes using letrozole combined with human menopausal gonadotropin (a natural cycle ovulation induction protocol) versus hormone replacement therapy (HRT). The ovulation induction (OI) protocol significantly improved reproductive outcomes, with higher implantation rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and live birth rates compared to HRT, while reducing obstetric complications including gestational diabetes mellitus, pregnancy-induced hypertension, and cesarean section rates. The mechanistic advantage of OI appears to involve preserved corpus luteum function, which secretes vasoactive factors like relaxin and VEGF critical for placentation, combined with improved follicular development and endometrial preparation via estradiol and progesterone supplementation. Notably, women with ovulation disorders and those over 35 years old particularly benefited from the OI protocol. The OI group demonstrated superior endometrial morphology (Type A endometrium) and higher proportions of high-quality blastocysts, suggesting that endogenous hormone production during ovulation induction optimizes both embryo quality and uterine receptivity compared to exogenous hormone replacement.

title: Does Prolonged Preservation of Blastocysts Affect the Implantation and Live Birth Rate? A Danish Nationwide Register-Based Study
summary: This Danish nationwide register study examined whether prolonged cryopreservation of blastocysts affects reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology. Using vitrification technology, which is superior to slow-freeze methods, the researchers found that blastocyst storage time—even when extended beyond 25 months—did not significantly reduce biochemical pregnancy rates, clinical pregnancy rates, or live birth rates (28.6%), with adjusted odds ratios remaining near unity across all timepoints. The freeze-all strategy, which involves prolonged blastocyst storage, offers the additional clinical benefit of reducing ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk through gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist application. Neonatal outcomes including small for gestational age, preterm birth, and congenital malformations showed no association with storage duration, though a non-significant trend toward increased large for gestational age infants was observed in the prolonged storage group, potentially related to PCOS prevalence or epigenetic modifications. Maternal age at oocyte retrieval emerged as the most significant confounding variable in the analyses. Overall, these findings demonstrate that prolonged vitrification and cryopreservation of blastocysts maintains reproductive efficacy and neonatal safety, supporting the clinical use of freeze-all strategies without concerns about extended storage duration.

title: A Clinically Applicable Nomogram for Live Birth Prediction After IVF: The Zubeyde Hanim Model
summary: The Zubeyde Hanim Model is a clinically applicable nomogram that predicts live birth outcomes following IVF by integrating multiple reproductive and embryological parameters. Female age emerged as the strongest predictor, with younger women and those with shorter infertility duration showing significantly higher live birth rates, reflecting age-related declines in oocyte competence and increased chromosomal abnormalities. Embryo quality parameters were equally critical, with blastocyst-stage transfers (day 5) and higher-quality embryo morphology independently predicting improved implantation success compared to cleavage-stage alternatives. Endometrial thickness, antral follicle count, and the number of normally fertilized oocytes also demonstrated positive associations with live birth, collectively indicating that both oocyte competence and endometrial receptivity contribute to reproductive success. The nomogram discriminated equally well between fresh and frozen embryo transfer cycles, suggesting its broad clinical applicability across IVF protocols.

title: Minimal Dose Paradigm in IUI Stimulation for Unexplained Infertility: Letrozole-Initiated Late Gonadotropin Protocol
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates a minimal-dose stimulation paradigm combining letrozole with late-onset recombinant FSH (rFSH) for intrauterine insemination in unexplained infertility, comparing it to conventional rFSH protocols. Letrozole suppresses estrogen synthesis through aromatase inhibition, creating a transient hypoestrogenic state that enhances endogenous FSH secretion while preserving endometrial receptivity—a key advantage over clomiphene citrate, which exerts prolonged anti-estrogenic effects on the endometrium. The sequential letrozole plus late-onset rFSH protocol achieved comparable pregnancy outcomes to conventional stimulation while reducing total gonadotropin requirements by 50% (median 375 versus 750 IU), thereby lowering medication costs and minimizing risks of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and multiple pregnancies. Female age emerged as the only independent predictor of pregnancy outcomes across both groups; after adjustment for age and ovarian reserve markers, the stimulation protocol itself did not independently predict pregnancy success. The crude pregnancy rate was higher in the letrozole group (14.8% versus 9.9%), with more ongoing pregnancies despite comparable singleton live birth rates, suggesting this cost-effective approach represents a clinically efficient, gonadotropin-sparing option particularly suitable for patients with polycystic ovary syndrome and normal ovarian reserve.

title: Impact of three-month tuberculosis preventive treatment (3HP) on IVF-ET outcomes in infertile women with tuberculosis infection: a retrospective before–after study
summary:
# Summary

Tuberculosis infection, including female genital tuberculosis and subclinical TB infection, impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms including tubal obstruction, ovarian dysfunction, endometrial dysfunction, and implantation failure. In infertile women undergoing IVF-ET, a three-month tuberculosis preventive treatment (3HP) regimen combining isoniazid and rifapentine dramatically improved reproductive outcomes, increasing live birth rates from 1.23% to 36.13% while reducing early miscarriage rates from 62.50% to 13.43%. The treatment was particularly beneficial in women with recurrent implantation failure, where live birth rates reached 34.38% after TPT. Independent predictors of successful live birth included blastocyst transfer and adequate endometrial thickness, while TPT duration beyond three months provided no additional benefit. Additionally, TPT eliminated ectopic pregnancies in treated cycles, suggesting restoration of normal reproductive tract anatomy and function following TB infection clearance.

title: Identifying cycle-specific predictive factors for clinical pregnancy after recurrent implantation failure: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study identified cycle-specific predictive factors for clinical pregnancy in patients with recurrent implantation failure (RIF), revealing distinct patterns between fresh and frozen embryo transfer cycles. In fresh cycles, younger maternal age, higher anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, elevated androgens, lower follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), greater endometrial thickness, and use of long or ultra-long stimulation protocols independently predicted clinical pregnancy success, alongside superior embryological outcomes including more transferable embryos and high-quality embryos. In contrast, frozen embryo transfer cycles showed that endometrial thickness and AMH emerged as key positive predictors while advanced maternal age remained a consistent negative predictor. The study underscores that impaired endometrial receptivity accounts for approximately two-thirds of implantation failures, with RIF associated with low AMH levels, insulin resistance, autoimmune abnormalities, intrauterine adhesions, elevated FSH, and high BMI, while androgens play essential roles in follicular development and ovarian responsiveness. These findings suggest that personalized assessment of ovarian reserve, endocrine profiles, and endometrial characteristics can guide targeted interventions to improve reproductive outcomes in RIF patients.

title: Clinical outcomes of long follicular-phase protocol in first-cycle patients with diminished ovarian reserve and AMH<1.2 ng/mL: A single-center study
summary:
# Summary

In this single-center study of patients with diminished ovarian reserve (AMH ≤1.2 ng/mL), the long follicular-phase protocol demonstrated clinical effectiveness comparable to standard protocols used in normal-reserve patients. Anti-Müllerian hormone served as a reliable biomarker for assessing ovarian reserve and predicting gonadotropin responsiveness, with decreased AMH levels associated with reduced oocyte retrieval and embryo quality, particularly in younger women. The long follicular-phase protocol, utilizing GnRH agonist down-regulation followed by FSH stimulation and GnRH antagonist co-treatment, yielded significantly higher numbers of retrieved oocytes, fertilized embryos, and high-quality embryos despite lower baseline hormone levels. Patient age emerged as the strongest negative predictor of clinical pregnancy and live birth rates, while antral follicle count and estradiol levels on human chorionic gonadotropin trigger day independently improved reproductive outcomes. Notably, ovarian reserve status had no significant impact on implantation rates, biochemical pregnancy rates, abortion rates, or live birth rates when controlled for age, suggesting that the long follicular-phase protocol effectively overcomes the reproductive limitations typically associated with diminished ovarian reserve.

title: Short-term vs. overnight insemination: which one is better for patients with only one or two oocytes retrieved in IVF cycles?
summary: In patients with limited oocyte retrieval during IVF cycles, short-term insemination (typically 5 hours) demonstrates superior embryological outcomes compared to overnight insemination by reducing reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation from prolonged sperm-oocyte co-incubation. Short-term insemination significantly improves normal fertilization rates, cleavage-stage embryo development, and blastocyst formation while reducing multiple pronuclei abnormalities and cycle cancellation rates—particularly critical for poor responders who face elevated risks of total fertilization failure and cycles with no transferable embryos. The mechanism involves limiting ROS production from cumulus cell and sperm metabolism, which otherwise impairs embryo division and development, while simultaneously enabling early rescue intracytoplasmic sperm injection and cumulus cell removal. When combined with extended blastocyst culture, short-term insemination substantially improves cumulative live birth rates by enabling better embryo selection and reducing the emotional and economic burden of failed cycles. Although short-term insemination does not independently increase cumulative live birth rates compared to overnight protocols in all populations, the strategy effectively reduces fertilization failure and optimizes embryo development in the challenging population of patients with minimal oocyte yields.

title: Impact of ovarian stimulation duration in GnRH antagonist protocols on the cumulative ongoing pregnancy rate in women with normal ovarian reserve: a cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how stimulation duration in GnRH antagonist protocols affects reproductive outcomes in women with normal ovarian reserve undergoing assisted reproduction. GnRH antagonists prevent the LH surge while allowing shorter stimulation periods compared to GnRH agonist protocols, with FSH driving follicular development and estradiol secreted by granulosa cells supporting both the LH surge and endometrial proliferation. Prolonged ovarian stimulation (≥12 days) was associated with reduced oocyte retrieval, lower maturation and fertilization rates, and fewer blastocysts formed, despite requiring higher total gonadotropin doses. However, contrary to previous clinical concerns, stimulation duration did not significantly influence cumulative ongoing pregnancy rates when analyzed in multivariate models; instead, younger age and higher anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels emerged as independent predictors of success. These findings suggest that while extended stimulation duration produces measurable reductions in embryonic yield, this does not translate into impaired pregnancy outcomes in women with adequate ovarian reserve, challenging conventional assumptions about optimal stimulation duration in antagonist protocols.

title: Effect of extended hormonal suppression in patients with adenomyosis undergoing embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is an estrogen-dependent uterine disorder characterized by invagination of endometrial tissue into the myometrium, creating a hyperestrogenic, inflammatory microenvironment that impairs endometrial receptivity and substantially increases miscarriage risk and infertility. The pathophysiology involves local estrogen production through aberrant aromatase expression in adenomyotic lesions, combined with progesterone resistance in the affected endometrium, which disrupts normal implantation and pregnancy maintenance. Extended hormonal pretreatment strategies prior to embryo transfer—including gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRHa), aromatase inhibitors (letrozole), the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS), dienogest, and continuous combined oral contraceptives—suppress adenomyotic activity, reduce lesion burden, restore endometrial receptivity, and improve clinical pregnancy and implantation rates. Among these approaches, GnRHa combined with letrozole appears particularly effective for severe adenomyosis by providing dual suppression of estrogen synthesis, while LNG-IUS and dienogest offer additional benefits including reduction of abnormal uterine contractions and inflammatory cytokine production (interleukin-1, tumor necrosis factor-alpha). The cumulative evidence supports prolonged hormonal suppression before frozen embryo transfer as an evidence-based approach to normalize the adenomyotic endometrium and enhance reproductive outcomes in women with adenomyosis undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: Effect of acupuncture pretreatment on clinical pregnancy rate in women with diminished ovarian reserve undergoing IVF-ET: study protocol for a multicenter randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), characterized by reduced oocyte quantity and quality alongside elevated follicle-stimulating hormone, decreased anti-Müllerian hormone, and reduced antral follicle count, significantly compromises fertility outcomes by reducing clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and increasing miscarriage risk in women undergoing IVF-ET. This multicenter randomized controlled trial investigates acupuncture as a therapeutic intervention for DOR, building on emerging evidence that acupuncture modulates reproductive hormone profiles, enhances ovarian reserve function, and increases clinical pregnancy rates while reducing treatment-related anxiety. The study will assess ovarian reserve using multiple markers including FSH, AMH, antral follicle count, luteinizing hormone, and estradiol, alongside measurement of anxiety using standardized scales. By examining acupuncture pretreatment efficacy in women with DOR undergoing IVF-ET, this trial aims to validate acupuncture as a beneficial alternative or adjunctive therapy to conventional interventions such as growth hormone or dehydroepiandrosterone supplementation. The primary outcome is clinical pregnancy rate, with secondary endpoints addressing live birth rates, hormonal markers, and psychological well-being, providing comprehensive evaluation of acupuncture's therapeutic potential in restoring reproductive outcomes for this vulnerable population.

title: Comparison of frozen embryo transfer outcomes in hormonal vs. mild stimulation protocols in polycystic ovary syndrome women: A randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial compared frozen embryo transfer outcomes between mild ovarian stimulation (MOS) and hormone replacement therapy (HRT) protocols in women with polycystic ovary syndrome. Letrozole-based MOS demonstrated superior clinical pregnancy and live birth rates compared to HRT, with reduced miscarriage risk. The mechanistic advantage of letrozole-based MOS derives from its upregulation of key endometrial receptivity markers, including leukemia inhibitory factor, dickkopf-related protein 1, leukemia inhibitory factor receptor, fibroblast growth factor 22, and integrin αVβ3, which collectively enhance endometrial thickness and receptivity. In contrast, HRT's reliance on synthetic estrogen exposure increases thrombotic complications and fails to create an optimal endometrial microenvironment. The natural rise in endogenous estrogen from MOS, combined with improved follicle maturation and oocyte quality, establishes superior embryo-endometrium synchronization and implantation conditions compared to the artificial hormonal environment of HRT protocols.

title: Comparison of intracytoplasmic sperm injection and in vitro fertilization outcomes in double stimulation with conventional protocol in participants with poor ovarian response with minimal injection: An RCT
summary:
# Summary

Poor ovarian response, characterized by diminished ovarian reserve and associated with advanced maternal age, prior ovarian surgery, pelvic adhesions, and elevated body mass index, significantly reduces oocyte quantity and quality, leading to lower clinical pregnancy and live birth rates in assisted reproductive technology. This randomized controlled trial evaluated the dual stimulation (DuoStim) protocol—which involves two rounds of controlled ovarian stimulation during both follicular and luteal phases within a single cycle—as an alternative strategy for poor responders. Although DuoStim resulted in fewer total retrieved oocytes (7.2 ± 4.5) compared to conventional single stimulation (9.7 ± 6.04), the protocol achieved comparable metaphase II oocyte counts (5.7 ± 3.9 vs. 6.2 ± 3.9) and similar embryo yields (4.5 ± 3.9 vs. 4.7 ± 2.8) while significantly reducing stimulation duration and gonadotropin requirements. The DuoStim protocol represents a time-efficient and cost-effective strategy for poor ovarian responders, maintaining embryo quality while reducing the number of injection procedures and overall treatment duration, though oocyte quantity advantage was modest and embryo quality was preserved across both protocols.

title: Effects of acupuncture and moxibustion therapy on endometrial receptivity of infertile women with in vitro fertilization–embryo transfer: status quo and countermeasures
summary: Acupuncture and moxibustion therapy improve endometrial receptivity in infertile women undergoing in vitro fertilization–embryo transfer (IVF-ET) through multiple mechanisms that enhance endometrial thickness, blood flow, and hormonal regulation of estradiol (E2) and progesterone (P). The beneficial effects operate at molecular and cellular levels, involving modulation of autophagy via the AMPK/mTOR pathway, upregulation of pinopode-related molecules (leukemia inhibitory factor and integrin αvβ3), and regulation of non-coding RNAs (miR-449a and circRNAs) that support endometrial receptivity and angiogenesis. Manual acupuncture treatment significantly improves vascularization indices and endometrial parameters, with adequate endometrial blood flow being essential for successful embryo implantation. Additionally, acupuncture and moxibustion therapy reduce anxiety, prevent and treat ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, and serve as adjunctive therapeutic strategies to optimize outcomes in IVF-ET procedures. These complementary approaches address multiple barriers to implantation including poor endometrial thickness, inadequate blood perfusion, and psychological stress that impair fertility in women with conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis.

title: Endometrial microbiota‐targeted therapies for chronic endometritis‐associated recurrent implantation failure and their impact on IVF outcomes: A systematic review and methodological quality assessment
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis, a persistent low-grade inflammatory condition linked to microbial dysbiosis, is strongly associated with recurrent implantation failure and adverse reproductive outcomes including miscarriage. The condition is characterized histologically by CD138-positive plasma cells in the endometrial stroma and can be identified through hysteroscopic findings such as stromal edema and hyperemia. A Lactobacillus-dominated endometrial microbiota correlates with improved implantation, pregnancy, and live birth rates, while reduced Lactobacillus abundance and proliferation of pathogenic species impair endometrial receptivity. Microbiota-targeted interventions including antibiotics, probiotics, and nutraceuticals show promise in improving reproductive outcomes in women with chronic endometritis-associated recurrent implantation failure, though careful monitoring is needed since indiscriminate or prolonged antibiotic use risks disrupting the beneficial microbial balance essential for embryo implantation.

title: Conventional in vitro fertilization at the age of fourties
journal: Journal of the Turkish German Gynecological Association
summary:
# Summary: Conventional IVF in Women Aged ≥40 Years

This study demonstrates that conventional in vitro fertilization (C-IVF) provides clinically acceptable outcomes for women aged 40 years and older with non-male factor infertility, achieving fertilization rates of approximately 75%, implantation rates of 10.44%, and live birth rates of 12.25%. Contrary to common clinical practice favoring ICSI, C-IVF achieves comparable or superior fertilization and implantation rates to ICSI in this age group while avoiding unnecessary oocyte denudation, which can cause mechanical damage and degeneration. The study challenges the misconception that ICSI provides superior fertilization rates by demonstrating that when fertilization rates are assessed per collected oocyte—the most clinically meaningful metric—C-IVF yields higher overall rates, with similar total fertilization failure rates between the two techniques. The authors propose a "C-IVF index" based on semen parameters to appropriately triage patients toward the most suitable insemination method, recommending that C-IVF be the preferred first-line approach in non-male factor cases due to its lower cost, ease of application, and evidence-based clinical efficacy. These findings are particularly relevant for advanced reproductive age, where time-sensitive declines in oocyte quality necessitate the most efficient and least invasive assisted reproductive techniques.

title: Impact of serum progesterone levels and rescue progesterone supplemental luteal support on pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer: a controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This controlled trial examined how serum progesterone levels and supplemental luteal support affect pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer (FET) cycles. The study enrolled women across three groups: those with naturally normal progesterone levels, those with low progesterone supplemented with additional vaginal progesterone, and a control group. While low serum progesterone on transfer day was associated with reduced implantation potential and early pregnancy loss in previous literature, supplemental progesterone administration in women with insufficient levels successfully mitigated these negative effects, resulting in comparable biochemical, clinical, and ongoing pregnancy rates across all treatment groups. Notably, maternal age emerged as the only consistent negative predictor of successful pregnancy outcomes across all measures, whereas progesterone supplementation, embryo quality, and ovarian reserve remained influential but non-independent factors. These findings suggest that rescue progesterone supplementation can normalize reproductive prognosis in women with initially low progesterone, potentially offsetting the typically adverse impact of progesterone deficiency during the luteal phase of FET cycles.

title: In vitro fertilization outcomes in previously cured tuberculosis patients: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study examined in vitro fertilization outcomes in infertile patients with a history of tuberculosis (TB), revealing that *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* causes substantial reproductive tract damage including fallopian tube and endometrial lesions that contribute to subfertility and pregnancy loss. While patients cured of TB demonstrated pregnancy and live birth rates comparable to controls overall, outcomes diverged significantly by treatment protocol: ICSI-treated TB patients showed substantially reduced pregnancy and live birth rates compared to controls, whereas IVF protocol results were not significantly different. TB infection was associated with reduced ovarian reserve (fewer antral follicles), diminished endometrial thickness, and prolonged infertility duration, yet paradoxically showed higher blastocyst and high-quality embryo rates, suggesting compensatory mechanisms. The location of TB infection affected outcomes substantially, with endometrial TB causing the lowest cumulative pregnancy rates and highest abortion rates, while frozen-thaw embryo transfer cycles produced better outcomes than fresh cycles in TB patients, likely due to hormone stimulation damage in fresh cycles. These findings suggest that while anti-TB treatment resolves some reproductive impairment, endometrial damage from genital TB remains largely irreversible and poses particular challenges for ICSI-dependent pregnancies.

title: Does rotation during catheter withdrawal in embryo transfer increase pregnancy rates?
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial investigated whether rotating the catheter 360° during withdrawal after embryo transfer improves pregnancy outcomes. While cervical mucus contamination is recognized as a potential barrier to successful embryo transfer—by mechanically blocking embryo expulsion or causing displacement—the study found that catheter rotation had no significant effect on clinical pregnancy rates (48% in the rotation group versus 50.8% in controls) or biochemical pregnancy rates (53.9% versus 58.2%). Although mechanical manipulation theoretically prevents embryo displacement and may trigger uterine contractions that could influence implantation, the clinical benefits of this technique remain undemonstrated. The findings suggest that catheter rotation provides no measurable advantage over standard transfer techniques, despite earlier promising preliminary reports. These results highlight the discrepancy between mechanistic hypotheses and actual clinical outcomes in optimizing embryo transfer procedures.

title: The Influencing Factors and Predictive Algorithm of Pregnancy Outcomes in IVF/ICSI-ET Patients
summary: This study identified multiple clinical and laboratory factors predicting pregnancy outcomes in IVF/ICSI-ET patients through multivariate regression analysis. Key positive predictors of clinical pregnancy included higher anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, increased numbers of high-quality embryos, and greater numbers of transferred embryos, all of which showed strong positive correlations with pregnancy success. Conversely, advanced maternal and paternal age, elevated progesterone levels on hCG day, and higher gonadotropin requirements were negatively associated with clinical pregnancy outcomes. Male factor infertility, particularly oligospermia, asthenozoospermia, and teratozoospermia, significantly impacted both fertilization and live birth rates. The study also identified female tubal obstruction and ovulation disorders as primary causes of infertility, alongside abnormal male semen parameters. These findings suggest that a predictive algorithm incorporating patient age, ovarian reserve (AMH), embryo quality and quantity, and endocrine parameters could optimize patient selection and counseling for assisted reproductive technology outcomes.

title: A Comparative Study of the Effect of Novel Dual Trigger and Delayed HCG With Dual Trigger in Poor Responder Poseidon Group 4 Patients in Antagonist IVF Cycles
summary:
# Summary

Decreased ovarian reserve is a significant challenge in infertility treatment, particularly in poor responder patients undergoing in vitro fertilization, as it leads to cycle cancellations and diminished pregnancy rates. This study compared novel dual trigger protocols with delayed human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) against standard HCG triggers in poor responder patients (POSEIDON Group 4) undergoing antagonist IVF cycles. The dual trigger approach, which combines GnRH agonists with HCG, enhances oocyte maturation by simultaneously activating endogenous LH and FSH pathways while avoiding the ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk associated with HCG alone. In the study population, the novel dual trigger with delayed HCG protocol resulted in higher chemical pregnancy rates, improved implantation rates, increased ongoing pregnancy rates, and reduced cycles with zero retrieved oocytes compared to standard protocols. This dual trigger strategy produced more retrievable and mature oocytes, higher-quality embryos, and greater numbers of transferable embryos overall. The findings suggest that novel dual trigger protocols offer a safe and effective approach to improve reproductive outcomes in poor responder patients with diminished ovarian reserve.

title: Elective single embryo transfer in in vitro fertilization cycles with or without preimplantation genetic testing using next-generation sequencing: A randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized clinical trial evaluated whether next-generation sequencing (NGS)-based preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-A) improves outcomes in elective single embryo transfer (SET) cycles for infertile couples with good prognosis. NGS enables comprehensive chromosomal analysis of blastocyst trophectoderm biopsies to identify euploid (genetically normal) embryos, which theoretically enhances implantation potential by selecting embryos with superior developmental capacity. However, the study found no significant differences between SET alone (76.7% clinical pregnancy rate) and NGS + SET (79.6% pregnancy rate) in clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, or miscarriage rates, suggesting that genetic screening does not provide additional reproductive benefits in this patient population. While euploid embryo selection may reduce costs associated with implantation failures and abortions, particularly for women over 37 years old, the substantial added expense of NGS (over $6,000) cannot be justified by improved outcomes in patients with adequate ovarian reserve and normal morphological embryo characteristics. These findings align with recent large randomized trials and suggest that morphological criteria and embryo development characteristics remain sufficient for embryo selection in good-prognosis IVF cycles.

title: An early LH surge may jeopardize ART outcomes of patients with PCOS and its predictive models
summary: In polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART), an early luteinizing hormone (LH) surge during controlled ovarian stimulation significantly compromises reproductive outcomes by increasing spontaneous abortion rates and reducing live birth rates across both fresh and frozen embryo transfer cycles. The early LH surge arises from the rapid rise in estradiol secreted by multiple follicles characteristic of PCOS, disrupting the normal oocyte maturation process that requires appropriately timed LH signaling to complete meiosis and promote embryo development. Although patients experiencing an early LH surge paradoxically retrieve more eggs and exhibit higher antral follicle counts, they produce lower-quality embryos, suggesting impaired oocyte developmental competence. Predictive models incorporating baseline factors (BMI, basal FSH, basal TSH) and early stimulation markers (LH, FSH, estradiol, and progesterone on cycle days 0 and 5/6) can identify at-risk patients as early as cycle day 0, with model 3 achieving an area under the curve of 0.881. Clinical management involves early introduction of GnRH antagonists starting on cycle day 3 or 4 to suppress the premature LH surge and optimize ART outcomes in PCOS patients.

title: Electroacupuncture for diminished ovarian reserve-associated infertility with a live birth: A case report
summary:
# Summary

Diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) causes infertility by reducing oocyte quantity and quality, leading to complications including spontaneous abortion and chromosomal aberrations. This case report demonstrates that electroacupuncture effectively treats DOR-associated infertility through multiple hormonal and molecular mechanisms: it upregulates anti-müllerian hormone and antral follicle count while downregulating follicle-stimulating hormone, thereby improving ovarian function. At the cellular level, electroacupuncture promotes granulosa cell proliferation through the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway, inhibits oxidative stress by altering gut microbiota, and reduces apoptosis of ovarian granulosa cells via the LncMEG3/miR-21-3p axis. After 24 weeks of electroacupuncture treatment, the patient achieved spontaneous pregnancy and live birth, supporting the therapeutic potential of this alternative approach for improving pregnancy outcomes in women with DOR-associated infertility.

title: Maternal age at frozen embryo transfer, not cryopreservation duration, is more important in the transfer of cleavage-stage embryos than blastocysts: A single-center retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This single-center retrospective study examined how embryo cryopreservation duration, maternal age, and embryo stage affect frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes in patients with endometriosis. The key finding is that embryo cryopreservation duration itself did not significantly affect clinical pregnancy or live birth rates regardless of whether cleavage-stage or blastocyst-stage embryos were transferred, contradicting concerns about prolonged storage. However, maternal age at FET emerged as a more critical determinant of success, with increased maternal age associated with reduced pregnancy and live birth rates, independent of storage time. Additional factors that enhanced outcomes included transferring more embryos and, for blastocyst transfers, using antagonist ovarian stimulation protocols. The study suggests that maternal age-related endometrial changes—including chromosomal abnormalities, increased collagen content, and reduced hormone receptors—may impair endometrial receptivity more substantially than cryopreservation duration. These findings support blastocyst transfer as a superior strategy in older women and indicate that modern vitrification techniques provide durable embryo preservation without compromising reproductive success.

title: Impact of dalteparin sodium and luteal phase support on serum markers, interleukins, and pregnancy outcomes in patients with in vitro fertilisation failure
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the combined therapeutic impact of dalteparin sodium (DS), a low-molecular-weight heparin, and luteal phase support (LPS) on IVF outcomes in patients with repeated implantation failures. The combination treatment significantly improved clinical pregnancy rates and embryo implantation rates while reducing miscarriage rates compared to controls. Treatment with LPS plus DS reduced inflammatory markers including CA-125 and interleukin-6, which are associated with endometrial receptivity and corpus luteum function, and simultaneously normalized reproductive hormone levels (FSH, E2, and LH). The mechanistic basis involves DS's anticoagulant and anti-inflammatory properties that enhance ovarian microcirculation, suppress inflammatory responses, and improve endometrial receptivity through nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation and reduced ovarian stromal inflammation. Beyond reproductive outcomes, patients receiving LPS plus DS demonstrated improved quality of life measures across physiological, emotional, social, and mental health domains, with enhanced family cohesion and social support, indicating a comprehensive therapeutic benefit in managing infertility-related complications.

title: Fertility preservation in women with diminished ovarian reserve: evaluating the AMH criteria
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates fertility preservation strategies for women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), with particular emphasis on anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) as a predictive marker. Serum AMH strongly correlates with ovarian follicle number and retrieved oocyte yield, making it a robust predictor of ovarian response and patient selection for cryopreservation; however, women with AMH ≤0.5 ng/mL achieve inadequate oocyte yields for successful fertility preservation even with intensive protocols like DuoStim, which combines follicular and luteal phase stimulations within a single cycle. The study demonstrates that vitrification significantly improves oocyte survival and developmental potential for cryopreservation, and that age inversely correlates with both AMH levels and oocyte retrieval success. DOR patients showed substantially lower stimulation efficiency and oocyte yields compared to non-DOR patients, with minimal benefit from increased gonadotropin doses. The findings reinforce that while AMH and age are critical factors in determining fertility preservation candidacy, younger women with severe DOR may still achieve clinically meaningful outcomes, suggesting individualized assessment is essential for this compromised population.

title: The Impact of Cleavage and Blastocyst Stage Sequential Transfer on Clinical Pregnancy Outcomes of Frozen Embryo Transfer in Patients with Multiple Transplant Failures: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: This retrospective cohort study evaluated sequential embryo transfer as a treatment strategy for patients with recurrent implantation failures, a condition partly attributed to endometrial receptivity abnormalities that impair embryo-endometrial interactions. The research compared sequential transfer (transferring cleavage-stage and blastocyst-stage embryos sequentially) against standard double cleavage-stage or blastocyst transfer protocols in frozen embryo cycles. Contrary to initial theoretical promise, sequential embryo transfer did not significantly improve clinical pregnancy rates (aOR = 0.94) or live birth rates compared to conventional approaches, despite some prior studies suggesting potential benefits. However, sequential transfer did reduce multiple pregnancy rates, suggesting a clinically meaningful safety advantage. The sequential transfer group had distinct baseline characteristics including higher average age, thicker endometrium, and lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels, which likely reflected their status as patients with multiple prior failures. These findings suggest that while sequential embryo transfer may reduce the risk of multiple gestations, it does not overcome the underlying barriers to implantation success in patients with recurrent transfer failures.

title: Artificial intelligence models and combined scoring approaches for endometrial receptivity assessment in in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

Artificial intelligence models significantly improve endometrial receptivity assessment in in vitro fertilization by automating the evaluation of key clinical parameters. The study developed a composite scoring system that integrates AI-derived embryo quality scores (using VGG16 classification of blastomere symmetry, cytoplasmic fragmentation, and inner cell mass quality) with endometrial receptivity scores (using U-Net segmentation to measure thickness, echogenicity pattern, and vascular flow) and clinical SART pregnancy probability scores. This integrated composite approach achieved superior predictive performance for biochemical pregnancy outcomes (AUC 0.94) compared to individual embryo or endometrial parameters alone. The U-Net segmentation model demonstrated high accuracy (Dice score 0.92) in automated endometrial boundary delineation and correctly identified endometrial patterns in 91.8% of cases, with trilaminar patterns representing approximately 60% of the dataset. These findings demonstrate that multidimensional AI-based scoring frameworks can optimize embryo transfer timing and improve pregnancy prediction in assisted reproductive technology by simultaneously accounting for both embryonic quality and uterine receptivity.

title: Effect of zero-time exercise intervention on endometrial receptivity in women with thin endometrium a single-center randomized sham-controlled trial
summary:
# Article Summary

This randomized sham-controlled trial demonstrates that zero-time exercise (ZTEx), a brief pelvic floor muscle activation intervention, effectively treats thin endometrium by enhancing endometrial thickness and receptivity in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology. The mechanism operates through improved pelvic circulation: ZTEx stimulates pelvic muscle contraction and relaxation, which promotes uterine artery blood perfusion and increases oxygen and nutrient delivery to the endometrium, thereby supporting endometrial cell proliferation. After 12 weeks of intervention, the ZTEx group showed significantly improved uterine artery hemodynamic parameters (decreased pulsatility index, resistance index, and systolic/diastolic ratio) alongside enhanced endometrial vascularization indices and increased blood flow volume. These improvements in endometrial blood supply were accompanied by increased insulin sensitivity and reduced metabolic dysfunction associated with sedentary behavior, factors that normally inhibit endometrial growth. By addressing the pathophysiology of thin endometrium through non-invasive means, ZTEx offers a promising alternative to conventional treatments like high-dose estrogen supplementation, which carries risks of thromboembolism and breast disorders, while directly improving the endometrial parameters critical for successful embryo implantation and pregnancy outcomes.

title: Serum progesterone and vaginal gel dose in artificial-cycle frozen embryo transfer: association with pregnancy outcome
summary:
# Summary

In artificial-cycle frozen embryo transfer, vaginal progesterone gel (Crinone) provides luteal phase support through sustained hormone release with preferential local endometrial exposure, while avoiding the extensive first-pass hepatic metabolism associated with oral progesterone. A higher Crinone dose of 180 mg daily was significantly associated with improved clinical pregnancy outcomes compared to 90 mg, including higher rates of gestational sac detection, fetal heartbeat detection, and live birth, even after adjusting for baseline differences. Notably, serum progesterone levels demonstrated poor predictive value for pregnancy outcomes (AUC 0.541–0.559), suggesting that local endometrial exposure rather than systemic progesterone concentration is the critical factor determining success. Blastocyst quality and the number of embryos transferred were also significantly associated with improved pregnancy outcomes. These findings indicate that optimizing vaginal progesterone dose is more clinically relevant than monitoring serum progesterone levels for improving reproductive outcomes in frozen embryo transfer cycles.

title: Impact of estradiol-to-progesterone ratio before progesterone initiation on pregnancy outcomes in frozen embryo transfer cycles with hormone replacement therapy: an analysis of over 25,000 cycles
summary:
# Summary

This large-scale analysis of over 25,000 frozen embryo transfer cycles demonstrates that the estradiol-to-progesterone (E2/P) ratio immediately before progesterone initiation is a critical predictor of pregnancy outcomes, with elevated ratios progressively reducing both clinical pregnancy and live birth rates across quartiles. Biologically, estradiol promotes endometrial proliferation and induces progesterone receptor expression, while progesterone stimulates glandular secretion and stromal decidualization—processes essential for embryo implantation and endometrial receptivity. When the E2/P ratio becomes disproportionately elevated, excessive estrogen exposure downregulates progesterone receptors, inhibits endometrial differentiation, and disrupts the implantation window, ultimately impairing the delicate hormonally-orchestrated transition from the proliferative to secretory endometrial phase. Notably, serum E2 concentration alone was not significantly associated with pregnancy outcomes when adjusted for the E2/P ratio, indicating that the balance between these steroids rather than absolute estrogen levels determines reproductive success. Positive predictors of pregnancy included blastocyst transfer, higher endometrial thickness, and certain infertility etiologies (polycystic ovary syndrome and male factor), while advancing female age, tubal factor infertility, and elevated BMI reduced success rates. These findings suggest that optimizing the E2/P ratio prior to progesterone supplementation may improve outcomes in hormone replacement therapy cycles.

title: Comparison of tubal reanastomosis and in vitro fertilisation for restoring fertility in women with a history of tubal ligation
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study evaluates tubal reanastomosis (TR) and in vitro fertilization (IVF) for restoring fertility in women with a history of tubal ligation, finding that treatment choice should be guided primarily by maternal age. IVF demonstrated significantly higher live birth rates (48.9% versus 35.5%) and shorter time to delivery, but was associated with substantially higher costs and lower ectopic pregnancy rates (2.0% versus 16.3%) compared to TR. For women under age 35, tubal reanastomosis offers a cost-effective alternative enabling natural conception, while IVF is recommended for women aged 38 years and older due to superior outcomes and faster achievement of pregnancy in the context of age-related ovarian decline. The study notes that both methods show poor prognosis in women aged 42 years or older, emphasizing that age-related ovarian decline is the predominant factor limiting reproductive success regardless of treatment modality. Multiple surgical factors including anastomosis site, tubal length, and prior ligation method influence TR outcomes, while microsurgical expertise remains critical for successful tubal reversal.

title: Spontaneous Conception After Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine Treatment for Chronic Endometritis with Recurrent Implantation Failure: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Chronic endometritis causes recurrent implantation failure through multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms, including endometrial microbiota dysbiosis, aberrant angiogenesis, abnormal immune responses, and impaired decidualization mediated by dysregulated estrogen and progesterone receptors. Microbial pathogens such as Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus, Enterococcus faecalis, and Mycoplasma trigger stromal plasma cell infiltration and dysregulated inflammatory cytokine expression that compromises endometrial receptivity and vascularization. This case report demonstrates that integrated treatment combining antibiotics (doxycycline, metronidazole) with traditional Chinese medicine—specifically a spleen-strengthening formula containing herbs such as Astragalus membranaceus, Atractylodes macrocephala, and Angelica sinensis—effectively reduces endometrial inflammation and improves endometrial receptivity. The modified Danggui Shaoyao San formula downregulates inflammation-related factors and promotes endometrial pinopodes development, further enhancing implantation potential. Following integrated medical treatment and subsequent assisted reproductive techniques with controlled ovarian stimulation and luteal phase support, the patient achieved spontaneous conception, suggesting that combining antibiotic therapy with traditional Chinese medicine represents a safe and effective therapeutic strategy for managing chronic endometritis-associated recurrent implantation failure.

title: Transdermal testosterone gel vs placebo in women with diminished ovarian reserve prior to in vitro fertilization: a randomized, clinical trial
summary: Diminished ovarian reserve, commonly associated with delayed childbearing and infertility, is linked to poorer in vitro fertilization outcomes due to declines in oocyte quality and embryo euploidy. While preclinical evidence suggested that testosterone treatment could promote follicular growth initiation, upregulate FSH receptors, and facilitate oocyte maturation through androgen receptor binding, a triple-blind randomized clinical trial found that transdermal testosterone gel did not significantly improve clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, or secondary outcomes including oocyte retrieval, embryo quality, or cycle progression compared to placebo. Although testosterone successfully elevated serum testosterone levels and produced some expected androgenic side effects like increased hair growth, it failed to enhance any reproductive or embryologic parameter measured in women with diminished ovarian reserve undergoing IVF. Safety outcomes were favorable, with no serious adverse events, congenital anomalies, or differences in obstetric and neonatal outcomes between groups. These findings indicate that despite its theoretical mechanisms of action in follicular development, testosterone supplementation does not improve fertility outcomes in this population.

title: Effect of progesterone timing on live birth rates in day-6 blastocyst frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles: a randomized controlled trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial examined how progesterone timing affects implantation success in frozen embryo transfer cycles using day-6 blastocysts. The study demonstrates that optimal embryo-endometrium synchrony depends critically on progesterone exposure duration, which defines the implantation window—a window that appears narrower for day-6 blastocysts compared to day-5 blastocysts. Notably, the timing of progesterone initiation interacts significantly with blastocyst developmental stage: early-stage day-6 blastocysts achieved higher live birth rates with six days of progesterone exposure (45.0%), while late-stage day-6 blastocysts showed a trend toward improved outcomes with seven days of progesterone (46.8% vs 31.7%). Suboptimal progesterone supplementation timing causes embryo-endometrium dys-synchrony and compromised implantation, underscoring the importance of matching progesterone duration to blastocyst maturity. While biochemical pregnancy rates, clinical pregnancy rates, and maternal/neonatal outcomes were comparable between treatment groups, these findings suggest that individualized progesterone protocols based on blastocyst expansion stage may optimize reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Vaginal Estradiol Hemihydrate as an Adjunct to Oral Estradiol Valerate in Hormone Replacement Therapy-Frozen Embryo Transfer (HRT-FET) Cycles: A Retrospective Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study evaluated vaginal estradiol hemihydrate as an adjunct to oral estradiol valerate in hormone replacement therapy for frozen embryo transfer cycles, addressing the critical problem that thin endometrium (less than 7-8 mm) impairs implantation and increases miscarriage risk. Vaginal estradiol supplementation significantly improved endometrial thickness from 7.47 mm to 8.68 mm and was associated with a 50% clinical pregnancy rate, along with higher implantation rates (50.8% versus 46.1%) and lower early miscarriage rates (2.5% versus 5.9%) compared to oral estradiol alone. The vaginal route achieves preferential endometrial absorption with 10-fold higher serum estradiol levels than oral administration while maintaining a superior safety profile, as it avoids the 1.8-fold increased thromboembolism risk associated with oral estradiol by bypassing hepatic first-pass metabolism. No adverse events were reported with vaginal estradiol hemihydrate therapy, demonstrating both efficacy and safety as an adjunctive strategy to optimize endometrial preparation in assisted reproductive technology cycles. This approach offers clinicians an effective, low-risk option for patients with persistent thin endometrium who require enhanced endometrial development for successful embryo implantation.

title: Using endometrial immune profiling in young IVF patients: implantation rates improved for morphologically sub-optimal embryos
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometrial immune profiling significantly improves implantation outcomes in IVF patients, particularly those transferring morphologically suboptimal embryos. A balanced endometrial immune profile—characterized by appropriate Th-2/Th-1 cytokine equilibrium assessed through biomarker RNA expression—was associated with substantially higher live birth rates in non-top quality embryo transfers (38.4% versus 21.2% in dysregulated profiles), whereas top-quality embryos showed similar implantation success regardless of immune status. Endometrial immune dysregulation, present in 78% of infertile patients, negatively impacts implantation by compromising the receptive environment necessary for early embryo-endometrium dialogue. The study identifies three dysregulation patterns—under-activate (42%), over-activate (48%), and mixed (10%)—amenable to precision therapeutic interventions to rebalance immunity. These findings suggest that integrating endometrial immune profiling with embryo morphology assessment provides a more comprehensive approach to optimizing reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: Comparative outcomes of day 4 versus day 5 embryo transfers by fertilization method
summary: This study demonstrates that day 4 embryo transfer offers comparable or superior reproductive outcomes to day 5 transfer while avoiding prolonged in vitro culture complications. Day 4 embryos exhibit activation of the embryonic genome, functional apoptotic systems, and cellular checkpoint mechanisms, along with higher euploidy rates and improved endometrial receptivity due to reduced uterine contractions at this developmental stage. Single high-quality day 4 embryo transfers achieved significantly higher clinical pregnancy rates (62.72%) compared to day 5 transfers in IVF cycles, while double high-quality transfers increased live birth rates and multiple pregnancy rates, highlighting the importance of embryo quality and transfer number. By shortening in vitro culture duration, day 4 transfer reduces the risk of cycle cancellation and avoids the adverse effects of prolonged culture, including abnormal imprinted gene expression, apoptosis, and oxidative stress. The timing of day 4 transfer physiologically mirrors natural embryo entry into the uterine cavity, and when combined with modern time-lapse imaging technology that stabilizes environmental conditions, it provides a viable alternative to conventional day 5 blastocyst transfer. The study recommends day 4 single high-quality embryo transfer for maximizing pregnancy outcomes while minimizing multiple pregnancy and preterm birth rates in assisted reproductive technology cycles.

title: Machine learning-based endometrial ultrasound radiomics habitat analysis for predicting pregnancy outcomes after embryo transfer
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial receptivity is critical for embryo implantation, accounting for approximately two-thirds of implantation failures in IVF cycles, and can be effectively assessed through ultrasound examination of features including endometrial thickness and blood flow patterns.
